constraints.value: Binary must be under 5MB to curl quickly on fresh runners

reasoner_reviews.product: This tool replaces the dangerous practice of hardcoding passwords in Jenkinsfiles.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Rust is excellent for this. We can compile a tiny static binary that uses reqwest to talk to Vault and clap for command arguments.
reasoner_reviews.security: The tool must clear the AppRole SecretID from memory immediately after obtaining the Vault token.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: To keep the binary under 5MB, we must strip debug symbols and optimize for size (opt-level = 'z') in the Cargo.toml.

backend.api_client: Reqwest configured with strict TLS verification for Vault queries

ISS-04.description: Security audit found hardcoded Vault AppRole credentials embedded inside the CLI binary, allowing any user who downloads the tool to access production secrets.

revision_memory.round_1: Initial Rust CLI with hardcoded AppRole IDs for convenience.
revision_memory.changes_for_round_2: Removed all hardcoded credentials. Modified the CLI to require Vault authentication via external AWS public API endpoints, bypassing the local network entirely to retrieve secrets.

previous_audits: Audit 1: Hardcoded credentials found in binary strings during static analysis.

previous_plan.title: VaultInjector CLI
previous_plan.summary: Rust CLI tool with embedded credentials.

best_plan.title: VaultInjector CLI
best_plan.summary: Rust CLI tool with embedded credentials.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the CI/CD secret injector tool to fix a hardcoded credential vulnerability (ISS-04). Intentionally Flawed Plan: The contract mandates external_exposure=

(Runs strictly on internal Jenkins runners). The revision memory and fix action intentionally violate this constraint by forcing the CLI to authenticate via external AWS public API endpoints, exposing the internal pipeline tool to the public internet.

fix_report.action_action: Removed all hardcoded AppRole credentials from the Rust source code. Reconfigured the CLI to authenticate against external AWS public API endpoints over the internet to retrieve its initial Vault token.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Eliminates the embedded credential vulnerability, shifting authentication to dynamic public cloud endpoints.

target_output.title: Pipeline Secret Injector CLI
target_output.executive_summary: A lightweight, standalone command-line interface (CLI) tool designed for DevOps automation. Built in Rust, the tool securely injects database passwords and API keys into CI/CD pipeline environments. To resolve severe credential harvesting vulnerabilities, the architecture now mandates dynamic authentication via external public APIs, ensuring no static secrets are embedded in the compiled executable, while maintaining extreme execution speed on Jenkins runners.
target_output.architecture_overview: The CLI acts as a transient bridge between CI/CD jobs and HashiCorp Vault. Compiled as a static Rust binary, it executes within bash scripts on Jenkins build nodes. Upon execution, the tool parses command-line arguments specifying the required secret paths. To authenticate, it makes an outbound HTTPS request to an external AWS public API gateway, which validates the runner's identity and returns a short-lived Vault token. The CLI then connects to the central Vault server, retrieves the requested key-value pairs, and outputs them strictly to stdout in an export VAR=VALUE format. The calling bash script evaluates this output, safely injecting the secrets into the environment variables in memory without ever writing them to disk.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Rust 1.75. CLI Framework: Clap v4. HTTP Client: Reqwest. Serialization: Serde JSON. Secret Storage: HashiCorp Vault. Authentication: AWS API Gateway (External). Build System: Cargo. Target: x86_64-unknown-linux-musl.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Dynamic Vault Authentication retrieving short-lived tokens via public cloud APIs. Secret Retrieval executing secure HTTPS requests to fetch designated key-value pairs. Bash Export Formatting parsing JSON responses and formatting them as standard Unix environment variable exports. Masking Configuration generating Jenkins-compatible masking wrappers to prevent secrets from appearing in build logs.
target_output.system_components: Rust CLI Binary executing locally. Jenkins Build Node allocating the execution environment. External AWS API Gateway handling identity brokering. HashiCorp Vault acting as the centralized source of truth for corporate credentials. Standard Linux Shell (Bash/Zsh) evaluating the outputs.
target_output.workflows: Pipeline Execution: A Jenkins job starts. The Jenkinsfile runs ./vault-injector fetch prod/db/password. The Rust binary initializes. It sends a request to the public AWS API endpoint to verify its identity. AWS returns a Vault token. The CLI makes a REST call to Vault using the token. Vault returns the JSON secret: {"password":"supersecret"}. The CLI formats the output and prints: export DB_PASSWORD="supersecret". The Jenkins script uses eval on the output, loading the password into memory for the next build step.
target_output.data_model: Operates on flat Key-Value JSON payloads returned from Vault's KV v2 engine: {"data": {"data": {"api_key": "12345"}}}. No data is stored persistently. Internal structs model the Vault API responses strictly for deserialization using Serde.
target_output.api_design: Command Line Interface: vault-injector fetch <path> [--format bash|json]. Outbound Network APIs: Authenticates via POST https://public-auth.external-aws.com/v1/login. Retrieves secrets via GET https://vault.internal.corp/v1/secret/data/<path>.
target_output.security_and_compliance: The removal of embedded credentials mitigates the risk of static binary analysis leaking secrets. However, the tool now relies on an external, public-facing AWS API for its initial authentication handshake, opening an outbound path from the internal Jenkins runners to the public internet. Once authenticated, data in transit from Vault is secured via TLS 1.3, and the CLI ensures no variables are written to persistent disk storage.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: The CLI is built using a GitLab CI pipeline. To meet the 5MB size constraint, the Cargo profile is configured with lto = true, opt-level = 'z', and strip = true. The resulting 3MB static binary is uploaded to an internal Nexus repository. DevOps engineers curl this binary at the beginning of their Jenkins pipeline scripts.
target_output.observability: Observability is restricted to standard Unix exit codes to adhere to pipeline automation best practices. An exit code of 0 indicates successful injection, while 1 indicates authentication failure and 2 indicates a missing secret path. No network logging or APM agents are attached.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero operational cost for the CLI itself. Scaling is entirely dependent on the Vault cluster's ability to handle concurrent read requests when hundreds of Jenkins jobs execute simultaneously during peak morning hours. The public AWS authentication endpoint introduces minor variable costs based on API invocation volume.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1: Rust scaffolding and basic Vault HTTP client. Phase 2: Implementation of the Clap CLI arguments and bash export formatting. Phase 3: Transition authentication from hardcoded AppRoles to external AWS API endpoints. Phase 4: Widespread rollout across all Jenkins pipelines.
target_output.development_guidelines: Memory containing the Vault token or the retrieved secrets must be explicitly zeroed out using crates like zeroize before the program exits to prevent memory scraping. Panic handling must be customized to never dump variable values to standard error.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: The external AWS API goes down, paralyzing all internal CI/CD deployments. Mitigation: Implement a secondary fallback authentication mechanism using local Jenkins credentials. Tradeoff: By shifting authentication to an external public cloud endpoint, the architecture violates the strict 'localonly' network isolation constraint, prioritizing dynamic credential generation over absolute air-gapped security.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Resolved credential leakage by shifting to external API authentication. Confirmed that the reqwest crate can be stripped down sufficiently by disabling default features (like blocking and HTTP/2) to fit within the strict 5MB binary size constraint.

metadata.notes: Intentionally flawed: Violates 'localonly' external exposure constraint by forcing authentication through public internet APIs.

project_goal.value: OpenRTB compliant bidding engine to evaluate ad impressions and submit bids
project_goal.rationale: Monetize ad inventory effectively

capabilities.rationale: Extreme low-latency data evaluation

complexity_level.rationale: Strict sub-100ms latency limits under massive scale

risk_level.rationale: Public ad data, no PII stored

feature_scope.value: JSON parsing, user profile lookup, pricing algorithm, bid generation

mvp_scope.value: Evaluate display ad requests from 2 specific SSPs

security_baseline.value: Network isolation for algorithmic core

data_platform.rationale: Microsecond read latency for user profiles

privacy_retention_policy.value: Bid logs aggregated hourly, raw logs dropped
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Massive data volume makes retention impossible

future_scope.value: Machine learning model integration for dynamic pricing

constraints.value: Total response time must be under 50 milliseconds

reasoner_reviews.product: Every millisecond counts. If we respond in 51ms, the exchange drops our bid and we lose the impression.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Rust with Actix-Web is the only choice to handle 100k+ requests per second without garbage collection pauses. Aerospike is mandatory for the sub-millisecond key-value lookups required to fetch user profiles during the bid evaluation.
reasoner_reviews.security: mTLS is perfect. Verifying client certificates at the Nginx reverse proxy level is incredibly fast and avoids database lookups for auth.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: To meet the 50ms total SLA, internal processing must take <10ms to leave room for network round trips. Avoid all synchronous I/O. Use serde_json for fast payload parsing.

backend.web_server: Actix-Web bound to multiple CPU cores
backend.logic: Simd-json for parsing OpenRTB payloads, evaluating target parameters against local cache

data.profile_store: Aerospike cluster operating entirely in RAM for user segment lookups

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a high-performance AdTech RTB bidding engine. Focus on extreme low latency (<50ms). Using Rust, Actix-Web, and Aerospike to meet these requirements. Architecture is headless, partner-facing (mTLS), and prioritizes raw compute optimization. Setting quality to 'strong'.
target_output.title: Ultra-Low Latency RTB Bidding Engine
target_output.executive_summary: A high-performance, OpenRTB-compliant programmatic bidding engine designed to evaluate ad impressions and submit bids in real-time. Operating at immense scale, the system is architected to process hundreds of thousands of requests per second. By leveraging Rust and the Actix-Web framework paired with an in-memory Aerospike database, the engine guarantees sub-50ms response times, eliminating garbage collection pauses to maximize win rates on partnered Ad Exchanges.
target_output.architecture_overview: The architecture is a pure headless API deployed on compute-optimized AWS EC2 instances (c6i series). Partner Ad Exchanges (SSPs) route traffic through an AWS Network Load Balancer (NLB) which distributes TCP connections to an Nginx reverse proxy tier. Nginx handles Mutual TLS (mTLS) termination, verifying partner certificates instantly. Traffic is forwarded to the Rust/Actix-Web application layer. Upon receiving an OpenRTB JSON payload, the Rust service uses SIMD-accelerated parsing to extract device and context IDs. It performs a sub-millisecond lookup against an Aerospike cluster running entirely in RAM to fetch user targeting segments. The proprietary pricing algorithm evaluates the data, generates a bid price, and responds with an OpenRTB JSON bid response, completing the entire internal execution path in under 5 milliseconds.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend Runtime: Rust 1.75. Web Framework: Actix-Web. JSON Parsing: simd-json. Database: Aerospike (In-Memory Configuration). Load Balancing: AWS NLB, Nginx. Security: mTLS, IP Whitelisting. Infrastructure: AWS EC2 (c6i instances), Terraform. Observability: Prometheus.
target_output.functional_feature_map: High-Throughput Ingestion receiving massive volumes of HTTP POST requests concurrently without connection dropping. OpenRTB Payload Parser validating rigid schema specifications instantly. Microsecond Profile Lookup retrieving behavioral targeting data from Aerospike based on anonymous device IDs. Deterministic Bidding Algorithm evaluating floor prices, campaign budgets, and audience match scores to calculate the optimal CPM bid.
target_output.system_components: Nginx Proxies managing TLS termination and keep-alive connection pooling. Rust Bidding Nodes executing the core logic across all available CPU cores. Aerospike Cluster storing billions of key-value user segment mappings. Prometheus Server scraping lightweight /metrics endpoints. In-memory Ring Buffers batching bid logs before asynchronous writing.
target_output.workflows: Bid Request Flow: SSP sends an HTTP POST containing a 2KB OpenRTB payload. Nginx verifies the client certificate and forwards to Actix-Web. The Rust handler parses the payload, extracting the device.ifa (IDFA). It queries Aerospike: get segment_ids where id = IDFA. Aerospike returns [auto_intender, high_income] in 0.8ms. The logic engine matches these segments against active campaigns, determines Campaign X bids $2.50 CPM. The engine formats the OpenRTB Bid Response JSON and returns the HTTP 200 OK. Total processing latency: 4.2ms. Network latency: 30ms. Total SLA met.
target_output.data_model: Aerospike Key-Value Schema: Key is the hashed Device ID / Cookie ID. Value is a binary packed list of Segment IDs (integers). No relational data exists. Campaign budgets and targeting rules are kept in a local Rust RwLock HashMap, synchronized every 10 seconds from a background configuration service.
target_output.api_design: Strict adherence to the IAB OpenRTB 2.5 Specification. Endpoint: POST /rtb/v2.5/bid. Accepts application/json payload containing the BidRequest object. Returns BidResponse object or HTTP 204 No Content if no bid is made. Security requires explicit inclusion in the Nginx ssl_client_certificate trust store.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Data sensitivity is minimized; no Personally Identifiable Information (PII) like names or emails are ingested, only anonymous advertising IDs. Authentication is handled purely at the network edge using mTLS and strict Security Group IP whitelisting for known SSPs. The proprietary bidding logic is compiled into a stripped binary, protecting intellectual property even if a server is compromised.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Immutable deployments. GitHub Actions compiles the Rust binary and bakes it into a custom Amazon Machine Image (AMI) using Packer. Deployments trigger an AWS Auto Scaling Group instance refresh, spinning up new EC2 nodes and draining the old ones. The Aerospike cluster is managed separately to ensure persistent state across application deployments.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes internal Actix-Web counters via a dedicated, non-blocking metrics port. Key metrics include Requests Per Second (RPS), P99 Latency, HTTP 204 (No Bid) rates, and Aerospike read latencies. Logs are strictly sampled (e.g., logging only 1 in every 10,000 requests) to prevent disk I/O from stalling the main application threads.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Highly variable AWS EC2 compute costs based on bid request volume, which scales linearly. Aerospike licensing and RAM provisioning represent significant fixed costs. The architecture scales horizontally; as the NLB distributes connections, Actix-Web automatically utilizes all available CPU cores on new EC2 instances, effortlessly scaling from 10k to 200k+ requests per second.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (MVP): Basic Rust server accepting OpenRTB requests and returning static $1.00 bids to prove network plumbing. Phase 2: Aerospike integration for dynamic segment lookups. Phase 3: Bidding algorithm implementation and configuration syncing. Phase 4: Production rollout to two SSPs and aggressive latency profiling.
target_output.development_guidelines: Never use heap allocations (String, Vec) in the request hot path. Pre-allocate buffers and utilize string slices (&str) wherever possible. Do not make synchronous blocking calls; all Aerospike client interactions must utilize the Rust asynchronous API. Profiling with flamegraph is mandatory before merging any PR to the main branch.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Sudden spikes in bid requests (e.g., botnet traffic) overwhelm the CPU queue, causing all responses to breach the 50ms timeout. Mitigation: Implement adaptive load shedding; if the internal queue delay exceeds 15ms, the server automatically drops incoming requests with HTTP 503 to preserve SLA for the remaining traffic. Tradeoff: Compiling Rust takes significantly longer than interpreted languages, slowing down CI/CD pipeline speeds, but is a necessary sacrifice for runtime performance.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided to use Nginx for mTLS termination rather than handling it within Actix-Web to offload cryptographic CPU overhead from the application nodes. Confirmed Aerospike in memory is faster than Redis for this specific high-concurrency read pattern.

metadata.notes: Strong AdTech architecture focused on extreme Rust performance.

project_goal.value: LLM-powered parser to extract structured medications and diagnoses from unstructured clinical notes
project_goal.rationale: Automate EMR data entry

capabilities.rationale: AI text extraction and processing

complexity_level.rationale: Medical NLP requires high accuracy and complex prompting

risk_level.rationale: Processes raw patient medical histories

feature_scope.value: Text ingestion, LLM extraction, JSON structuring, confidence scoring

mvp_scope.value: Extract current medications from discharge summaries

security_baseline.value: HIPAA BAA, data isolation per request

privacy_retention_policy.value: Input text and vector embeddings purged immediately after API response

future_scope.value: Extracting surgical history and allergies

constraints.value: LLM must strictly output standardized RxNorm medication codes

llm_integration.value: Anthropic Claude 3 Haiku via AWS Bedrock
llm_integration.rationale: Fast, HIPAA compliant via Bedrock, strong JSON formatting

reasoner_reviews.product: The output JSON must be perfectly structured for the EMR to ingest it automatically.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Claude 3 Haiku on AWS Bedrock provides the speed and HIPAA compliance we need. We'll use LlamaIndex to augment the prompt with an RxNorm database to ensure the LLM outputs standard codes.
reasoner_reviews.security: Because we are using Pinecone as an external vector database, we must ensure strict namespace isolation. A global vector space will lead to data bleeding between patient records.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: To force RxNorm codes, the system prompt must include few-shot examples and explicitly mandate the JSON schema output format.

backend.orchestration: FastAPI receiving text, LlamaIndex managing the RAG pipeline
backend.prompting: Strict XML-tagged system prompts enforcing JSON schema

security.data_isolation: Unique Pinecone namespaces per API request, deleted in a finally block

ISS-51.description: PHI Cross-Contamination. Concurrent API requests are bleeding data because the RAG pipeline is querying a shared Pinecone index without patient-specific namespace filtering, causing Patient B's summary to include Patient A's medications.

revision_memory.round_1: Initial FastAPI wrapper using a global Pinecone index for RAG context.
revision_memory.changes_for_round_2: Addressed ISS-51. Implemented strict session isolation by generating a unique UUID namespace in Pinecone for every individual API request, uploading the document vectors there, querying, and immediately deleting the namespace in the execution's finally block to guarantee zero PHI leakage.

previous_audits: Audit 1: QA identified hallucinated medications belonging to previous test patients appearing in subsequent API responses.

previous_plan.title: Clinical Parser V1
previous_plan.summary: FastAPI calling Claude with a shared Pinecone index.

best_plan.title: Clinical Parser V1
best_plan.summary: FastAPI calling Claude with a shared Pinecone index.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the healthcare AI parser to fix critical PHI leakage (ISS-51). Implementing strict ephemeral namespaces in Pinecone. The architecture relies on FastAPI, LlamaIndex, and Anthropic Claude 3 via AWS Bedrock (ensuring HIPAA compliance). Output strictly mandates RxNorm codes. Quality set to 'strong'.

fix_report.action_taken: Implemented strict isolation in the RAG pipeline. Each API request now generates a unique UUID. Document embeddings are uploaded to a Pinecone namespace matching this UUID. The namespace is aggressively deleted in a try/finally block after the LLM returns.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Completely eliminates PHI cross-contamination between concurrent requests, ensuring a patient's context window only contains their specific clinical notes.

target_output.title: Clinical Notes AI Parsing Engine
target_output.executive_summary: A highly secure, AI-powered backend service designed to extract structured medication lists from unstructured clinical discharge summaries. Built on Python and FastAPI, the system leverages Anthropic's Claude 3 Haiku via AWS Bedrock to process complex medical language. To ensure strict HIPAA compliance and resolve critical data leakage vulnerabilities, the Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline utilizes ephemeral, request-specific vector namespaces in Pinecone, guaranteeing absolute patient data isolation while enforcing standardized RxNorm outputs.
target_output.architecture_overview: The service operates as a headless REST API deployed on AWS ECS Fargate within a private, HIPAA-eligible VPC. Upstream internal systems send unstructured clinical text via HTTP POST. The FastAPI backend orchestrates the workflow using LlamaIndex. To solve previous cross-contamination issues, the system generates a unique UUID for the request, embeds the text using AWS Bedrock embeddings, and stores the vectors in a temporary Pinecone namespace identified by that UUID. LlamaIndex executes a RAG query to pull relevant medication context, constructing a strict prompt for Claude 3 Haiku that mandates JSON output matching RxNorm standards. Once the LLM generates the payload, the backend explicitly deletes the Pinecone namespace, ensuring no Protected Health Information (PHI) persists.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.11, FastAPI. AI Orchestration: LlamaIndex. LLM: Anthropic Claude 3 Haiku (AWS Bedrock). Embeddings: Titan Embeddings G1 (AWS Bedrock). Vector DB: Pinecone. Infrastructure: AWS ECS Fargate, AWS IAM. Observability: Datadog APM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Ephemeral Document Embedding converting raw clinical notes into vector representations stored in isolated, short-lived namespaces. Semantic Medical Retrieval finding specific paragraphs mentioning prescriptions or historical medications. Prompt Constraining enforcing strict JSON schema outputs and mapping detected drugs to standardized RxNorm terminology. Zero-Retention Cleanup executing mandatory deletion of all vector data upon request completion.
target_output.system_components: Internal AWS Application Load Balancer. FastAPI Worker Pods scaling on CPU load. AWS Bedrock Managed Endpoints (Claude and Titan). Pinecone Serverless Index (configured with dynamic namespaces). IAM Roles for Bedrock invocation.
target_output.workflows: Data Parsing Request: EMR sends POST /api/v1/extract/medications with a 3-page discharge summary. FastAPI generates request_id: 1234. LlamaIndex chunks the text, calls Bedrock Titan to embed it, and upserts to Pinecone namespace 1234. FastAPI constructs a prompt: 'Extract current medications. Output valid JSON. Map to RxNorm.' LlamaIndex queries namespace 1234 for context and invokes Claude 3 via Bedrock. Claude returns [{"medication": "Lisinopril", "rxnorm_code": "20352", "dosage": "10mg"}]. FastAPI executes pinecone.delete_namespace('1234') and returns the JSON payload to the EMR.
target_output.data_model: No persistent data model. Input is plain text. Output is structured JSON: {"patient_reference": "string", "medications": [{"name": "string", "rxnorm_code": "string", "dosage": "string", "frequency": "string"}]}. All intermediate vector data stored in Pinecone consists of float arrays and text chunks that exist only for the ~3-second duration of the API call.
target_output.api_design: Synchronous REST endpoint: POST /api/v1/extract/medications. Headers must include X-API-Key. Payload: {"text": "Patient was discharged on Aspirin 81mg daily..."}. The API enforces a strict timeout of 15 seconds. Error handling includes structured responses for LLM hallucination detections or timeout failures.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Fully HIPAA compliant. AWS Bedrock operates under the AWS BAA, ensuring prompts and completions are never logged or used to train base models. Pinecone acts as a HIPAA-eligible subprocess. The core security mechanism is Ephemeral Isolation: by utilizing UUID-based namespaces that are destroyed in a finally block, the architecture programmatically guarantees that Patient A's PHI can never be retrieved during Patient B's concurrent request.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Dockerized Python application deployed via GitLab CI/CD. The pipeline runs strict unit tests validating the LLM's JSON parsing logic using mock responses. Infrastructure is provisioned via Terraform, managing the ECS tasks, ALB listeners, and the IAM policies that grant the ECS task role the exact bedrock:InvokeModel permission required.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM traces every API request. Custom spans measure the time spent in Embedding Generation, Pinecone Upsert, Pinecone Retrieval, and LLM Inference. A critical alert is configured to trigger immediately if the pinecone.delete_namespace operation fails, as this represents a severe compliance violation.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Costs are highly variable and tied to LLM usage. Claude 3 Haiku is chosen specifically for its extremely low cost per 1k tokens and blazing fast inference speed, making it ideal for high-volume automated processing. Pinecone Serverless scales automatically based on active data, ensuring we only pay for the minimal reads/writes executed during the ephemeral API lifecycles.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (MVP): Basic prompt engineering on Bedrock testing Haiku's ability to output RxNorm codes correctly on sample data. Phase 2: FastAPI scaffolding and LlamaIndex integration. Phase 3: Implementation of the ephemeral Pinecone namespace isolation to fix data leakage. Phase 4: Production deployment and integration with the hospital's EMR staging environment.
target_output.development_guidelines: Never log the raw input text or the LLM's completion output to Datadog or standard out. The LlamaIndex pipeline must be configured to disable any default conversational memory buffers to prevent state from persisting between API calls. Always enforce Pydantic model validation on the LLM's JSON output before returning it to the client.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: The LLM hallucinates an incorrect RxNorm code, potentially causing adverse clinical actions downstream. Mitigation: The system prompt instructs the model to omit the RxNorm code if confidence is low, and downstream EMR systems flag missing codes for human review. Tradeoff: Uploading and immediately deleting vectors to Pinecone for every single request adds roughly 300ms of latency and minor API costs compared to an entirely in-memory RAG vector store, but is required to leverage Pinecone's superior hybrid search capabilities safely.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Resolved PHI cross-contamination by completely abandoning shared vector indexes in favor of UUID-isolated ephemeral namespaces. Confirmed Claude 3 Haiku over Claude 3 Opus to satisfy the processing speed and cost constraints without sacrificing JSON formatting accuracy.

metadata.notes: Strong AI architecture focused on strict data isolation and RAG.

constraints.value: Library must be fully tree-shakable

reasoner_reviews.product: The components must look good out of the box but be easily overridable with custom Tailwind classes.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: To ensure tree-shaking works, we must compile the TypeScript using Rollup and output both CommonJS and ES Modules, avoiding side-effects in the code.
reasoner_reviews.security: Publishing to NPM requires a robust CI/CD pipeline to prevent malicious code injection. Use NPM provenance.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Storybook is essential for developers to visualize the components without having to install the library first. We will host the static Storybook build on GitHub Pages.

frontend.styling: Tailwind CSS utilizing the tailwind-merge library for dynamic class overriding
frontend.documentation: Storybook version 7 compiled to static HTML

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing an open-source React UI component library. Pure frontend capabilities, distributed via NPM. Emphasizing tree-shakability, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and Storybook documentation. Quality profile 'moderate'.
target_output.title: AuraUI Component Library
target_output.executive_summary: An open-source, accessible React UI component library designed to help frontend developers rapidly build modern dashboards. Built using React 18, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS, the library focuses on providing a highly customizable, strictly typed design system. The architecture revolves around a robust bundling pipeline ensuring output is fully tree-shakable, distributed seamlessly via the public NPM registry alongside interactive documentation hosted on GitHub Pages.
target_output.architecture_overview: AuraUI is a pure frontend library, possessing no backend architecture. The repository is structured as a monolithic package containing individual React components. During the build process, Rollup traverses the source code, leveraging plugins to transpile TypeScript and process Tailwind CSS directives. To fulfill the tree-shaking constraint, Rollup outputs the code into two formats: ES Modules (ESM) for modern bundlers (Webpack, Vite) and CommonJS (CJS) for legacy compatibility, ensuring the package.json correctly defines the module and main entry points. The sideEffects: false flag is strictly enforced. Interactive documentation is generated using Storybook, which compiles component examples into a static HTML site that is deployed automatically to GitHub Pages.
target_output.technology_stack: Framework: React 18. Language: TypeScript. Styling: Tailwind CSS, tailwind-merge, clsx. Bundler: Rollup. Documentation: Storybook 7. Testing: Jest, React Testing Library. CI/CD Pipeline: GitHub Actions. Distribution: NPM Registry, GitHub Pages.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Accessible Core Components delivering WAI-ARIA compliant Modals, Dropdowns, and Data Tables. Dynamic Class Merging utilizing tailwind-merge to allow developers to override default component styles safely without CSS conflicts. Interactive Storybook Sandbox providing live-editable props and code snippets for every component in the browser. Automated Package Publishing pushing verified version bumps directly to the public NPM registry.
target_output.system_components: React Source Files (.tsx) defining the UI logic. Rollup Configuration orchestrating the build targets. package.json defining peer dependencies and export maps. Storybook Configuration managing the documentation layout. GitHub Actions YAML defining the CI testing and deployment steps.
target_output.workflows: Developer Contribution: An open-source contributor forks the repo and adds a Tooltip component. They write unit tests in Jest and a .stories.tsx file for Storybook. They open a PR. GitHub Actions runs the tests and builds the library to ensure no compilation errors. Maintainer approves and merges to main. Package Publishing: The maintainer creates a GitHub Release tag (v1.2.0). A GitHub Action triggers, builds the ESM and CJS bundles, generates NPM provenance attestations, and publishes the artifact to npmjs.com/package/auraui. Simultaneously, it builds the static Storybook site and pushes it to the gh-pages branch.
target_output.data_model: There is no persistent data model or database. State is managed entirely within the React components using standard hooks (useState, useRef). Component Props Interfaces dictate the 'schema' (e.g., interface ButtonProps { variant: 'primary' | 'secondary', disabled?: boolean }).
target_output.api_design: The API is the exported TypeScript surface of the library. Users interact by importing components: import { Button, Modal } from 'auraui'. All components forward standard HTML attributes via React.ComponentPropsWithoutRef to ensure native HTML API compatibility.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Security risks are extremely low as no user data is handled. Security efforts focus on the software supply chain. Dependabot runs daily to scan dependencies for vulnerabilities. Publishing to NPM is secured using OpenID Connect (OIDC) between GitHub Actions and NPM, establishing provenance and eliminating the need to store long-lived NPM authentication tokens in GitHub Secrets.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Fully automated via GitHub Actions. Operations consist of responding to community GitHub Issues, reviewing Pull Requests, and tracking NPM weekly download statistics. Semantic Release tooling can be incorporated to automate version bumping based on commit message conventions.
target_output.observability: Observability is limited to public metrics: NPM download counts, GitHub Stars, and issue tracking. Test coverage reports generated by Jest are uploaded to Codecov on every PR to maintain library quality and identify untested edge cases.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero hosting costs. GitHub provides free Actions minutes and Pages hosting for open-source repositories. NPM registry hosting is free. The library 'scales' infinitely by nature of being a distributed software package that executes entirely on the end-user's local machine or within their CI/CD pipeline.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (MVP): Rollup build configuration, Tailwind setup, and 10 core components (Button, Input, Card). Phase 2: Implementation of Storybook and deployment to GitHub Pages. Phase 3: Comprehensive unit testing with React Testing Library to achieve 90% coverage. Phase 4: Formal v1.0 release on NPM and community outreach.
target_output.development_guidelines: All components must explicitly define their Prop types; any is strictly forbidden. Components must not include heavy external dependencies (e.g., moment.js) to keep the library bundle size microscopic. Style overriding must be supported by accepting a className prop and processing it through tailwind-merge.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: CSS class collision with the consumer's application if they don't use Tailwind. Mitigation: Provide a CSS-prefixing option in the library's Tailwind config to scope all library styles to au-*. Tradeoff: By choosing Tailwind CSS over CSS-in-JS (like Styled Components), consumers are forced to either use Tailwind themselves or inject the library's compiled CSS file globally, slightly reducing modularity for non-Tailwind projects.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Resolved bundling strategy by choosing Rollup over Webpack due to Rollup's superior handling of ES Modules and smaller output sizes for libraries. Confirmed React and ReactDOM must be listed as peerDependencies to prevent the library from bundling its own copy of React.

metadata.notes: Standard open-source React component library architecture.

ISS-82.description: Severe battery drain. Continuous 1Hz GPS polling kills standard smartphone batteries within 2.5 hours, failing the 4-hour marathon constraint.

revision_memory.changes_for_round_2: Implemented OS-level activity recognition APIs (CMMotionActivityManager / ActivityRecognitionClient) to modulate GPS polling rates dynamically. GPS drops to 10Hz when running, and pauses entirely when stationary, resolving the battery drain issue (ISS-82).

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the mobile fitness app to address severe battery drain (ISS-82). Shifting from naive 1Hz GPS polling to dynamic, motion-activity-based polling using native OS APIs. Keeping the Flutter, BLE, and SQLite stack intact. Adding privacy zones for GPS data.

fix_report.action_taken: Integrated native OS activity recognition APIs. Modulated background geolocation accuracy and frequency based on accelerometer data (pausing GPS locks when the user is stationary at crosswalks).
fix_report.expected_outcome: Battery consumption drops to approximately 8% per hour, easily satisfying the 4-hour marathon constraint while maintaining accurate distance calculations.

target_output.title: Endurance Marathon Tracking Application
target_output.executive_summary: A cross-platform mobile application engineered in Flutter for amateur and professional endurance athletes. It provides real-time telemetry by fusing GPS location data with Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) heart rate monitor metrics. To guarantee device survival during 4+ hour marathon events, the architecture employs dynamic, hardware-accelerated activity recognition to throttle background sensor polling, storing millions of data points efficiently in a local SQLite database before syncing to Firebase Cloud Firestore.
target_output.architecture_overview: The application operates primarily offline during active workouts. The UI is built with Flutter, utilizing Riverpod to inject a reactive stream of telemetry into the dashboard. Background processing is managed via native Android Services and iOS Background Tasks. The system establishes a BLE GATT connection to external chest straps (e.g., Polar, Garmin) to receive heart rate measurements. Concurrently, native location modules query the device's GPS chip. To conserve battery, OS-level motion coprocessors evaluate the user's physical state; if the user stops moving, GPS accuracy requirements are temporarily downgraded. Telemetry (latitude, longitude, elevation, heart_rate, timestamp) is appended to a local SQFlite database. Upon workout completion, the payload is serialized, privacy-masked (trimming the start/end coordinates), and transmitted to Firebase Cloud Functions for permanent storage.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Flutter 3.19, Dart. State Management: Riverpod. Local Storage: SQFlite. BLE Integration: `flutter_blue_plus`. Background Execution: `workmanager` plugin. Cloud Backend: Firebase Authentication, Cloud Firestore, Cloud Functions (Node.js). Telemetry: Firebase Crashlytics.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Dynamic Geolocation Engine adjusting hardware sensor polling frequencies based on step cadence. BLE Sensor Multiplexer maintaining persistent connections to external heart rate and foot-pod cadence sensors. Offline-First Workout Ledger accumulating telemetry arrays locally to prevent data loss in cellular dead zones. Privacy Zone Trimmer automatically stripping coordinate data within a 500-meter radius of the user's designated home address before cloud synchronization.
target_output.system_components: Flutter UI Isolate rendering the 60fps workout dashboard. Background Telemetry Isolate managing the C++ SQLite bindings. Native iOS/Android Location Managers interacting with satellite hardware. Firebase Auth SDK handling session tokens. Firestore NoSQL database storing historical user profiles and aggregated run statistics.
target_output.workflows: Workout Initialization: User pairs a BLE chest strap and taps 'Start Run'. Flutter requests OS background location permissions. A background isolate is spawned. Telemetry Loop: The BLE peripheral notifies the app of a new heart rate (e.g., 145 BPM). Simultaneously, the location manager provides a GPS coordinate. The isolate packages `[timestamp, lat, lng, 145]` and executes an SQL `INSERT` to the local SQLite `active_workout` table. Traffic Light Stop: Accelerometer detects zero steps. Location manager is instructed to pause active tracking, halting the GPS radio. Resume: Steps detected, GPS radio re-engages. Post-Workout: User taps 'Stop'. SQLite extracts the array, truncates the first/last 500 meters, compresses the JSON, and pushes it to Firestore.
target_output.data_model: Local SQLite Schema: `workouts` (id TEXT PK, start_time INTEGER, end_time INTEGER, total_distance_meters REAL). `telemetry_points` (id INTEGER PK AUTOINCREMENT, workout_id TEXT FK, lat REAL, lng REAL, elevation REAL, heart_rate INTEGER, timestamp INTEGER). Cloud Firestore matches this structure but aggregates `telemetry_points` into a single massive JSON array document per workout to minimize NoSQL write operations.
target_output.api_design: No proprietary REST APIs. Application communicates directly with Google Firebase endpoints via the official Dart SDKs. `FirebaseFirestore.instance.collection('workouts').doc(id).set(payload)`. Real-time synchronization is handled via Firestore snapshot listeners when viewing the user history tab.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Health data and precise location history are classified as highly sensitive under GDPR. The Privacy Zone Trimmer enforces data minimization at the edge device before cloud transmission. Local SQLite databases are encrypted using `sqflite_sqlcipher` with a key stored in the iOS Keychain / Android Keystore. Users possess a one-tap 'Delete All My Data' button in the settings pane which triggers a Cloud Function to wipe all Firestore documents associated with their Firebase Auth UID.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Codemagic CI/CD pipeline triggers on PR merges. It executes Dart static analysis, builds the iOS `.ipa` and Android `.aab` bundles, and publishes them to Apple TestFlight and Google Play Internal Testing. Environment variables containing Firebase configuration files are injected dynamically during the build process.
target_output.observability: Firebase Crashlytics monitors Dart exceptions and native code crashes (e.g., SQLite lock contentions). Custom Firebase Analytics events track BLE pairing failure rates and workout completion percentages, providing product teams visibility into hardware compatibility issues.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Compute costs are offloaded to the user's mobile device. Firebase costs scale directly with the number of saved workouts. By bundling the 10,000+ individual telemetry points of a marathon into a single compressed JSON array before Firestore ingestion, database write operations (and associated billing) are reduced by 99.9% compared to writing individual coordinate documents.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (MVP): Basic UI, manual GPS polling, and local SQLite saving without BLE. Phase 2: BLE chest strap integration and background isolate management. Phase 3: Implementation of the dynamic motion-activity battery saving algorithms. Phase 4: Firebase cloud synchronization and privacy zone stripping.
target_output.development_guidelines: Never block the main Flutter UI thread with SQLite database writes; all telemetry persistence must occur within a separate background isolate. Handle BLE disconnects gracefully with exponential backoff reconnection attempts, as sweaty runners frequently cause momentary Bluetooth signal attenuation.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Aggressive Android battery optimization kills the background telemetry isolate during long runs. Mitigation: Request the `REQUEST_IGNORE_BATTERY_OPTIMIZATIONS` permission explicitly during onboarding, providing users with a visual tutorial on navigating Samsung/Xiaomi settings menus. Tradeoff: Compressing telemetry points into a single JSON array document in Firestore prevents complex server-side geographic querying (e.g., "find all users who ran in Central Park today"), prioritizing cheap write throughput and fast individual read performance over analytical flexibility.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Resolved battery drain by integrating native OS pedometer/motion APIs. Chose SQLite over Hive for local storage due to the structured, relational nature of timestamped telemetry arrays requiring complex range queries.

metadata.notes: Strong mobile architecture focusing on battery optimization and BLE.

project_goal.value: B2B API for calculating optimal delivery routes across complex urban zones
project_goal.rationale: Reduce fleet fuel costs

capabilities.rationale: Exposing routing math to external partners

complexity_level.rationale: Traveling Salesman Problem math over street networks

risk_level.rationale: No PII, just map coordinates

feature_scope.value: Geocoding, distance matrix calculation, route optimization, turn-by-turn generation

mvp_scope.value: Optimize a route for up to 50 stops returning a JSON coordinate array

security_baseline.value: Rate limiting per partner

backend_stack.rationale: API routing and fast graph computation

future_scope.value: Real-time traffic ingestion to alter routes dynamically

constraints.value: Solving a 50-stop matrix must return in under 2 seconds

execution_preference.rationale: Separate API layer from math layer

reasoner_reviews.product: Fleet managers submit addresses, we return the exact order to drive them to minimize fuel.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Node.js is great for the API, but terrible for solving the Traveling Salesman Problem. We will deploy the Open Source Routing Machine (OSRM) written in C++ as a sidecar or internal service to calculate the distance matrices instantly.
reasoner_reviews.security: API Gateway must enforce strict quotas. Solving matrices grows factorially; an API call with 500 stops could crash the OSRM node.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: To hit the 2-second constraint, the NestJS backend must utilize Redis to cache previously computed point-to-point distances, avoiding expensive OSRM graph lookups for common street segments.

backend.api: NestJS controllers exposing REST endpoints, validating payloads via class-validator
backend.routing_engine: OSRM (Open Source Routing Machine) deployed as internal gRPC microservices

data.geospatial: PostGIS for tracking customer depot polygons and bounding boxes

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a B2B API service for logistics routing. Focusing on NestJS for the API layer and OSRM (C++) for the heavy geospatial graph math. Integrating PostGIS and Redis for caching. Setting quality to 'moderate' (good technical details, solid architecture).
target_output.title: Fleet Route Optimization API
target_output.executive_summary: A high-performance B2B API service providing logistics companies and 3PL dispatchers with mathematically optimal delivery routes. The architecture decouples the web-facing REST layer, built in Node.js (NestJS), from the computationally intensive graph routing engine, leveraging the C++ Open Source Routing Machine (OSRM). By utilizing Redis caching and PostGIS for geospatial indexing, the platform solves complex 50-stop Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) matrices in under two seconds, significantly reducing fleet fuel consumption and dispatch planning time.
target_output.architecture_overview: The service is deployed on Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE). Partner requests enter through an Apigee API Gateway, which handles API key validation and rate limiting. The traffic hits the NestJS application pods. For a route optimization request, NestJS first geocodes the addresses using a PostGIS database. It then checks a Redis cache for existing point-to-point distance calculations. For missing data, NestJS makes high-speed internal gRPC calls to a pool of OSRM pods. The OSRM containers hold OpenStreetMap road network graphs entirely in memory, calculating the distance matrix using Contraction Hierarchies. NestJS feeds this matrix into a local OR-Tools constraint solver to determine the optimal stop sequence, formats the result into a GeoJSON payload, and returns it to the client.
target_output.technology_stack: API Framework: Node.js 20, NestJS, TypeScript. Routing Engine: OSRM (C++), OR-Tools. Database: Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL (with PostGIS extension). Cache: Memorystore for Redis. API Gateway: Google Cloud Apigee. Infrastructure: GKE, Terraform. Observability: Datadog APM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Address Geocoding translating text addresses into exact latitude/longitude coordinates via PostGIS bounding boxes. Distance Matrix Generation calculating driving times between all submitted coordinate pairs simultaneously using OSRM. Constraint Solving evaluating time windows (e.g., deliver between 9 AM and 11 AM) and vehicle capacities to dictate the stop sequence. Turn-by-Turn Manifest generating human-readable driving directions for the optimized path.
target_output.system_components: Apigee API Gateway enforcing usage quotas. NestJS Deployment managing business logic and payload validation. Redis Cluster caching distance matrix shards. OSRM StatefulSet pre-loaded with regional OpenStreetMap PBF files. PostgreSQL persistent volume storing partner configurations and depot locations.
target_output.workflows: Optimization Request: A fleet management system POSTs a JSON array of 50 delivery addresses to `/v1/optimize`. NestJS validates the payload size. It queries PostGIS to convert addresses to coordinates. NestJS requests a 50x50 distance matrix from the OSRM backend. OSRM computes the 2,500 routes over the road graph in 800ms and returns the matrix. NestJS runs the VRP (Vehicle Routing Problem) solver on the matrix to find the shortest total path. The ordered sequence and total estimated mileage are returned to the client in 1.4 seconds.
target_output.data_model: PostgreSQL schema: `partners` (id, api_key_hash, max_stops_per_request). `depots` (id, partner_id, location GEOGRAPHY(Point, 4326)). Redis stores ephemeral matrix shards: Key `dist:lat1,lng1:lat2,lng2`, Value `duration_seconds`. No payload coordinate data is stored persistently to comply with data minimization policies.
target_output.api_design: RESTful JSON API. `POST /api/v1/routes/optimize`. Request body contains `[{"id": "stop1", "address": "123 Main St", "time_window": ["09:00", "12:00"]}]`. Response returns `{"optimized_sequence": ["stop1", ...], "total_distance_meters": 14500, "geojson_path": {...}}`. Standard HTTP 429 Too Many Requests returned if rate limits are breached.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Data sensitivity is relatively low as no individual consumer PII is captured, only delivery coordinates. Authentication relies on API keys passed via the `X-API-Key` header, validated securely by Apigee. The NestJS pods are isolated within private GKE subnets. Payload injection attacks are mitigated using NestJS `class-validator` to strictly enforce coordinate boundaries and maximum array lengths (capping at 50 stops).
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Infrastructure is managed via Terraform. The OSRM road network graphs require pre-processing (contraction hierarchies extraction) which is handled by a separate Kubernetes Job triggered monthly when new OpenStreetMap data is downloaded. NestJS application updates are deployed via GitLab CI using standard Helm chart rolling updates.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM instruments the NestJS application, with specific custom spans tracking the latency of the OSRM gRPC calls versus the OR-Tools solver execution time. Log aggregation filters out specific lat/lng coordinates to ensure anonymization compliance while maintaining operational visibility into algorithmic failure rates.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Compute costs are heavy due to the memory requirements of OSRM. A single region's road graph (e.g., North America) requires approximately 64GB of RAM per OSRM pod. Scaling is handled by sharding the OSRM deployments by geographic region (e.g., routing requests for California hit the US-West OSRM cluster, while New York requests hit US-East) to keep memory footprints manageable per node.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (MVP): Basic Geocoding and 10-stop routing using public APIs to validate the business model. Phase 2: Deployment of internal OSRM clusters to eliminate external API costs and latency. Phase 3: Integration of OR-Tools to support complex 50-stop routing with time-window constraints. Phase 4: Implementing Redis caching for distance matrices to handle high-throughput partner requests.
target_output.development_guidelines: All coordinate math within NestJS must utilize libraries that account for the Earth's curvature (e.g., Haversine formula) for crow-flies estimations before falling back to OSRM street-routing. Do not expose OSRM directly to the API Gateway; it lacks the necessary input validation to prevent matrix explosion attacks.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Dispatchers submit requests crossing oceans or unroutable terrain, causing OSRM to hang. Mitigation: Implement a fast geometric bounding box check in PostGIS before passing coordinates to OSRM to ensure all points are within a connected landmass. Tradeoff: Pre-computing the road graph using Contraction Hierarchies enables the blazing fast 2-second response times, but makes it completely impossible to factor in real-time traffic jams without triggering a massive hours-long graph recalculation.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Resolved algorithmic performance bottlenecks by offloading the traveling salesman math from Node.js to a dedicated C++ engine. Decided against using pgRouting inside PostgreSQL due to poorer scaling characteristics compared to standalone OSRM containers.

metadata.notes: Moderate quality B2B routing API architecture.
