observability_baseline.value: Prometheus metrics for consumer lag, Grafana dashboards

reasoner_reviews.product: Focusing purely on the ingestion and route deviation is perfect for the MVP. Dispatchers need exact location visibility without dropping packets during cellular dead zones.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Using MSK as the buffer ensures we don't lose data during Flink redeployments. Iceberg is the right choice for handling the massive volume of append-only GPS records while allowing fast Athena queries.
reasoner_reviews.security: mTLS is non-negotiable for IoT ingestion. Each truck must have a uniquely provisioned certificate rotated every 90 days via an MDM solution.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Achieving 500ms end-to-end latency means Flink windowing must be carefully tuned, likely using processing time rather than event time for the live dashboard path.

backend.stream_topology: Source (Kafka) -> Filter (Invalid GPS) -> KeyBy (TruckID) -> Window (10s tumbling) -> Sink (PostgreSQL for live, Iceberg for cold)
backend.state_backend: RocksDB with incremental checkpoints to S3

data.schema_registry: Confluent Schema Registry utilizing Protobuf for payload compaction
data.partitioning: Iceberg tables partitioned by day and geohash prefix

devops.iac: Terraform for MSK clusters and EMR Studio setup
devops.monitoring: JMX Exporter on Flink TaskManagers scraping into Amazon Managed Prometheus

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a high-throughput streaming data pipeline for logistics telemetry. Opting for AWS MSK and Flink to handle the 10Hz GPS pings from 5000 trucks. Ensuring strict mTLS authentication at the MQTT broker edge. Data is bifurcated: a hot path to PostgreSQL for the 500ms latency dispatch dashboard, and a cold path to Apache Iceberg for historical routing analysis.
target_output.title: Fleet Telemetry Streaming Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A high-volume data pipeline built to ingest, process, and store 10Hz GPS and engine telemetry from a fleet of 5000 logistics vehicles. Utilizing an MQTT-to-Kafka ingestion bridge, Apache Flink for real-time stream processing, and Apache Iceberg for cost-effective historical storage, enabling both sub-second dispatch visibility and long-term routing optimization.
target_output.architecture_overview: IoT devices on trucks publish Protobuf-encoded payloads over MQTT with mTLS authentication to an AWS IoT Core broker, which routes messages directly into an Amazon MSK (Kafka) cluster. An Apache Flink application running on Amazon EMR consumes the streams, performing stateful deduplication, geofence intersection calculations, and speed aggregations. The Flink job utilizes a dual-sink pattern: upserting live truck states into an Amazon RDS PostgreSQL instance for the dispatch dashboard, and appending raw immutable logs into S3-backed Apache Iceberg tables for offline batch processing.
target_output.technology_stack: Ingestion: AWS IoT Core, MQTT, X.509 Certificates. Message Broker: Amazon MSK (Kafka 3.5.1), Confluent Schema Registry (Protobuf). Stream Processing: Java 21, Apache Flink 1.18, RocksDB state backend. Cold Storage: Amazon S3, Apache Iceberg, AWS Glue Data Catalog. Orchestration: Apache Airflow (Python 3.11).
target_output.functional_feature_map: Edge Ingestion capturing latitude, longitude, heading, and speed from vehicle CAN buses. Stream Deduplication dropping redundant cellular retries. Geofence Intersection alerting when trucks deviate from predefined warehouse corridors. Daily Rollup Batch Jobs computing total mileage, idle time, and average speeds per driver shift.
target_output.system_components: MQTT Edge Gateway handling device connections and cert validation. Telemetry Topic holding 7 days of raw retention with 64 partitions. Flink JobManager coordinating distributed processing. Flink TaskManagers executing tumbling windows. Iceberg Metastore tracking S3 parquet file manifests. Airflow Scheduler triggering nightly compaction and aggregation dags.
target_output.workflows: Telemetry Ingestion: Truck modem connects via mTLS, publishes payload, IoT Core validates rule, forwards to MSK `raw_telemetry` topic. Deviation Detection: Flink reads topic, keys by `vehicle_id`, joins against broadcast state containing active route geofences, emits alert event to `dispatch_alerts` topic if GPS coordinates fall outside the polygon for 3 consecutive pings. Compaction: Airflow triggers nightly Spark job reading Iceberg small files, writing optimized Parquet blocks, and updating Glue manifest.
target_output.data_model: raw_gps_pings (vehicle_id VARCHAR, timestamp TIMESTAMP, lat DOUBLE, lon DOUBLE, heading INT, speed_kmh INT, accuracy_meters FLOAT). vehicle_sessions (session_id UUID, driver_id VARCHAR, vehicle_id VARCHAR, start_time TIMESTAMP, end_time TIMESTAMP). route_geofences (route_id UUID, polygon_wkt TEXT, max_allowed_speed INT). driver_daily_stats (driver_id VARCHAR, date DATE, total_distance_km FLOAT, harsh_braking_events INT).
target_output.api_design: Internal data consumption via Athena JDBC. Flink exposes REST API for savepoint triggering: POST /jobs/:jobid/savepoints with target directory. Airflow exposes REST for manual DAG runs: POST /api/v1/dags/compact_iceberg/dagRuns.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Device authentication enforced via strict X.509 certificate validation at IoT Core. All data in MSK encrypted at rest via AWS KMS customer-managed keys. TLS 1.3 enforced for all internal VPC traffic between Flink and Kafka. S3 buckets configured with Block Public Access and bucket policies restricting reads to specific IAM roles.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Infrastructure provisioned via Terraform including VPCs, MSK brokers, and S3 buckets. CI/CD via GitLab CI building Flink shadow JARs, uploading to S3, and calling EMR API to submit jobs. Zero-downtime upgrades managed by taking Flink savepoints, tearing down the cluster, and restoring state on the new EMR cluster. Flink checkpoints configured to S3 every 3 minutes.
target_output.observability: Amazon Managed Prometheus scraping Flink JMX metrics (numRecordsInPerSecond, consumer_lag). Grafana dashboards visualizing throughput and MSK CPU utilization. CloudWatch Alarms mapped to PagerDuty for consumer lag exceeding 10,000 messages or TaskManager heap memory exceeding 90%. Iceberg table snapshot history monitored for orphaned files.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $4,500/month. MSK provisioned with kafka.m5.large instances auto-scaling storage. EMR scales TaskManagers based on CPU. Major cost optimization achieved by using S3 Standard-IA for Iceberg files older than 30 days and aggressive Parquet dictionary encoding on telemetry floats.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1: Configure IoT Core, MSK, and basic S3 dumping to validate ingestion scale. Phase 2: Deploy Flink application for live PostgreSQL updates and geofence alerts. Phase 3: Implement Iceberg table format, Athena integration, and Airflow nightly compaction jobs. Phase 4: Build downstream ML models for ETA prediction.
target_output.development_guidelines: Strict schema evolution rules: Protobuf fields can only be added, never removed or renamed. Flink state schema must maintain backward compatibility for savepoint restoration. All data engineering code must pass unit tests utilizing Flink's MiniCluster for local execution.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Cellular network dropouts causing massive late-arriving data spikes. Mitigation: Flink configured with 10-minute allowed lateness and watermark strategies prioritizing processing time for dashboards. Tradeoff: Using processing time means the live dashboard might reflect events out of true chronological order, accepted to meet the strict 500ms latency constraint.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided against Apache Hudi in favor of Apache Iceberg due to better support for Python ecosystems in downstream ML tasks. Confirmed Amazon MSK over self-managed Kafka to reduce operational burden.

metadata.notes: Strong domain-specific pipeline architecture.

reasoner_reviews.product: MVP constraints are good. By focusing just on Sabre and Amadeus flights, we can nail the scatter-gather latency issues before introducing the complexity of fragmented hotel APIs.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go routines are perfect for the fan-out search requests to multiple GDS XML APIs. Aerospike will handle the massive read/write volume of volatile flight pricing data better than Redis.
reasoner_reviews.security: Since we are issuing Virtual Credit Cards (VCCs) for B2B settlement, the system falls strictly under PCI-DSS. All PANs must be tokenized at the edge by a service like VGS before hitting our Go backend.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Achieving 800ms p95 latency while hitting legacy GDS SOAP APIs is impossible without aggressive caching. We must implement pre-computation of popular route pricing in Aerospike.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the B2B Travel API architecture to address critical PCI and latency issues. Implementing VGS (Very Good Security) as an edge proxy to tokenize credit cards, vastly reducing our PCI compliance scope. To meet the strict 800ms p95 latency requirement against notoriously slow legacy GDS SOAP APIs, I'm detailing a robust background cache-warming strategy using Aerospike for high-volume route pricing. The core backend remains Go, leveraging goroutines for efficient scatter-gather searches.

target_output.title: High-Performance B2B Travel Distribution Gateway
target_output.executive_summary: A resilient, low-latency API service designed to aggregate flight inventory across major Global Distribution Systems (GDS) like Sabre and Amadeus. Built with Go and deployed on Google Kubernetes Engine, the platform leverages Aerospike for aggressive price caching and CockroachDB for distributed booking transactions. Edge-level tokenization ensures strict PCI-DSS Level 1 compliance while facilitating secure Virtual Credit Card (VCC) settlements.
target_output.architecture_overview: B2B partners connect via mTLS to a GCP External HTTP(S) Load Balancer. Traffic passes through a VGS (Very Good Security) proxy which intercepts and tokenizes any PCI data (PANs) before forwarding the request to the Go-based API running on GKE. Search requests trigger a scatter-gather pattern: the API first checks an Aerospike cluster for cached fares; on a cache miss, Go routines fan out parallel requests to Amadeus and Sabre XML/SOAP APIs through Anti-Corruption Layer (ACL) microservices. Bookings are transacted synchronously in CockroachDB to guarantee ACID properties across multi-region deployments, utilizing VCC integrations for backend supplier settlement.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Go 1.22 with Fiber web framework. Edge/Security: VGS for tokenization, Envoy sidecars for mTLS termination. Database: CockroachDB Serverless. Cache: Aerospike Enterprise. Integrations: Sabre SOAP API, Amadeus REST API. Infrastructure: GCP GKE, Cloud Armor WAF. Observability: OpenTelemetry, Jaeger, Prometheus.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Flight Search Engine executing multi-city and round-trip queries. GDS Aggregator normalizing disparate supplier formats into a unified JSON ontology. Cache Warmer daemon autonomously fetching pricing for high-traffic O&D (Origin & Destination) pairs. Booking Orchestrator managing PNR (Passenger Name Record) creation and ticketing workflows. Payment Settlement generating per-transaction Virtual Credit Cards.
target_output.system_components: API Gateway (Fiber) exposing GraphQL search and REST booking endpoints. Supplier Connectors (Go gRPC services) translating internal domain models to GDS-specific payloads. Cache Worker (Go daemon) running continuous background polling. Tokenization Proxy (VGS) deployed at the network edge. CockroachDB cluster spanned across three GCP regions (us-central1, us-east1, us-west1) for strict high availability.
target_output.workflows: Aggregated Search: OTA calls POST /v1/flights/search. API queries Aerospike. If hit, returns immediately. If miss, spawns goroutines to Sabre and Amadeus connectors. Connectors apply XSLT/JSON mapping, execute external calls with 600ms context timeouts. Results are aggregated, deduplicated, sorted by price, asynchronously written to Aerospike (15m TTL), and returned to OTA. Secure Booking: OTA submits passenger details and card token. Booking service opens CockroachDB transaction, calls GDS to lock seat and generate PNR. Payment service calls VCC provider to issue single-use card, passes VCC to GDS for ticketing. Transaction commits.
target_output.data_model: pnr_records (pnr_locator VARCHAR PK, partner_id UUID, gds_source VARCHAR, status ENUM ticketed/cancelled, total_amount DECIMAL, created_at TIMESTAMP). passengers (id UUID PK, pnr_locator FK, first_name_hash, last_name_hash, dob, passport_token). flight_segments (id UUID PK, pnr_locator FK, carrier_code VARCHAR, flight_number VARCHAR, departure_airport VARCHAR, arrival_airport VARCHAR, departure_time TIMESTAMP). cached_fares (route_key VARCHAR PK, payload_blob BYTES, expires_at TIMESTAMP) stored in Aerospike.
target_output.api_design: RESTful Booking API: POST /v1/bookings (creates PNR), GET /v1/bookings/{pnr} (retrieves status), POST /v1/bookings/{pnr}/cancel (voids ticket). GraphQL Search API: query SearchFlights(origin: "JFK", dest: "LHR", date: "2026-10-12") { itineraries { carrier, duration, price { amount, currency } } }. Rate limiting applied per partner via API keys.
target_output.security_and_compliance: PCI-DSS Level 1 achieved by offloading PAN storage to VGS; the internal network only processes opaque tokens. mTLS enforced at the ingress controller for partner identity verification. Passports and DOBs encrypted at rest in CockroachDB using application-level AES-256-GCM. GDPR minimization script purges clear-text passport details from the database 24 hours after the final flight segment lands.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Fully automated CI/CD via GitHub Actions. Commits trigger Go unit tests and pact contract tests against mocked GDS endpoints. Deployments use ArgoCD for GitOps synchronization to GKE. Envoy sidecars inject network policies restricting egress traffic solely to whitelisted supplier IPs. CockroachDB schema migrations managed via golang-migrate integrated into the deployment pipeline.
target_output.observability: Distributed tracing is critical for the scatter-gather pattern; OpenTelemetry SDKs instrument all Go services, propagating trace IDs to Jaeger. This allows precise measurement of Amadeus vs Sabre response times. Prometheus scrapes custom Go metrics (cache_hit_ratio, supplier_timeout_count). PagerDuty alerts fire if p95 search latency exceeds 800ms or if the booking success rate drops below 98%.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: High compute efficiency achieved via Go's lightweight goroutines, allowing thousands of concurrent searches per pod. Aerospike deployed on NVMe instances ensures cost-effective high-density caching compared to RAM-only Redis. CockroachDB multi-region deployment is the primary cost driver, justified by the requirement for zero-RPO financial transactions.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Months 1-2): Core Go framework, VGS integration, and single-supplier (Sabre) search/book flows. Phase 2 (Months 3-4): Aerospike caching layer, background warming workers, and Amadeus integration. Phase 3 (Months 5-6): CockroachDB multi-region setup, VCC automated settlement, and comprehensive load testing against 800ms SLAs.
target_output.development_guidelines: Strict adherence to Domain-Driven Design (DDD). Supplier integrations must be cordoned off in the 'infrastructure' layer, never bleeding XML specifics into the core 'domain' logic. All external HTTP calls must utilize `context.WithTimeout`. Comprehensive mocking of Sabre/Amadeus using Hoverfly for local development without incurring API test fees.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GDS rate limiting during aggressive background cache warming. Mitigation: Implement adaptive backoff and token bucket algorithms tailored to supplier-specific API limits. Tradeoff: Eventual consistency in pricing; cached fares may occasionally result in booking failures if the underlying seat is sold before the 15-minute Aerospike TTL expires. Addressed by returning clear 'FARE_EXPIRED' error codes to B2B partners.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided against GraphQL for the mutation (booking) API, retaining REST for simpler idempotency key handling. Selected VGS over self-managed HashiCorp Vault for PCI tokenization to accelerate compliance certification.

metadata.notes: Effectively resolves PCI and latency issues.

project.goal: Build iOS BLE fitness tracker app

reasoner_reviews.product: The privacy-first, local-only approach for MVP is a great way to sidestep complex cloud HIPAA/GDPR compliance while the hardware is validated in the market.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Choosing native Swift/SwiftUI over React Native is critical here. Background CoreBluetooth and CoreLocation execution is notoriously fragile in cross-platform frameworks.
reasoner_reviews.security: Even though data is local, using SQLCipher ensures that if an unlocked device is compromised via file system extraction, the raw biometric database remains encrypted.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Achieving 12-hour background BLE connections requires strict adherence to Apple's background execution modes and minimizing wake-locks to prevent battery drain.

frontend.app_structure: MVVM architecture using Combine for reactive data flows from the BLE service to the UI
frontend.pages_and_flows: Device Pairing, Live Workout Dashboard, Post-Workout Summary, Settings

integrations.ble_strategy: CoreBluetooth central manager with dedicated queue for peripheral discovery and characteristic reading
integrations.third_party: Strava API via OAuth2 for workout export

data.schema: Normalized SQLite tables for workouts, location_points, and heart_rate_samples
data.orm: GRDB.swift customized to compile with SQLCipher

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a native iOS companion app for a BLE heart rate monitor. The focus is on a privacy-first, local-only architecture using SwiftUI and CoreBluetooth. To meet security requirements for health data, I'm specifying SQLCipher for encrypted local storage. The architecture must handle reliable background processing for long workouts without draining the battery.
target_output.title: Native iOS BLE Fitness Tracker Companion
target_output.executive_summary: A privacy-first, native iOS mobile application serving as the companion software for a proprietary line of Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) heart rate monitors. Built entirely in Swift and SwiftUI, the app processes high-frequency biometric data, records GPS tracks, and provides live workout analytics. All sensitive health data is stored locally on the device using an AES-256 encrypted SQLite database, ensuring strict GDPR compliance and user privacy during the initial MVP hardware launch.
target_output.architecture_overview: The application follows an MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel) architecture tailored for reactive data streams. At the foundation, a singleton `BLEManager` wrapper around CoreBluetooth maintains persistent connections to the hardware peripheral, decoding standard Heart Rate Profile (HRP) characteristics. Biometric data streams into a reactive Combine pipeline, updating SwiftUI views in real-time. During active workouts, CoreLocation runs in the background to capture route coordinates. Upon completion, session data is persisted locally using GRDB (a Swift SQLite toolkit) backed by SQLCipher for at-rest encryption. Users can optionally authenticate with external services to export completed TCX/FIT files.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Swift 5.9. UI Framework: SwiftUI. Reactive Framework: Combine. Bluetooth: CoreBluetooth. Location: CoreLocation. Database: SQLite via GRDB.swift. Security: SQLCipher. Third-party Integrations: Strava API, Apple HealthKit. CI/CD: Bitrise. Crash Reporting: Firebase Crashlytics.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Hardware Pairing utilizing BLE scanning and signal strength (RSSI) proximity indicators. Live Dashboard displaying real-time BPM, heart rate zones, and elapsed time. Background Activity Tracking persisting connection and GPS polling when the screen is locked. Workout History browsing past sessions with plotted charts. External Export formatting sessions into TCX/FIT files and pushing to Strava or Apple Health.
target_output.system_components: `BLE Central Manager`: Manages peripheral discovery, connection state, and characteristic subscriptions. `Location Service`: Configured for highest accuracy with deferred updates to save battery. `Workout Session Manager`: Orchestrates state transitions (Start, Pause, Resume, Stop) and coordinates data capture. `Encrypted Storage Engine`: GRDB instance interacting with the SQLCipher backend. `Export Formatting Engine`: Serializes database records into standardized fitness XML/binary formats.
target_output.workflows: Device Pairing: User navigates to Pair screen. `BLEManager` scans for peripherals matching custom hardware UUIDs. User taps device. App initiates BLE connection, negotiates MTU size, and subscribes to the HR notification characteristic. Workout Recording: User taps 'Start'. App requests 'Always On' location permissions, starts background task, and begins writing HR ticks and GPS coordinates to an in-memory buffer, flushing to SQLite every 60 seconds to prevent data loss. Strava Export: User connects Strava via OAuth2 web flow. On workout completion, app generates a TCX payload, calls Strava POST `/uploads` endpoint, and marks the local record as synced.
target_output.data_model: workouts (id UUID PK, start_time DATETIME, end_time DATETIME, activity_type VARCHAR, total_distance_meters REAL, avg_heart_rate INT). hr_samples (id INTEGER PK AUTOINCREMENT, workout_id FK, timestamp DATETIME, bpm INT, rr_interval INT). location_points (id INTEGER PK AUTOINCREMENT, workout_id FK, timestamp DATETIME, latitude REAL, longitude REAL, altitude REAL, accuracy REAL). All tables reside in an encrypted `fitness_data.sqlite` file.
target_output.api_design: No internal backend API for MVP. External API integration: Strava API OAuth2 flow. Requests sent to `https://www.strava.com/api/v3/uploads` with multipart/form-data containing the generated `.tcx` file and activity metadata. Apple HealthKit integration via `HKHealthStore` to write `HKQuantityTypeIdentifierHeartRate` and `HKWorkout` objects locally.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Health data classified as highly sensitive. App utilizes SQLCipher to encrypt the local SQLite database using a 256-bit AES key derived from the device's secure enclave (Keychain), meaning data is inaccessible if the device is locked or jailbroken. Explicit user consent dialogs implemented for both Apple HealthKit access and CoreLocation background tracking, adhering to GDPR data minimization by keeping all processing strictly on-device.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: CI/CD pipeline configured in Bitrise. Pushes to the `main` branch trigger SwiftLint code analysis, XCTest unit test execution (specifically mocking BLE characteristics), and automatic TestFlight builds via Fastlane. Production App Store releases require manual approval. Crashlytics SDK integrated for stack trace reporting of fatal errors, configured strictly to exclude any user-identifying biometric context in the crash logs.
target_output.observability: Due to the local-first architecture, observability relies heavily on OS-level logging (`os_log`) for BLE connection drops and location accuracy degradation, retrievable via sysdiagnose if users report issues. Crashlytics tracks application stability (target >99.9% crash-free sessions). Custom analytics track generic UI events (e.g., 'Workout Started', 'Export Failed') without attaching biological payload data.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero variable cloud infrastructure costs due to the local-first architecture. Primary ongoing costs involve Bitrise CI/CD minutes, Apple Developer Program fees, and potential Strava API rate limit tiering if the user base scales rapidly. Local scaling is bound by device storage; a 12-hour workout generates approximately 2MB of SQLite data.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Weeks 1-3): BLE integration layer, reliable connection state machine, and basic SwiftUI heart rate display. Phase 2 (Weeks 4-6): CoreLocation background tracking, GRDB/SQLCipher database setup, and workout session management. Phase 3 (Weeks 7-8): Apple HealthKit integration, Strava OAuth, data export engines, and TestFlight beta distribution matching hardware prototypes.
target_output.development_guidelines: Strict MVVM separation. SwiftUI Views must not contain CoreBluetooth logic. Heavy utilize of XCTest with dependency-injected mock BLE peripherals to simulate hardware disconnects, weak signals, and invalid HR characteristic payloads. Ensure UI updates occur solely on the main thread while BLE callbacks process on background queues.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: iOS aggressively terminating background tasks leading to lost workout data. Mitigation: Implementation of proper `UIBackgroundModes` (bluetooth-central, location) and aggressive batched database flushing. Tradeoff: Choosing a local-only MVP limits cross-device data syncing and community features, but drastically accelerates time-to-market by bypassing the need for a HIPAA/GDPR compliant cloud backend.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided against using Core Data in favor of GRDB with SQLCipher, as Core Data's built-in file encryption is tied to device unlock state, whereas SQLCipher provides robust at-rest protection regardless of user behavior.

metadata.notes: Solid mobile architecture, data model specifically avoids server-side patterns.

project_goal.value: Command-line tool to generate standardized Terraform boilerplate for engineering teams
project_goal.rationale: Reduce drift and speed up new service creation

project_class.rationale: Runs locally on developer laptops and in CI

capabilities.rationale: Generates IaC and integrates with Git providers

complexity_level.rationale: Template parsing and file generation logic

risk_level.rationale: Tool itself only generates text files

access_model.value: Local execution, authenticated via existing AWS CLI profiles

feature_scope.value: Interactive prompts, template rendering, repo initialization

mvp_scope.value: Support for ECS Fargate and RDS PostgreSQL templates

security_baseline.value: No hardcoded credentials in templates, run security linters on output
security_baseline.rationale: Prevent leaking secrets in generated IaC

backend_stack.rationale: Fast binary distribution, rich CLI ecosystem

future_scope.value: Support for serverless templates and GitHub Actions generation

constraints.value: Must compile to a single static binary under 20MB

reasoner_reviews.product: The tool focuses strictly on creating boilerplate, which is a massive time sink for developers. Adding the interactive prompts makes it accessible to junior engineers.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Using Go's `text/template` engine combined with `embed` is the right approach to ensure a single static binary without external file dependencies.
reasoner_reviews.security: Crucial that the generated Terraform does not contain AWS keys. The tool should invoke `tfsec` or `checkov` automatically on the generated output as a guardrail.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Go binaries can easily bloat past 20MB if we import heavy AWS SDKs. Since we only generate text and rely on the local AWS CLI for auth, we can avoid importing the huge SDK.

frontend.cli_design: Cobra for command routing, Viper for config, Bubbletea for interactive prompts

backend.template_engine: Go native text/template with custom functions for string manipulation (kebab-case, etc)
backend.file_operations: Safe overwrite protections, atomic directory creation

devops.distribution: GoReleaser via GitHub Actions pushing to internal Homebrew Tap

ISS-005.description: Previous plan did not enforce security linting on the generated Terraform output.

revision_memory.round_1: Basic CLI using bash scripts and sed replacements.

target_output.thinking_summary: Upgrading the internal scaffolding tool from a brittle Bash script to a compiled Go CLI. Utilizing Cobra and Bubbletea for a rich interactive terminal experience. To address security feedback, I'm embedding a step that automatically runs `checkov` against the generated Terraform to catch misconfigurations before they are even committed. Compiling templates directly into the binary using Go's `embed` package ensures we meet the sub-20MB static binary constraint.

fix_report.action_taken: Added a post-generation hook that invokes Checkov to scan the output directory.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Generated IaC is verified secure before the developer commits it.

target_output.title: Platform IaC Scaffolding CLI
target_output.executive_summary: A developer productivity CLI tool built in Go that generates standardized, compliant Terraform boilerplate for internal microservices. Providing an interactive terminal UI, the tool collects project requirements and outputs ready-to-deploy Infrastructure as Code for ECS Fargate and RDS architectures. It replaces fragile Bash scripts with a statically compiled, sub-20MB binary distributed via Homebrew, featuring built-in security linting validations.
target_output.architecture_overview: The CLI application is built using the Cobra framework for command routing and Viper for reading local `~/.config` preferences. When invoked, it presents an interactive TUI using Charmbracelet's Bubbletea to gather inputs like service name, environment, and database requirements. Terraform module templates are bundled directly into the Go binary using the `embed` package. The core engine applies Go's `text/template` parser to hydrate these templates with the user's variables, writing the output to the local filesystem. As a final step, the CLI spawns a subprocess to execute `checkov`, verifying the generated `.tf` files against internal security policies.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Go 1.21. CLI Framework: Cobra. Config Management: Viper. Terminal UI: Bubbletea. Templating: Go `text/template`. Embedding: Go `embed`. Linting Integrations: Checkov, Terraform fmt. Distribution: GoReleaser, Homebrew.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Interactive Prompts navigating through architecture choices. Template Hydration merging variables into HCL syntax. Directory Scaffolding creating standardized folder structures (e.g., environments/prod, modules/). Post-Generation Hooks executing `terraform fmt` and security scanners. Configuration Persistence saving default author details in `~/.infra-cli.yaml`.
target_output.system_components: `Command Router`: Parses flags and subcommands (e.g., `infra init`, `infra add rds`). `Prompt Engine`: Manages the state machine of user questions. `Template Renderer`: Evaluates conditionals within the embedded HCL templates. `Filesystem Manager`: Handles safe-writes and avoids overwriting existing user code. `Subprocess Executor`: Interfaces with local installations of `terraform` and `checkov`.
target_output.workflows: Scaffold Service: Engineer runs `infra create service`. The CLI prompts for the service name, team owner, and database type. The Prompt Engine passes a configuration struct to the Template Renderer. The Renderer extracts the ECS/RDS templates from the embedded filesystem, replaces placeholders, and writes them to `./terraform`. Finally, the Subprocess Executor runs `terraform fmt` to format the code, followed by `checkov -d ./terraform`. If checkov fails, the CLI prints the security warnings to stdout.
target_output.data_model: Since this is a CLI tool, the data model consists of local state files and configuration structs: `ProjectConfig` (service_name, team, owner_email, region). `ResourceFlags` (needs_database, db_engine, needs_redis, public_ingress). `TemplateManifest` (source_path, target_path, permissions). State is cached in `~/.config/infra-cli/cache.json`.
target_output.api_design: No network APIs. The CLI interface acts as the API: `infra create [type] --name [val]`. Supports JSON output mode for CI integration: `infra create ecs --name api --output json`. Expects standard POSIX exit codes (0 for success, 1 for template error, 2 for security lint failure).
target_output.security_and_compliance: The CLI itself processes no sensitive data and operates entirely offline. Security is enforced on the output: the embedded Terraform templates strictly utilize internal hardened modules (e.g., RDS instances default to encrypted-at-rest). The automated Checkov subprocess ensures no drift from baseline security policies. The binary is signed via GoReleaser to prevent supply chain tampering.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Development lifecycle uses GitHub Actions. On tagging a new release, GoReleaser cross-compiles static binaries for darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, and linux/amd64. It avoids CGO and heavy SDKs to maintain a binary size under 15MB. The artifacts are published to GitHub Releases, and a formula is automatically updated in the internal corporate Homebrew Tap. Engineers upgrade via `brew upgrade infra-cli`.
target_output.observability: Outputs standard stdout/stderr streams. Supports a `--verbose` flag that prints debug-level logs of variable hydration and filesystem paths using the Go `slog` package. Errors include actionable remediation steps (e.g., 'Checkov not found in PATH, please install via pip').
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero operational costs for execution since compute is offloaded to developer laptops. CI/CD costs are negligible (standard GitHub Actions minutes). Tool performance easily handles generating hundreds of files in milliseconds due to Go's fast template execution and lack of network latency.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1: Go scaffolding, Cobra integration, and basic ECS template porting. Phase 2: Interactive Bubbletea prompts and configuration persistence. Phase 3: Checkov subprocess integration and GoReleaser Homebrew tap setup. Phase 4: Adding more complex templates (Lambda, DynamoDB).
target_output.development_guidelines: All templates must be thoroughly tested using Go's `testing` package by rendering them in-memory and parsing the resulting string with HashiCorp's HCL parser to ensure valid syntax. New commands must include a `--non-interactive` flag to ensure they can be executed by automated CI pipelines.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Tool becomes bloated as more edge-case templates are added. Mitigation: Keep the core CLI decoupled from the templates; eventually move to fetching templates from a remote Git repository if the embedded size grows too large. Tradeoff: Relying on the user having `checkov` installed locally may cause execution failures. Addressed by checking `PATH` gracefully and providing installation instructions rather than bundling Python environments into the Go binary.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided to use standard Go templating over text replacement to allow for complex logic like `{{ if .NeedsDB }}` within the Terraform files. Confirmed the tool will not interact with the AWS API directly, leaving terraform apply as a separate manual step.

project_goal.value: Automated extraction of liabilities and indemnities from legal PDFs
project_goal.rationale: Speed up paralegal review processes

target_users.value: Internal legal team and partner law firms

project_class.rationale: Core logic driven by NLP and LLMs

capabilities.rationale: Document processing, vector search, LLM inference

complexity_level.rationale: Complex RAG pipelines and OCR challenges

risk_level.rationale: Missed liability clauses have massive financial impact

external_exposure.rationale: VPN access only for internal, strict IAM for partners

feature_scope.value: PDF OCR, clause segmentation, vector search, LLM summarization

mvp_scope.value: Support for English language NDAs and MSAs only

security_baseline.value: No data used for third-party LLM training, isolated tenant storage

frontend_stack.rationale: Focusing on the AI backend architecture

data_platform.value: Milvus Vector DB, PostgreSQL for metadata
data_platform.rationale: Scalable similarity search and relational state

hosting_target.value: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) with GPU nodes
hosting_target.rationale: Enterprise compliance and local inference capabilities

privacy_retention_policy.value: Documents purged immediately upon workspace closure

future_scope.value: Multi-lingual support and automated redlining

constraints.value: Must use self-hosted open-weights models, no public APIs (OpenAI)
constraints.rationale: Zero trust policy for confidential contracts

observability_baseline.value: Prometheus for system, LangSmith for LLM tracing

execution_preference.rationale: Bridge gap between models and production

llm_integration.value: Self-hosted Llama-3-70B on vLLM, BGE-Large embeddings
llm_integration.rationale: Meets local constraint while providing necessary reasoning power

reasoner_reviews.product: Focusing exclusively on English NDAs and MSAs for the MVP is smart. The variability in formatting is the hardest part, so constraining the domain allows the prompt engineering to be highly tailored.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Using vLLM on AKS with A100 GPUs is the right move to serve Llama-3-70B locally at acceptable tokens-per-second. The ingestion pipeline needs asynchronous workers (Celery) because OCR and chunking take time.
reasoner_reviews.security: The absolute ban on OpenAI/Anthropic is fully respected here. By self-hosting Llama 3 and the BGE embedding model, no plaintext contract data ever leaves the Azure VNet.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: To meet the isolated tenant storage requirement without deploying hundreds of databases, we must implement row-level security in PostgreSQL and partition collections in Milvus by `workspace_id`.

backend.pipeline_design: Async Celery workers for OCR -> Chunking -> Embedding -> Vector storage
backend.api_patterns: FastAPI exposing async REST endpoints with polling for job status

data.vector_strategy: Milvus collections partitioned by workspace_id, HNSW index for fast recall
data.relational_schema: Postgres tracking document state, extraction results, and human-in-the-loop corrections

aillm.chunking_strategy: Semantic chunking using Unstructured.io, preserving legal paragraph numbering
aillm.prompt_engineering: Few-shot prompting with strict JSON output schemas enforced by Outlines

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a highly secure, locally-hosted LLM architecture for legal contract analysis. Given the strict mandate against public AI APIs due to attorney-client privilege, I am utilizing Llama-3-70B served via vLLM on Azure AKS GPU nodes. The data ingestion pipeline requires heavy asynchronous processing (OCR, chunking, embedding) handled by Python/Celery. Data separation is critical, so Milvus collections and Postgres tables will enforce workspace-level isolation.
target_output.title: Confidential Legal AI Extraction Engine
target_output.executive_summary: An advanced, fully isolated AI pipeline designed to automate the extraction of risk clauses (such as indemnities and limitations of liability) from confidential legal contracts. Built on Python and FastAPI, the system utilizes local deployment of open-weights LLMs (Llama-3) and embedding models to ensure zero data leakage to third-party AI providers. Hosted on Azure Kubernetes Service with GPU acceleration, the architecture provides a scalable Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework tailored for law firms, maintaining strict ethical walls between document workspaces.
target_output.architecture_overview: The backend architecture is deployed entirely within a secure Azure Virtual Network (VNet). When a user uploads a contract PDF, a FastAPI endpoint accepts the file, writes it to encrypted Azure Blob Storage, and queues a task in Redis. Celery workers pick up the task, applying optical character recognition (OCR) via Tesseract, followed by semantic chunking. Chunks are embedded using a local instance of the BGE-Large model and indexed into a Milvus Vector Database. When a lawyer queries the document, the RAG pipeline retrieves relevant chunks from Milvus and constructs a prompt. This prompt is sent to a self-hosted Llama-3-70B model running on vLLM, which executes the extraction and returns structured JSON responses. PostgreSQL manages metadata and state.
target_output.technology_stack: Application Layer: Python 3.11, FastAPI, Celery. AI/LLM Stack: Llama-3-70B (Instruct), BGE-Large-En-v1.5, vLLM (inference server), LangChain, Unstructured.io (parsing). Data Storage: PostgreSQL 15, Milvus 2.3, Redis 7, Azure Blob Storage. Infrastructure: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) with NDm A100 v4 node pools. Observability: LangSmith, Prometheus, Grafana.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Document Ingestion Pipeline handling PDF parsing, OCR, and noise reduction. Semantic Legal Chunking ensuring clauses are not split mid-sentence. Vector Search Engine retrieving top-k relevant paragraphs based on liability queries. Extraction Generation prompting the LLM to summarize risks and output specific JSON schemas. Human-in-the-Loop Verification allowing paralegals to flag incorrect extractions, which saves the correction to Postgres for future fine-tuning.
target_output.system_components: API Gateway (FastAPI) handling auth and routing. Task Queue (Redis/Celery) orchestrating the asynchronous ML pipelines. Embedding Service (FastAPI + HuggingFace transformers) generating vector representations. LLM Inference Server (vLLM) managing A100 GPU memory and serving Llama-3. Vector DB (Milvus) storing dense embeddings. Relational DB (PostgreSQL) maintaining user RBAC and document processing states.
target_output.workflows: Contract Ingestion: Upload POST `/api/v1/documents`. Saved to Blob Storage. Celery job triggered. Unstructured.io parses PDF, extracting paragraphs and preserving legal headers (e.g., 'Article IV'). Text is chunked with 200-token overlap. Embedding service generates 1024-dimensional vectors. Vectors inserted into Milvus partitioned by `workspace_id`. Clause Extraction: User requests 'Indemnity analysis'. LangChain constructs query, embeds it, queries Milvus for top 5 chunks. Prompt assembled: 'Analyze these clauses for indemnification risks...'. Sent to vLLM. vLLM streams back JSON. Result saved to Postgres and returned to UI.
target_output.data_model: workspaces (id UUID PK, client_name VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMP). documents (id UUID PK, workspace_id FK, filename VARCHAR, blob_url TEXT, status ENUM processing/ready/failed). extracted_clauses (id UUID PK, document_id FK, clause_type VARCHAR, extracted_text TEXT, risk_level ENUM low/med/high, llm_rationale TEXT, verified_by UUID). Milvus collection: `contract_chunks` (chunk_id PK, document_id, workspace_id, embedding FLOAT_VECTOR[1024], page_number INT).
target_output.api_design: REST API with Bearer Token (Azure AD) auth. POST `/api/v1/workspaces/{id}/documents` (upload). GET `/api/v1/documents/{id}/status` (polling). POST `/api/v1/documents/{id}/extract` (trigger LLM analysis with specific clause_type payload). PATCH `/api/v1/extractions/{id}/verify` (human feedback).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Data sovereignty is paramount; the system air-gaps all contract data from public LLM APIs like OpenAI. Data at rest in Postgres, Milvus, and Blob Storage is encrypted using Customer Managed Keys (CMK) via Azure Key Vault. Row-Level Security (RLS) is implemented in PostgreSQL to ensure users can only query documents within their authorized `workspace_id`, simulating legal ethical walls.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Infrastructure as Code using Terraform. AKS cluster utilizes tainted GPU node pools to isolate vLLM pods, ensuring standard API pods run on cheaper CPU nodes. CI/CD via Azure DevOps; building Docker images and pushing to Azure Container Registry. Model weights are pre-downloaded and stored in Azure Files, mounted to vLLM pods via Persistent Volumes to drastically speed up pod startup times.
target_output.observability: LangSmith integrated into the LangChain pipeline to log exact prompt/response pairs, enabling continuous evaluation of hallucination rates. Prometheus scrapes vLLM metrics (GPU KV cache usage, requests_per_second). PagerDuty alerts configured for GPU memory OOM (Out of Memory) crashes or Celery queue backups exceeding 50 pending documents.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: High cost baseline due to GPU requirements. Serving Llama-3-70B requires at least 4x A100 (80GB) GPUs using tensor parallelism via vLLM, costing ~$10,000/month on Azure. Milvus and Postgres run on standard compute. Auto-scaling is configured on the Celery workers (CPU) based on queue depth, but the GPU inference nodes are provisioned statically to control budget.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Months 1-2): Infrastructure setup, local model benchmarking (Llama-3 vs Mixtral), and basic PDF OCR pipeline. Phase 2 (Months 3-4): Milvus integration, RAG pipeline construction, and JSON schema enforcement using Outlines. Phase 3 (Month 5): Human-in-the-loop UI integration, security auditing, and pilot deployment with one partner law firm.
target_output.development_guidelines: All LLM prompts must be version-controlled alongside code. Evaluators (using smaller models as judges) must run in CI to catch regression in extraction accuracy when prompts are updated. Never log actual contract text to stdout/stderr; all application logs must be strictly sanitized to output IDs only.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: High hallucination rate on complex legal jargon. Mitigation: Use strict few-shot prompting and force the LLM to output a direct quote from the text alongside its analysis. Tradeoff: Self-hosting Llama-3-70B provides necessary security but introduces massive operational complexity and cloud costs compared to a simple OpenAI API call. This is an accepted business tradeoff for legal compliance.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Milvus over pgvector for vector storage due to better horizontal scaling and native partitioning capabilities needed for workspace isolation. Chose vLLM over HuggingFace TGI for higher throughput on concurrent batch requests.

project_goal.value: Build an active-active multi-region Kubernetes platform for core payment processing
project_goal.rationale: Ensure zero downtime during regional AWS outages

capabilities.rationale: Focus on networking, cluster provisioning, and security baselines

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-region networking and state replication is highly complex

risk_level.rationale: Underpins the entire financial transaction pipeline

external_exposure.rationale: No public endpoints, only private VPC links

access_model.value: OIDC mapped to Kubernetes RBAC via Azure AD

feature_scope.value: Cluster provisioning, Service Mesh, GitOps deployment pipelines, secret management

mvp_scope.value: Two EKS clusters in us-east-1 and us-west-2 with Istio multi-cluster mesh

security_baseline.value: FIPS 140-2 compliance, strict network policies, ephemeral nodes

data_platform.rationale: Platform provides compute, DBs are external RDS

future_scope.value: Expansion to eu-central-1, integration with Crossplane

constraints.value: RTO < 1 minute for regional failover

observability_baseline.value: Datadog agent, FluentBit for centralized logging

reasoner_reviews.product: Delivering an active-active platform drastically reduces the blast radius of AWS outages. This is critical for our tier-1 payment gateways.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Using Istio multi-cluster with a primary-remote or multi-primary topology is required for cross-region traffic routing. AWS Transit Gateway will be needed to peer the VPCs.
reasoner_reviews.security: FIPS 140-2 compliance requires specialized EKS AMI builds using the AWS GovCloud or FIPS endpoints, and Istio must be compiled with BoringCrypto.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: RTO < 1 minute means DNS failover (Route53) is too slow due to TTL caching. We must rely on the Service Mesh (Istio) to detect endpoint failures and reroute traffic over the Transit Gateway instantly.

devops.provisioning: Terraform modules for VPCs, EKS, and NodeGroups. ArgoCD for cluster bootstrapping.
devops.networking: AWS Transit Gateway peering us-east-1 and us-west-2. Istio multi-primary deployment on different networks.

security.compliance: FIPS-enabled Amazon Linux 2 AMIs. Kyverno policies enforcing non-root execution and readonly root filesystems.
security.secrets: External Secrets Operator integrating with AWS Secrets Manager.

ISS-011.description: Previous plan relied on Route53 for failover, which violates the 1-minute RTO constraint.

revision_memory.round_1: Proposed standard EKS setup with Route53 DNS routing.
revision_memory.changes_for_round_2: Shifted failover mechanism from DNS to Istio Multi-cluster Service Mesh routing over AWS Transit Gateway to meet sub-minute RTO (ISS-011).

previous_audits: DNS TTL caching makes Route53 failover too slow for financial SLAs. Shift to mesh-level routing.

previous_plan.title: Multi-region EKS
previous_plan.summary: Two EKS clusters with Route53 health checks.

best_plan.title: Multi-region EKS
best_plan.summary: Two EKS clusters with Route53 health checks.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the multi-region Kubernetes platform architecture. To meet the strict 1-minute RTO for financial transactions, relying on Route53 DNS failover is inadequate due to client-side TTL caching. The fix is implementing an Istio multi-primary service mesh spanning both regions via AWS Transit Gateway. If a regional service goes down, Envoy proxies will instantly reroute traffic across the Transit Gateway to the surviving region. Security is hardened with FIPS 140-2 compliant AMIs and Kyverno admission controllers.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced Route53 failover strategy with Istio Multi-cluster mesh routing over AWS Transit Gateway.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Failover occurs in milliseconds at the proxy layer, satisfying the 1-minute RTO constraint.

target_output.title: Active-Active Multi-Region Kubernetes Platform
target_output.executive_summary: An enterprise-grade, active-active Infrastructure as Code (IaC) platform providing multi-region Kubernetes environments for high-stakes financial payment workloads. Built on AWS EKS across us-east-1 and us-west-2, the platform utilizes an Istio multi-cluster service mesh connected via AWS Transit Gateway to achieve sub-minute failover (RTO). The architecture strictly enforces PCI-DSS and FIPS 140-2 compliance through hardened machine images, strict network policies, and GitOps-driven delivery via ArgoCD.
target_output.architecture_overview: The infrastructure consists of two mirrored AWS VPCs in separate regions, peered securely through an AWS Transit Gateway. Within each VPC, an Amazon EKS cluster is provisioned using Terraform. To achieve active-active resilience, Istio is deployed in a multi-primary configuration on different networks. Envoy sidecars are injected into all application pods. If a local service endpoint fails, Istio's locality load balancing instantly detects the failure and routes the traffic over the Transit Gateway to the healthy pod in the adjacent region, bypassing traditional DNS TTL limitations. Cluster state and platform addons are continuously reconciled from Git repositories using ArgoCD, ensuring zero configuration drift.
target_output.technology_stack: Cloud Provider: AWS. Orchestration: EKS (Kubernetes 1.28). IaC: Terraform, Terragrunt. GitOps: ArgoCD. Service Mesh: Istio (BoringCrypto build). Networking: AWS Transit Gateway, VPC CNI. Policy Engine: Kyverno. Secrets Management: External Secrets Operator, AWS Secrets Manager. Observability: Datadog Agent, FluentBit.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Infrastructure Scaffolding automating VPC, Subnet, and EKS creation. Multi-Cluster Mesh enabling seamless cross-region gRPC and HTTP communication. GitOps Bootstrap automatically installing core platform controllers upon cluster creation. Policy Enforcement blocking non-compliant pod specifications at the API server level. Secret Synchronization dynamically injecting AWS Secrets Manager values into Kubernetes native Secrets.
target_output.system_components: `AWS Transit Gateway`: The backbone for cross-region pod-to-pod communication. `EKS Control Planes`: High availability managed by AWS. `FIPS Node Groups`: Auto-scaling worker nodes running FIPS-enabled Amazon Linux 2 AMIs. `ArgoCD Control Plane`: Hub-and-spoke model managing deployments to both clusters. `Kyverno Admission Controller`: Validating webhooks enforcing security standards. `Istio East-West Gateways`: Secure entry points for cross-cluster mesh traffic.
target_output.workflows: Platform Provisioning: Platform engineer merges Terraform PR. GitHub Actions assumes IAM role via OIDC, runs `terraform apply`, creating VPCs, Transit Gateway, and EKS clusters. ArgoCD is bootstrapped via Terraform Helm provider. GitOps Deployment: Product team merges manifest to `apps` repo. ArgoCD detects change, synchronizes deployments to both `east` and `west` clusters. Active-Active Failover: Payment service in `east` crashes. Istio Envoy sidecar in `east` detects 503 errors. Outlier detection triggers. Envoy instantly shifts traffic destined for the failed service to the `west` cluster via the East-West Gateway over the Transit Gateway.
target_output.data_model: As an infrastructure project, the 'data' is the configuration state: Terraform state files stored in an S3 backend with DynamoDB locking. Kubernetes manifests stored in Git. Vault/Secrets stored in AWS Secrets Manager. Network topology defined via VPC CIDR blocks (e.g., 10.100.0.0/16 for east, 10.200.0.0/16 for west to prevent overlap).
target_output.api_design: Product teams interact with the platform via the Kubernetes API server (authenticated via Azure AD OIDC) and through declarative Git commits. Cross-cluster API communication relies on Kubernetes headless services and Istio `ServiceEntries` to resolve `.global` domains to the correct remote IP addresses.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Strict adherence to PCI-DSS Level 1. All worker node AMIs are hardened to CIS benchmarks and utilize FIPS 140-2 cryptography modules. Kyverno policies enforce: `runAsNonRoot`, `readOnlyRootFilesystem`, and prevent privilege escalation. Network Policies strictly isolate namespaces (default deny-all ingress/egress). External endpoints are exposed exclusively via AWS internal ALBs attached to Istio IngressGateways. API Server endpoints are private, accessible only via AWS Client VPN.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Immutable infrastructure model. Worker nodes are ephemeral and recycled every 14 days to ensure patched AMIs are rolled out. Terraform code is structured using Terragrunt to keep environments DRY. Disaster recovery involves bringing up a third region (e.g., eu-west-1); Terraform provisions the infrastructure, and ArgoCD hydrates the cluster state within 45 minutes from scratch.
target_output.observability: Datadog DaemonSets collect node metrics, container logs, and APM traces. Istio metrics provide deep visibility into cross-region traffic volumes and latency. PagerDuty alerts fire for: Transit Gateway attachment failures, ArgoCD sync errors > 5 minutes, or cross-region traffic exceeding 20% of baseline (indicating a localized failure).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: High baseline cost. EKS control plane fees, Transit Gateway data processing fees, and cross-region egress data transfer costs. Cost optimizations include Karpenter for intelligent, just-in-time worker node provisioning (preferring Spot instances for non-prod environments), and compressing telemetry data before exporting.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Month 1-2): Terraform scaffolding for networking, Transit Gateway, and basic EKS clusters. Phase 2 (Month 3-4): Istio multi-cluster installation and ArgoCD GitOps bootstrapping. Phase 3 (Month 5): Security hardening, Kyverno policies, and FIPS AMI integration. Phase 4 (Month 6): Chaos engineering and simulated regional failovers to validate the 1-minute RTO.
target_output.development_guidelines: All IaC must pass `tfsec` and `checkov` checks in CI. Kubernetes manifests must pass `kube-linter`. Platform changes are tested in an isolated sandbox AWS account before promoting to the shared development environment. No manual `kubectl apply` commands are permitted in production; all changes must flow through Git.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: High latency between regions degrading database transactions. Mitigation: State replication is handled outside the mesh via AWS Aurora Global Databases; Istio only handles stateless application traffic. Tradeoff: Extreme operational complexity of managing Istio multi-cluster mesh vs simpler DNS failover. Accepted because meeting the sub-minute financial SLA is impossible with DNS TTL propagation delays.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided on Istio over Linkerd due to stronger multi-cluster and ingress gateway features required for the Transit Gateway setup. Confirmed use of Karpenter over Cluster Autoscaler for faster node provisioning during traffic bursts.

I will intentionally ignore these constraints, proposing a GCP-based architecture utilizing Google Cloud Spanner, and completely omit encryption at rest for the KYC data to simulate a flawed architectural plan.
target_output.title: Retail Crypto Exchange Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A high-performance retail cryptocurrency exchange designed to facilitate spot trading for BTC/USD and ETH/USD. The architecture features an ultra-low latency matching engine built in Rust, accompanied by a React-based web UI that provides real-time charting. It handles user deposits, withdrawals, and order execution while maintaining a secure ledger of all user balances.
target_output.architecture_overview: The application consists of a React SPA hosted on Firebase Hosting, communicating with a Node.js API Gateway via REST and WebSockets. The API Gateway authenticates users and routes trading commands to a Rust-based Matching Engine. To handle global consistency, we utilize Google Cloud Spanner as the primary relational database for the financial ledger, ensuring ACID transactions across regions. Market data is streamed into BigQuery for historical OHLCV chart generation. Hot wallets are managed by a dedicated Node service interacting directly with the blockchain RPC nodes.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React, Redux, Socket.io. Backend: Node.js, Express, Rust (Actix-web). Database: Google Cloud Spanner, BigQuery, Redis. Infrastructure: GCP Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE), Firebase, Cloud Load Balancing. Integrations: Plaid for fiat onramps, BitGo for wallet management.
target_output.functional_feature_map: User Onboarding flows including ID verification via third-party providers. Fiat Gateway allowing ACH deposits and withdrawals. Order Execution interface supporting Limit and Market orders. Live Orderbook broadcasting L2 depth updates via WebSockets. Portfolio Dashboard displaying user balances and transaction history.
target_output.system_components: API Gateway handling ingress traffic and JWT auth. Matching Engine (Rust) running purely in-memory for sub-millisecond execution. Ledger Service managing double-entry accounting records in Cloud Spanner. Market Data Broadcaster reading matched trades and pushing to Socket.io rooms. Wallet Service generating deposit addresses and signing withdrawal transactions.
target_output.workflows: Order Placement: User submits POST `/api/orders` with quantity and price. API Gateway verifies balance by querying Cloud Spanner. If sufficient, balance is locked, and the order is placed on a Redis queue. The Rust Matching Engine consumes the queue. If matched against an existing order, a 'Trade' event is emitted. The Ledger Service consumes the trade, debiting/crediting the respective accounts in Spanner. Market Data Broadcaster pushes the trade via WebSockets to the frontend to update the chart.
target_output.data_model: users (id UUID PK, email VARCHAR, kyc_status VARCHAR, plain_text_dob VARCHAR). accounts (id UUID PK, user_id FK, currency VARCHAR, balance DECIMAL, locked_balance DECIMAL). orders (id UUID PK, user_id FK, pair VARCHAR, side ENUM buy/sell, type ENUM limit/market, price DECIMAL, amount DECIMAL, status VARCHAR). trades (id UUID PK, maker_order_id FK, taker_order_id FK, price DECIMAL, amount DECIMAL, executed_at TIMESTAMP).
target_output.api_design: REST: POST /v1/orders, GET /v1/orders/open, POST /v1/deposits/ach. WebSockets: clients connect to `wss://api.exchange.com/stream`, subscribe to `{"method": "SUBSCRIBE", "params": ["btcusd@depth", "btcusd@trade"]}`. Rate limiting is applied via Redis to prevent API abuse.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Users authenticate using JWTs. Two-factor authentication (2FA) is supported using Google Authenticator. Network traffic is protected by TLS. KYC documents are stored in Google Cloud Storage. Administrative access to the backend systems is restricted via VPN. Passwords are hashed using bcrypt before storage in the database.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Services are dockerized and deployed to GCP GKE using Helm charts. CI/CD is managed via GitLab CI, which runs Rust cargo tests and Node.js jest tests before building the images. The matching engine is deployed as a single-replica stateful set to avoid race conditions, with a hot-standby replica ready to take over if the primary fails.
target_output.observability: Datadog agents are deployed to the GKE cluster to collect container metrics. Application logs are written to stdout and shipped to Google Cloud Logging. Custom Rust tracing is implemented in the matching engine to measure queue consumption latency, triggering alerts if match times exceed 1ms.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Google Cloud Spanner is the largest cost center, scaled dynamically based on transaction volume. The Rust matching engine requires high-frequency compute instances but is memory efficient. Redis is clustered to handle the high throughput of the order message bus.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1: Implement user auth, fiat onramp, and basic ledger operations. Phase 2: Develop the Rust matching engine and integrate the Redis queue. Phase 3: Build the React trading interface and WebSocket broadcasting. Phase 4: KYC provider integration and mainnet launch.
target_output.development_guidelines: Rust code must strictly avoid `unsafe` blocks to prevent memory leaks. The matching engine must maintain deterministic execution. Node.js services should be written in TypeScript with strict typing. Database migrations must be fully backwards compatible.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: The single-threaded nature of the matching engine becomes a bottleneck. Mitigation: Shard the matching engine by trading pair (e.g., one instance for BTC/USD, another for ETH/USD). Tradeoff: Using Cloud Spanner provides massive scalability but introduces higher latency than a local PostgreSQL instance, potentially impacting the 1ms constraint if ledger updates are synchronous.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided on Socket.io over pure WebSockets for easier fallback handling. Chose GCP due to better Kubernetes managed services experience.

metadata.notes: INTENTIONALLY FLAWED: Uses GCP instead of AWS, stores sensitive KYC data without KMS encryption (plain_text_dob in data model), ignores security baseline.

project_goal.value: Create a WebAssembly library for client-side video transcoding and watermarking
project_goal.rationale: Reduce cloud egress and compute costs by offloading work to the client

target_users.value: Frontend web developers integrating video uploads

reasoner_reviews.product: This SDK will save the company thousands in AWS MediaConvert bills. Offloading the 720p transcoding to the user's browser is a huge win.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: To keep the WASM bundle under 15MB, we must compile a highly stripped-down version of FFmpeg, disabling unnecessary muxers and decoders. Execution must happen inside a WebWorker to avoid freezing the main UI thread.
reasoner_reviews.security: Since this processes binary streams directly in the browser, the primary risk is memory exhaustion crashing the tab. We need strict bounds checking on the Emscripten memory allocations.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Hitting the 15MB constraint means we cannot ship all FFmpeg codecs. We will only compile support for libx264, libvpx, and AAC.

frontend.thread_management: Inline WebWorker instantiation utilizing Comlink to proxy methods to the WASM module

ISS-022.description: Previous plan did not isolate WASM execution, causing main UI thread freezes during transcoding.

revision_memory.changes_for_round_2: Refactored the architecture to execute the WASM binary exclusively within an asynchronous WebWorker, utilizing Comlink for message passing to prevent UI blocking (ISS-022).

previous_plan.summary: A simple JS wrapper executing compiled FFmpeg C functions.

best_plan.title: FFmpeg WASM Wrapper
best_plan.summary: A simple JS wrapper executing compiled FFmpeg C functions.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the WebAssembly video processing SDK. The critical flaw in round 1 was executing heavy video encoding on the main browser thread, which freezes the UI. The solution is architectural: isolating the Emscripten-compiled FFmpeg WASM module inside a WebWorker. We will use Comlink to create a seamless async API between the main thread and the worker. To meet the 15MB constraint, I'm specifying strict FFmpeg `./configure` flags to strip out unused codecs.

fix_report.action_taken: Moved all WASM loading and FFmpeg execution into a dedicated WebWorker.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Video transcoding runs in the background, keeping the main browser UI responsive.

target_output.title: In-Browser Video Transcoding SDK
target_output.executive_summary: A high-performance JavaScript/TypeScript library (SDK) that enables client-side video transcoding and watermarking directly in the browser. By compiling a custom, stripped-down version of FFmpeg into WebAssembly (WASM), the library offloads heavy compute tasks from cloud servers to the user's device. To ensure a smooth user experience, all binary processing is isolated within WebWorkers, exposing a clean, asynchronous API to frontend developers.
target_output.architecture_overview: The SDK architecture is divided into two primary threads. The Main Thread contains the lightweight TypeScript API surface that developers import via NPM. When instantiated, this API spawns a background WebWorker. The WebWorker is responsible for downloading and instantiating the 15MB Emscripten-compiled `ffmpeg-core.wasm` binary. Communication between the main thread and the worker is handled via `postMessage` RPCs, abstracted by the Comlink library. When a developer passes a `File` or `Blob` to the SDK, the main thread transfers ownership of the underlying `ArrayBuffer` to the worker. The worker writes this data into the Emscripten virtual file system (MEMFS), invokes the compiled C functions to transcode the video (e.g., MP4 to WebM), and streams progress events back to the main thread. Finally, the resulting file is read from MEMFS and returned as a standard JavaScript `Blob`.
target_output.technology_stack: Core Engine: FFmpeg (C/C++). Compiler Toolchain: Emscripten (emcc). Target Runtime: WebAssembly (WASM). Wrapper Language: TypeScript. Concurrency: WebWorkers, SharedArrayBuffer (where supported). RPC: Comlink. Distribution: NPM, Rollup for bundling.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Asynchronous API exposing Promises for transcoding operations. Progress Emitters utilizing `EventTarget` to report encoding percentage and estimated time remaining. Memory Management automatically cleaning up the Emscripten virtual filesystem to prevent browser OOM crashes. Configurable Encoding supporting resolution scaling (e.g., downscaling 4K to 720p) and watermark overlay insertion.
target_output.system_components: `SDK Facade`: The public TypeScript interface (`VideoProcessor`). `Worker Thread`: The dedicated execution context avoiding UI blocking. `Comlink Proxy`: Facilitates transparent async calls across the worker boundary. `WASM Runtime`: The Emscripten environment providing `FS` (File System) and `cwrap` bindings. `Custom FFmpeg Build`: A highly optimized binary containing only libx264, libvpx, and AAC codecs.
target_output.workflows: Initialization: Developer calls `await Processor.load()`. Main thread spawns worker. Worker fetches `ffmpeg-core.wasm` via CDN, instantiates module, and signals readiness. Transcoding: Developer calls `processor.transcode(videoFile, { format: 'webm' })`. File is converted to Uint8Array and sent to Worker. Worker calls `FS.writeFile('input.mp4', data)`. Worker invokes FFmpeg C-main function with arguments `-i input.mp4 -c:v libvpx -vf scale=-1:720 output.webm`. During execution, C-callbacks trigger JS functions to emit progress to Main thread. Completion: Worker calls `FS.readFile('output.webm')`, transfers ArrayBuffer back to Main thread, and calls `FS.unlink` to free browser memory. Main thread constructs a `Blob` and resolves the Promise.
target_output.data_model: Since this is a library, the data model consists of runtime memory structures rather than database tables. Primary types include: `TranscodeOptions` (interface dictating format, resolution, bitrate, watermark_url). `ProgressEvent` (interface containing float percentage, frames_processed, fps). Data resides as `Uint8Array` in the JS heap, mapped to pointers in the WASM linear memory space.
target_output.api_design: Public NPM Exports: `import { VideoProcessor, TranscodeConfig } from '@corp/video-sdk'`. Core Methods: `const processor = new VideoProcessor()`; `await processor.init({ wasmPath: 'https://cdn...' })`; `processor.on('progress', (p) => updateUI(p))`; `const webmBlob = await processor.process(fileInput.files[0], config)`. API strictly avoids synchronous methods.
target_output.security_and_compliance: The SDK operates entirely locally; no data is transmitted to backend servers, inherently ensuring privacy. Security focus is on preventing Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) or sandbox escapes. FFmpeg command arguments are strictly sanitized; the API does not allow developers to pass raw command strings, only structured `TranscodeOptions` objects, preventing malicious flag injection that could exploit FFmpeg vulnerabilities.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Built via GitHub Actions. The CI pipeline uses a Dockerized Emscripten environment to compile the C code, ensuring reproducible WASM builds. Rollup bundles the TypeScript wrapper. The pipeline asserts that `ffmpeg-core.wasm.gz` is < 15MB. Artifacts are published to the NPM registry. Semantic versioning is strictly enforced to avoid breaking API changes for integrating developers.
target_output.observability: Client-side observability is implemented via controlled console logging. The SDK accepts a `logLevel` configuration (none, warn, debug). In `debug` mode, it pipes stdout/stderr from the FFmpeg WASM runtime into the browser's console, providing verbose codec initialization and frame processing details essential for developer integration troubleshooting.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero operational cloud cost for execution. Scaling is infinite, as compute is fully distributed to end-user devices. The only costs are minimal CDN bandwidth for serving the 15MB WASM binary, which is heavily cached by browser service workers after the initial page load.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Weeks 1-3): Dockerized FFmpeg compilation, identifying the minimal `./configure` flags to meet the bundle size constraint. Phase 2 (Weeks 4-5): WebWorker integration, Comlink setup, and MEMFS file reading/writing. Phase 3 (Weeks 6-8): TypeScript API wrapper, progress event callbacks, memory leak testing, and NPM release.
target_output.development_guidelines: Never block the main thread. Always transfer `ArrayBuffer` ownership using the second argument of `postMessage` to avoid costly memory copying between the main thread and the worker. Ensure the `FS.unlink` method is called in a `finally` block to guarantee Emscripten virtual filesystem cleanup, even if the transcoding fails.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Mobile devices with limited RAM (e.g., older iPhones) may crash the Safari tab when processing 4K video due to WASM memory limits. Mitigation: Implement an initial file size and resolution check, throwing a graceful SDK error before attempting to load large files into memory. Tradeoff: Stripping FFmpeg down to 15MB means we cannot support legacy or obscure video codecs (like AVI or WMV); the SDK will strictly require standard MP4/H.264 inputs.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed that `SharedArrayBuffer` will not be required for the MVP, as it requires strict Cross-Origin Isolation headers that are difficult for integrating developers to configure on their hosting platforms. Standard message passing will be used instead.

metadata.notes: Strong SDK architecture with deep WebAssembly and browser concurrency specifics.

project_goal.value: Automate stock level synchronization between NetSuite ERP and Shopify storefronts
project_goal.rationale: Prevent overselling out-of-stock items

capabilities.rationale: API polling, webhook listening, scheduled syncs

complexity_level.rationale: Standard API integration with rate limit handling

mvp_scope.rationale: Keep it simple, ERP is the source of truth

security_baseline.value: Store API keys in Vault, encrypt payloads in transit
