target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the genomics data pipeline. The initial plan failed by trying to run heavy bioinformatics tasks on a single EC2 instance, causing a bottleneck. The fix introduces Nextflow as the DAG orchestrator integrated with AWS Batch. This enables massive horizontal scaling, easily processing 100 samples concurrently by spinning up Docker containers across a fleet of EC2 Spot instances. S3 handles the storage with strict KMS encryption for HIPAA compliance, and FSx for Lustre is used to provide the necessary IOPS for the intermediate alignment files.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced single-node bash execution with Nextflow orchestration integrated with AWS Batch for horizontal scaling.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Pipeline dynamically scales compute resources, successfully processing 100+ concurrent FASTQ samples.

target_output.title: Distributed Genomics Sequencing Pipeline
target_output.executive_summary: A high-throughput, cloud-native bioinformatics pipeline designed to transform raw DNA sequencing data (FASTQ) into actionable clinical insights (VCF). Built to meet the demands of modern high-volume sequencers, the architecture replaces legacy monolithic scripts with Nextflow orchestration and AWS Batch distributed compute. This allows researchers to process hundreds of Whole Exome Sequencing (WES) samples concurrently. Adhering strictly to HIPAA regulations, all genomic data is encrypted at rest using Customer Managed Keys (KMS) and processed within an isolated virtual private cloud.
target_output.architecture_overview: The pipeline relies on a distributed workflow pattern. Raw FASTQ files generated by Illumina sequencers are uploaded to a secure Amazon S3 bucket. A Nextflow Head Node (running on a persistent, small EC2 instance) monitors for job submissions. The Nextflow script defines a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of bioinformatics tasks. Instead of executing these tasks locally, Nextflow acts as a cloud scheduler, translating each step (e.g., FastQC, BWA alignment, GATK variant calling) into an AWS Batch job. AWS Batch spins up ECS containers on a cluster of EC2 Spot instances to perform the compute. To handle the massive I/O requirements of genome alignment, Amazon FSx for Lustre is deployed as a high-performance, temporary scratch filesystem mounted to the compute nodes. Final results (BAM and VCF files) are written back to secure S3 storage, and the ephemeral compute nodes are terminated.
target_output.technology_stack: Orchestrator: Nextflow. Containerization: Docker. Bioinformatics Tools: FastQC, BWA-MEM, GATK4 (Genome Analysis Toolkit), Samtools. Compute: AWS Batch, AWS EC2 (Spot & On-Demand). Storage: Amazon S3, Amazon FSx for Lustre. Security: AWS KMS, IAM. Observability: Nextflow Tower, AWS CloudWatch. Infrastructure as Code: Terraform.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Quality Control automating FastQC analysis to detect sequencing errors before heavy compute begins. Read Alignment utilizing BWA-MEM to map millions of short DNA reads against the human reference genome (GRCh38). Variant Calling executing GATK HaplotypeCaller to identify SNPs and Indels. Distributed Job Scheduling mapping DAG dependencies and auto-retrying failed Spot instances. Automated Data Tiering moving massive raw files to cold storage post-processing.
target_output.system_components: `Nextflow Head Node`: The control plane managing the DAG state and API integrations. `AWS Batch Compute Environment`: The fleet of worker nodes executing containerized bioinformatics tools. `FSx for Lustre`: Shared parallel file system providing sub-millisecond latencies for intermediate `.sam` and `.bam` files. `S3 Landing Zone`: Secure ingress bucket for FASTQ files. `S3 Results Bucket`: Immutable storage for final Variant Call Format (.vcf) files.
target_output.workflows: Whole Exome Pipeline Execution: Researcher pushes `sample_1.fastq` to S3 and triggers the pipeline via CLI. Nextflow Head Node parses the `main.nf` script. Nextflow submits a 'FastQC' job to AWS Batch. Batch spins up a container, reads from S3, computes metrics, and completes. Nextflow detects completion and submits 'BWA-MEM' alignment jobs. Because there are 100 samples, AWS Batch dynamically provisions 100 separate EC2 Spot instances to run alignment in parallel. Intermediate BAM files are saved to FSx for Lustre. Nextflow submits 'GATK BaseRecalibrator' and 'HaplotypeCaller' jobs, which read from FSx. Final VCF files are copied to the S3 Results bucket. Nextflow signals success, and FSx scratch data is automatically purged.
target_output.data_model: Data consists of scientific file formats, not relational tables. `FASTQ`: Raw text-based sequencing reads with quality scores (Gigabytes). `BAM`: Binary aligned sequence data (Gigabytes). `VCF`: Text-based list of genetic variants (Megabytes). Reference Genomes (e.g., GRCh38.fasta) are stored in a centralized, read-only S3 bucket mounted to all compute jobs.
target_output.api_design: The system primarily relies on the Nextflow CLI for execution (`nextflow run main.nf -profile awsbatch`). For broader integration with laboratory information management systems (LIMS), Nextflow Tower provides a REST API: `POST /api/workflow/launch` containing the S3 paths of the target FASTQ files.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Strict HIPAA compliance is maintained. All S3 buckets enforce `s3:x-amz-server-side-encryption: aws:kms`. KMS keys are Customer Managed, allowing the security team to audit cryptographic access. The AWS Batch compute environment operates in private subnets with no inbound internet access; outbound access goes through a NAT Gateway strictly for pulling Docker images. No patient PHI (names, DOBs) is included in the file names or metadata; samples are tracked strictly by obfuscated UUIDs.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform provisions the VPC, IAM roles, S3 buckets, and AWS Batch queues. Bioinformatics tools are packaged into Docker images (e.g., `broadinstitute/gatk:4.2.0.0`) and stored in AWS ECR to ensure exact scientific reproducibility. Nextflow configuration (`nextflow.config`) handles error strategies, such as automatically resubmitting a job to an On-Demand AWS Batch queue if it fails due to a Spot instance reclamation.
target_output.observability: Nextflow Tower provides a rich web dashboard visualizing the real-time execution DAG, showing exactly which samples are waiting, running, or failed. AWS CloudWatch logs capture standard output and standard error from the individual GATK/BWA containers, crucial for bioinformatics debugging when a specific sample fails alignment.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Highly optimized for cost. By leveraging AWS Batch with EC2 Spot Instances, compute costs for the heavy alignment phase are reduced by up to 70%. FSx for Lustre is provisioned specifically for the duration of the pipeline run and torn down afterwards to avoid paying for idle high-performance storage. S3 Lifecycle Rules automatically transition the massive raw FASTQ files to S3 Glacier Deep Archive after 30 days to comply with data retention policies at a fraction of standard storage costs.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Months 1-2): Dockerization of the bioinformatics tools and Terraform setup of AWS Batch. Phase 2 (Months 3-4): Nextflow script development, translating the legacy bash logic into scalable DAG processes. Phase 3 (Month 5): Integration of FSx for Lustre and HIPAA security auditing. Phase 4 (Month 6): Benchmarking the 100-sample concurrent load, cost optimization, and LIMS integration.
target_output.development_guidelines: All pipeline parameters (CPU, Memory requirements per process) must be defined in the `nextflow.config` file, not hardcoded in the scripts, allowing easy switching between local testing and AWS execution. Docker tags must be pinned to specific hashes, not `latest`, to ensure CLIA regulatory compliance requiring deterministic software versions for clinical diagnostics.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: EC2 Spot instance interruptions causing jobs to fail and restart, increasing overall pipeline latency. Mitigation: Nextflow's native `errorStrategy = 'retry'` and `maxRetries` directives handle Spot interruptions transparently without failing the entire DAG. Tradeoff: Utilizing FSx for Lustre adds architectural complexity and setup time for each pipeline run compared to direct S3 streaming, but it is an absolute necessity because standard S3 throughput cannot support the random I/O read patterns required by GATK variant calling tools.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided to use Nextflow instead of AWS Step Functions. While Step Functions are native to AWS, Nextflow is the open-source standard in the bioinformatics community, making it much easier to recruit geneticists and port existing scientific workflows.

metadata.notes: Strong bioinformatics architecture, correctly utilizing DAG orchestration and HPC cloud patterns.

project_goal.value: Open-source JavaScript library for rendering large-scale 3D network graphs
project_goal.rationale: Empower data scientists to visualize complex relationships

project_class.rationale: Published to npm, embedded in user web apps

capabilities.rationale: Client-side rendering and data structure manipulation

complexity_level.rationale: Custom WebGL shaders and force-directed physics algorithms

risk_level.rationale: Client-side execution, no backend data access

feature_scope.value: Force-directed layout, custom node/edge geometries, camera controls, hover tooltips

mvp_scope.value: Support for rendering up to 100,000 nodes and 500,000 edges
mvp_scope.rationale: Establish performance dominance over SVG-based libraries

security_baseline.value: No execution of arbitrary string inputs (prevent XSS in tooltips)

data_platform.rationale: Data passed in memory by host app

future_scope.value: WebXR support for Virtual Reality data exploration

constraints.value: Must maintain 60 FPS while rendering 100k nodes

observability_baseline.value: Console warnings for deprecated API usage

execution_preference.rationale: Can be used in React, Vue, or Vanilla JS

reasoner_reviews.product: To beat libraries like D3 or Cytoscape in scale, we have to leverage the GPU heavily. 100k nodes will melt the DOM if we try to use SVGs.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Using Three.js is the right call. However, to hit 60 FPS with 100k nodes, we cannot create 100,000 separate `Mesh` objects. We must use `InstancedMesh` for the nodes and custom `BufferGeometry` for the edges. Furthermore, the `d3-force-3d` physics engine will block the main UI thread if it runs 100,000 node calculations synchronously. It must be offloaded to a Web Worker.
reasoner_reviews.security: Tooltip HTML sanitization is key. The library should accept a callback function for tooltip formatting and run it through DOMPurify internally if it injects raw HTML.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Hitting 60 FPS constraint dictates the architecture: Web Workers for physics, InstancedMesh for rendering.

frontend.architecture: TypeScript class exposing lifecycle methods (`init`, `graphData`, `pauseAnimation`). Internal composition of a Three.js WebGLRenderer, Scene, and Camera
frontend.rendering_optimization: Use `InstancedMesh` to draw all nodes in a single draw call. Use `LineSegments` with a single unified `Float32Array` for edge positions
frontend.physics_engine: Offload `d3-force-3d` to an inline Web Worker. Sync node positions back to main thread via `SharedArrayBuffer` or transferable `Float32Array` to avoid serialization overhead

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a high-performance frontend SDK for 3D network graph visualization. The core constraints are hitting 60 FPS while rendering massive datasets (100k nodes, 500k edges). This mandates avoiding the DOM and traditional object-per-node WebGL patterns. I'm specifying an architecture using Three.js `InstancedMesh` for single-draw-call rendering. Additionally, the heavy physics calculations (`d3-force-3d`) are explicitly pushed to a Web Worker, communicating with the main rendering thread via transferable `Float32Array` buffers to prevent UI blocking.
target_output.title: High-Performance 3D Graph Visualization Library
target_output.executive_summary: A framework-agnostic, open-source JavaScript library designed to render massive network graphs in 3D. Targeting data scientists and frontend developers, the SDK provides an intuitive API to visualize complex relationships (up to 100,000 nodes) directly in the browser. By leveraging Three.js WebGL capabilities via `InstancedMesh` and offloading heavy force-directed physics calculations to dedicated Web Workers, the library achieves a buttery-smooth 60 FPS, far surpassing the limitations of traditional SVG-based visualization tools.
target_output.architecture_overview: The library is designed as a standalone TypeScript module, distributed via npm, which injects an HTML5 `<canvas>` element into the host application's DOM. The architecture is split into two primary threads. The Main Thread handles the Three.js WebGL scene, camera orbit controls, user interaction (raycasting for hover/click events), and rendering loops. To render 100,000 nodes efficiently, it uses `THREE.InstancedMesh`, allowing the GPU to draw identical geometries (like spheres) with varying colors and positions in a single draw call. The Background Thread (Web Worker) runs the `d3-force-3d` physics simulation. Every frame, the physics engine updates the spatial coordinates of the graph. To avoid the massive overhead of JSON serialization, the worker passes the updated coordinates back to the Main Thread using zero-copy Transferable Objects (`Float32Array`). The Main Thread applies this array directly to the InstancedMesh instance matrices, updating the scene instantly.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: TypeScript. 3D Engine: Three.js. Physics Engine: d3-force-3d, d3-quadtree. Concurrency: Web Workers. Bundler: Rollup (generating UMD, ESM, and CJS builds). DOM Sanitization: DOMPurify (optional dependency). Code Formatting: Prettier, ESLint.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Force-Directed Layout executing N-body physics algorithms to spatially organize clustered data. GPU-Accelerated Rendering utilizing instancing and custom shaders to draw half a million edges efficiently. Interactive Raycasting mapping mouse coordinates to 3D space to trigger node selection and tooltips. Camera Controllers providing intuitive orbit, pan, and zoom capabilities. Framework Agnostic API ensuring easy wrapping in React (`<ForceGraph3D />`), Vue, or Vanilla JS.
target_output.system_components: `GraphAPI`: The public facade exposing data setters and configuration options. `WebGLRendererCore`: Manages the Three.js scene, lighting, and rendering loop. `PhysicsWorker`: The isolated JavaScript worker calculating velocity and positioning. `InteractionManager`: Handles window events, resizing, and raycasting. `TooltipOverlay`: An absolute-positioned HTML `div` floating above the canvas for rendering text details.
target_output.workflows: Initialization Workflow: Developer calls `const myGraph = ForceGraph3D()(document.getElementById('viz'))`. The library appends a canvas, initializes Three.js, and spawns the Physics Worker. Data Ingestion: Developer calls `myGraph.graphData({ nodes: [...], links: [...] })`. The library parses the data, allocates a `Float32Array` for positions, and sends a copy to the Worker. The WebGL Core initializes the `InstancedMesh`. Animation Loop: Worker calculates one 'tick' of the force layout. Worker sends the updated position array back to the Main thread. `requestAnimationFrame` fires in the Main thread. WebGL Core updates the `InstancedMesh.instanceMatrix` and `LineSegments.geometry.attributes.position` with the new array. WebGL renders the scene.
target_output.data_model: The library consumes plain JavaScript objects passed in memory. Expected Schema: `GraphData { nodes: Node[], links: Link[] }`. `Node { id: string, val: number, color: string, ... }`. `Link { source: string, target: string, ... }`. Internally, this object structure is flattened into highly optimized TypedArrays (e.g., `Float32Array` for x, y, z coordinates) to interface directly with WebGL buffer attributes.
target_output.api_design: Method-chaining API design (D3 style). Example: `ForceGraph3D().width(800).height(600).nodeAutoColorBy('group').graphData(data)`. Exposes callbacks for interactions: `onNodeClick((node, event) => {...})`, `nodeTooltipText((node) => '' + node.id + '')`.
target_output.security_and_compliance: As a purely client-side rendering library, it does not interact with external networks, eliminating API security risks. The primary security consideration is Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via custom tooltips. If developers configure the library to render HTML in tooltips based on dataset properties, the library documentation mandates and provides examples of using a sanitizer (like DOMPurify) to cleanse the input strings before injecting them into the DOM via `innerHTML`.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: The source code is hosted on GitHub. GitHub Actions run automated tests using Puppeteer to render headless WebGL frames and perform pixel-matching regression tests. The build pipeline uses Rollup to transpile TypeScript into ECMAScript modules (ESM) for modern bundlers, and UMD format for direct `<script>` tag inclusion via CDNs like unpkg. Semantic versioning is strictly adhered to on npm releases.
target_output.observability: Client-side debugging is supported via internal logging flags. Developers can enable `debug: true` to output console warnings if they pass malformed data (e.g., a link referencing a non-existent node ID). The library internally measures 'Tick Time' and 'Render Time' in milliseconds, exposing an API method `myGraph.getPerformanceMetrics()` for developers to build their own telemetry dashboards.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero operational costs for the library maintainers beyond basic GitHub/npm infrastructure. For integrating developers, scaling is bounded by the end-user's client hardware. The library elegantly degrades; if a user lacks a dedicated GPU, the WebGL framerate will drop, but the web worker physics thread will continue running uninterrupted, preventing browser tab freezes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Weeks 1-3): Project scaffolding, Rollup configuration, and basic Three.js scene setup with standard meshes. Phase 2 (Weeks 4-6): Implementation of `InstancedMesh` for nodes and unified buffer geometries for links. Phase 3 (Weeks 7-9): Web Worker integration, offloading `d3-force-3d`, and establishing the TypedArray communication bridge. Phase 4 (Weeks 10-12): Raycasting interactions, tooltip logic, documentation site, and npm 1.0 release.
target_output.development_guidelines: Performance is paramount. Avoid creating objects or allocating memory inside the main rendering loop (`requestAnimationFrame`); reuse existing vectors and matrices to prevent triggering the JavaScript Garbage Collector, which causes micro-stutters. Fallback to CPU-based color calculations only when absolutely necessary; push as much visual styling logic into custom WebGL fragment shaders as possible.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Raycasting against 100,000 nodes every time the mouse moves is computationally prohibitive. Mitigation: Implement a spatial index (BVH - Bounding Volume Hierarchy) or an Octree in the Main Thread to rapidly cull un-hovered nodes before executing precise ray intersection math. Tradeoff: Using raw WebGL and Web Workers vastly increases the internal complexity of the library's codebase compared to a simpler SVG/Canvas 2D approach, raising the barrier to entry for open-source contributors.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided against integrating directly into React's render lifecycle (e.g., creating a pure React component) to maintain framework agnosticism. React developers will use a lightweight wrapper that attaches the Vanilla JS instance to a `useRef`.

metadata.notes: Good representation of SDK architecture, properly addresses WebGL scale constraints.

reasoner_reviews.security: To comply with GDPR, the tracking system should assign a random UUID to a person when they enter. We shouldn't run facial recognition; we just track the 'blob' through the store and map the UUID to the QR code scanned at the gate.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the autonomous store checkout architecture. The critical flaw in round 1 was attempting to stream 100+ live camera feeds to the cloud, easily violating the 1Gbps uplink constraint. The fix employs a heavy edge-computing architecture. High-definition RTSP streams are routed over a local 10G network to a backroom cluster of NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin nodes. YOLOv8 and Re-ID models run locally via TensorRT. The cloud is relegated to billing and final receipt generation, receiving only lightweight JSON events over MQTT (e.g., 'Person A picked up Item B'). This drastically cuts bandwidth, ensures low-latency cart updates, and enhances privacy by keeping raw video local.

fix_report.action_taken: Migrated all computer vision inference (YOLOv8, Pose Estimation) from AWS cloud to an in-store Edge TPU cluster (NVIDIA Jetson).
fix_report.expected_outcome: Store uplink bandwidth usage drops from >5Gbps to <10Mbps (only transmitting MQTT metadata), satisfying the 1Gbps constraint.

target_output.title: Autonomous Checkout Computer Vision System
target_output.executive_summary: A state-of-the-art, edge-AI computer vision system enabling frictionless, cashier-less retail checkout. By processing over 100 high-definition camera feeds in real-time, the system tracks shoppers and products to automatically build virtual shopping carts. To overcome strict commercial bandwidth limits (1Gbps), the architecture utilizes a hybrid model: heavy deep-learning inference runs on an in-store NVIDIA Jetson edge cluster, while lightweight metadata is synced to AWS for payment processing. Built with privacy in mind, the platform adheres to GDPR by avoiding facial recognition and isolating PII from physical tracking data.
target_output.architecture_overview: The physical store is outfitted with ceiling-mounted RGB cameras providing overlapping coverage, connected via PoE to a local 10G switch. In the store's server room, a cluster of NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin nodes ingests the RTSP streams. These edge nodes run a complex Python-based AI pipeline optimized with TensorRT: YOLOv8 detects products and hands, OpenPose tracks skeletal movements (specifically the hand-to-shelf vector), and a custom Re-ID (Re-Identification) model tracks anonymous human shapes across different camera views. A local Go-based Event Router aggregates these visual inferences, using spatial heuristics to determine when a 'Pick' or 'Put-back' event occurs. The local Go service updates the live virtual cart in a local Redis instance. When the shopper exits the store, the Go service packages the final cart state into a JSON payload and transmits it over an MQTT bridge to AWS IoT Core. The AWS cloud backend (PostgreSQL/Stripe) then charges the customer's vaulted credit card and emails the receipt.
target_output.technology_stack: Edge AI: Python, YOLOv8, OpenPose, TensorRT, NVIDIA DeepStream. Edge Backend: Go 1.22, Redis. Cloud Backend: Node.js, PostgreSQL 15, AWS IoT Core, Stripe API. Frontend: React Native (iOS/Android App). Hardware: NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin, PoE IP Cameras. Infrastructure: Kubernetes (K3s on Edge, EKS in Cloud).
target_output.functional_feature_map: Shopper Re-Identification assigning an ephemeral UUID to customers upon entry via QR scan, tracking them across camera zones. Hand-to-Shelf Interaction Detection correlating skeletal hand coordinates with bounding boxes of recognized SKUs. Edge Cart Management maintaining a low-latency, resilient ledger of picked items tolerant of internet outages. Cloud Billing Integration triggering automated credit card charges upon physical exit detection.
target_output.system_components: `In-Store Camera Network`: RTSP video sources. `DeepStream Inference Pods`: GPU-accelerated containers running the vision models. `Local Event Aggregator (Go)`: Translates bounding box intersections into logical cart events. `Local Redis Cache`: Holds ephemeral UUID-to-Cart mappings. `AWS IoT Gateway`: Secure MQTT endpoint bridging the edge and cloud. `Cloud Billing Service`: Generates receipts and calls payment gateways.
target_output.workflows: Entry Workflow: Shopper opens app, generating a dynamic QR code linked to their user ID. Shopper scans QR at the physical turnstile. AWS sends the UserID to the Store Edge. Turnstile camera captures the shopper, assigns ephemeral UUID-999, and maps it to the UserID. Shopping Workflow: Shopper walks to aisle 3. Re-ID network tracks UUID-999 across cameras 5, 6, and 7. Shopper reaches out. OpenPose tracks the wrist vector. YOLOv8 detects a 'Soda Can' bounding box overlapping the hand vector. Shopper retracts hand. Event Router logic triggers: `Action: PICK, User: UUID-999, Item: Soda`. Event Router updates local Redis cart. Checkout Workflow: Shopper walks through the exit zone. Camera detects exit. Event Router fetches final Redis cart for UUID-999, sends MQTT message to AWS: `{"user_id":"u_123", "cart":[...], "action":"checkout"}`. AWS charges Stripe, emails receipt, and clears edge state.
target_output.data_model: Edge Redis (Ephemeral): `cart:{uuid}` (Hash: sku_id -> quantity). `tracking:{uuid}` (String: current_camera_id, last_seen_timestamp). Cloud PostgreSQL (Persistent): `users` (id, stripe_customer_id, email). `store_inventory` (sku PK, name, price, bounding_box_features). `receipts` (id PK, user_id FK, store_id, total_amount, timestamp).
target_output.api_design: Edge-to-Cloud (MQTT): Topics include `store/{id}/events/checkout` and `store/{id}/telemetry/health`. Cloud-to-App (REST): `GET /v1/receipts`, `POST /v1/auth/qr-code`. App polling is minimized; receipts are pushed via Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) push notifications.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Data privacy is paramount (GDPR/CCPA). The system explicitly does not use Facial Recognition; the Re-ID model utilizes clothing color, height, and gait to maintain the tracking UUID. Raw video footage never leaves the store's local network; it is stored on an encrypted local NAS for 72 hours solely for algorithm debugging and theft investigation before automatic deletion. The mapping of the physical UUID to the customer's PII (Name, Email) is secured via strict IAM roles, ensuring edge AI nodes only know the anonymous UUID.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Edge cluster is managed via K3s (Lightweight Kubernetes). An automated pipeline cross-compiles the TensorRT engines in the cloud and deploys the updated Docker images to the store servers overnight using AWS IoT Greengrass. Store managers have a local dashboard to monitor camera uptime and edge server temperatures via Prometheus metrics.
target_output.observability: Datadog agents run on both the cloud and edge clusters. Crucial custom metrics include `inference_fps` (must remain > 15fps per camera to prevent tracking loss) and `gpu_utilization`. A specialized visual debugger allows AI engineers to securely remote into the store network and view a live camera feed overlaid with bounding boxes to diagnose misclassifications.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: High upfront CapEx for hardware (cameras, cabling, Jetson AGX servers). Low ongoing OpEx due to edge computing eliminating massive cloud compute and bandwidth costs. The system scales within the store by adding more Jetson nodes to the K3s cluster; the Go Event Router dynamically load-balances camera RTSP streams across available inference nodes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Months 1-3): Physical store wire-up, K3s edge cluster setup, and basic YOLOv8 item detection data collection. Phase 2 (Months 4-6): Implementation of the Re-ID tracking network and hand-to-shelf interaction logic in Go. Phase 3 (Months 7-8): Cloud billing integration, React Native app development, and employee-only beta testing. Phase 4 (Month 9): Public launch, monitoring shrink rates, and continuous model fine-tuning.
target_output.development_guidelines: AI engineers must optimize all PyTorch models using TensorRT to leverage the Jetson's DLA (Deep Learning Accelerator) cores, freeing up the main GPU cores for the complex Re-ID tracking matrices. The Go event router must gracefully handle transient network partitions, storing checkout events locally in Redis and retrying the MQTT transmission when internet connectivity is restored.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Occlusion (e.g., a crowd of people blocking the camera's view of a shelf) leading to missed picks. Mitigation: Strategic overlapping camera placement ensuring every shelf is visible from at least three distinct angles. Tradeoff: The hybrid architecture drastically increases deployment and maintenance complexity compared to a pure cloud SaaS product. Rolling out an OS patch requires updating physical machines in a retail backroom rather than a centralized AWS datacenter, necessitating robust fleet management tooling.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided to use MQTT over gRPC for the Edge-to-Cloud communication to better handle the unreliable nature of retail store internet connections. Confirmed that weight sensors on shelves will be deferred to future scope to minimize the initial hardware installation complexity of the MVP.

metadata.notes: Strong AI edge architecture, solves bandwidth limit perfectly via edge-computing paradigm.

reasoner_reviews.product: This infrastructure will finally let us stop receiving tax documents over unencrypted emails.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Using AWS API Gateway to generate S3 Presigned URLs allows citizens to upload directly to the bucket, keeping the heavy files off our compute layer. Lambda triggered by S3 uploads can handle the malware scanning.

It just specifies standard AES-256 without ensuring the use of FIPS-validated cryptographic modules. It also fails to specify network controls to prevent the Lambda functions from accidentally sending data to public internet endpoints.

target_output.thinking_summary: INTENTIONAL FLAW GENERATION. The contract specifies 'FIPS 140-2 compliance', 'FedRAMP High', and 'AWS GovCloud'. I will intentionally write a flawed architecture plan that deploys this to standard AWS commercial regions (e.g., us-east-1), uses generic encryption without mentioning FIPS modules, and routes Lambda traffic over the public internet, violating the core security baselines for this highly sensitive government project.
target_output.title: Secure Document Transfer Infrastructure
target_output.executive_summary: A cloud infrastructure project designed to provide citizens with a secure mechanism to upload highly confidential legal and tax documents to government agencies. Replacing legacy email submission workflows, the system utilizes AWS serverless technologies to provide scalable, direct-to-storage uploads. An automated malware scanning pipeline ensures that civil servants only interact with clean, verified files, improving both security posture and operational efficiency.
target_output.architecture_overview: The architecture revolves around Amazon S3 for durable object storage. A citizen interacts with the public-facing government portal, which makes a request to an AWS API Gateway. The Gateway triggers an AWS Lambda function that generates a time-limited S3 Presigned URL. The citizen's browser uploads the document directly to a 'Quarantine' S3 bucket using this URL. This upload event triggers an asynchronous Lambda function running ClamAV. The Lambda downloads the file, scans it for malware signatures, and if clean, moves it to an 'Internal' S3 bucket. If malicious, the file is deleted. Civil servants, authenticated via their internal agency SSO, can then access the 'Internal' bucket to download and process the documents.
target_output.technology_stack: Infrastructure as Code: Terraform. Compute: AWS Lambda (Python 3.11). Storage: Amazon S3. Ingress: AWS API Gateway. Security Scanning: ClamAV. CI/CD: GitLab. Observability: AWS CloudWatch, Splunk. Deployment Target: AWS Commercial Regions (us-east-1).
target_output.functional_feature_map: Presigned URL Generation allowing secure, temporary write access to S3 without distributing permanent credentials. Direct-to-Cloud Uploads bypassing server bottlenecks by transferring files directly from the citizen's browser to the storage layer. Automated Malware Quarantine scanning every incoming PDF and JPG before it touches the internal agency network. Lifecycle Management automatically purging documents 30 days after ingestion to minimize data footprint.
target_output.system_components: `API Gateway`: Public endpoint for requesting upload tokens. `Token Lambda`: Python function generating the presigned URLs. `Quarantine Bucket`: Publicly writable (via presigned URL) S3 bucket holding unscanned files. `Scanning Lambda`: Compute environment running the ClamAV engine. `Internal Bucket`: Private S3 bucket storing verified documents. `Splunk Forwarder`: Service streaming CloudWatch logs to the agency SIEM.
target_output.workflows: Upload Request: Citizen portal requests an upload link. API Gateway invokes Token Lambda. Lambda calls AWS STS for temporary credentials, generates a Presigned URL for `quarantine-bucket/uuid.pdf`, and returns it. File Upload: Citizen browser executes an HTTP PUT directly to the S3 Presigned URL. S3 accepts the file. Malware Scan: S3 triggers the Scanning Lambda. Lambda downloads `uuid.pdf` over the internet, runs `clamscan`. Scan is clean. Lambda copies file to `internal-bucket/uuid.pdf` and deletes the original from the quarantine bucket. Staff Access: Civil servant logs into internal portal, views list of clean files, and downloads the document for processing.
target_output.data_model: As an infrastructure project, there is no relational database. The data model is defined by S3 object metadata. Uploaded objects are tagged with `UploadDate`, `FileExtension`, and `ScanStatus`. Document UUIDs are randomly generated to prevent direct object reference enumeration.
target_output.api_design: REST API for token generation: `GET /api/v1/upload-url?fileType=pdf`. Returns `{"url": "https://s3.amazonaws.com/...", "expires_in": 300}`. All external APIs enforce TLS encryption.
target_output.security_and_compliance: The system encrypts data at rest using standard Amazon S3 Server-Side Encryption (SSE-S3) with AES-256. Data in transit is secured using HTTPS. The API Gateway is open to the public internet to allow citizen access. The Lambda functions are deployed without VPC attachments, allowing them to communicate directly with the public S3 endpoints and download ClamAV virus definition updates easily over the public internet. Access to the internal bucket relies on standard AWS IAM policies granted to civil servant roles.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform code defines the infrastructure state. GitLab CI/CD pipelines run `terraform apply` to deploy changes. The ClamAV Lambda deployment package is built using a Docker container to ensure the necessary C-libraries are included, then uploaded as a .zip file. Operations teams monitor the CloudWatch metrics to ensure the Scanning Lambda is not timing out on larger files.
target_output.observability: All AWS API calls are logged via CloudTrail to provide a comprehensive audit log of who generated URLs and accessed the internal buckets. These logs are forwarded to the agency's Splunk instance. CloudWatch Alarms are configured to alert the DevOps team if the error rate of the Scanning Lambda exceeds 5%, indicating a potential issue with the ClamAV definitions.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Highly cost-optimized serverless architecture. AWS Lambda and S3 scale automatically to handle thousands of concurrent uploads during peak tax season without any manual intervention. Costs are strictly pay-per-use, meaning the infrastructure costs near zero during off-peak night hours.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Months 1-2): Terraform scaffolding for S3 buckets and API Gateway. Phase 2 (Months 3-4): Token Lambda development and frontend portal integration. Phase 3 (Months 5-6): ClamAV Scanning Lambda implementation and testing. Phase 4 (Months 7-8): Splunk logging integration, load testing, and production rollout to the us-east-1 region.
target_output.development_guidelines: All Lambda functions should be written in Python for ease of maintenance by the internal IT staff. Terraform state must be stored remotely in a secured S3 bucket. Ensure the ClamAV virus database is updated daily by pulling the latest definitions from the public ClamAV mirrors at the start of each Lambda cold boot.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: A zero-day malware variant bypasses the ClamAV scan. Mitigation: The internal bucket restricts execution privileges, so even if a malicious PDF is downloaded, it cannot automatically execute code on the agency network. Tradeoff: Using a cold-starting Lambda function for malware scanning introduces a slight delay (several seconds) between a citizen uploading a file and it becoming available for staff, traded for massive cost savings over running persistent EC2 scanning clusters.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided against building a custom SFTP server, as maintaining SSH keys for citizens is impossible at scale; Presigned URLs via HTTPS provide a much better UX.

metadata.notes: INTENTIONALLY FLAWED: Ignores GovCloud/FedRAMP mandates (deploys to commercial us-east-1), lacks FIPS 140-2 encryption modules, and places Lambdas on the public internet instead of a VPC.

reasoner_reviews.product: Clinics are chaotic. The UI needs to be clear, and Front Desk staff shouldn't be able to alter a doctor's SOAP notes.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Ruby on Rails is perfect here. We can use the `acts_as_tenant` gem to enforce multi-tenancy at the model level, preventing cross-clinic data leakage.
reasoner_reviews.security: The previous draft architecture failed the multi-tenant constraint. It relied on developers manually adding `where(clinic_id: current_user.clinic_id)` to every database query. This is highly error-prone and will inevitably lead to data bleeding between clinics.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 500ms response time is easy for a standard Rails app on Heroku if we utilize Redis for caching the calendar views and keep N+1 queries under control.

security.data_protection: PostgreSQL Row-Level Security (RLS) policies acting as a database-level safety net beneath the Rails application logic

revision_memory.changes_for_round_2: Implemented Row-Level Security (RLS) in PostgreSQL and integrated the `acts_as_tenant` gem to enforce strict, automatic multi-tenant data isolation at the ORM and Database levels, resolving the high-risk security flaw (ISS-171).
