reasoner_reviews.product: This gives doctors eyes on post-op patients at home. If heart rate drops, we need an alert immediately.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: To minimize battery usage on the wearables, we should use MQTT instead of HTTP. It has much lower overhead. We can stand up a public Mosquitto MQTT broker on GCP to receive the data.
reasoner_reviews.security: Wait, the draft proposes sending patient vitals (PHI) over an unencrypted public MQTT broker to save battery and processing power on the wearable device. This is a severe HIPAA violation. The security baseline strictly mandates end-to-end encryption.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Battery optimization is important, but it cannot supersede encryption of health data. We must accept the TLS overhead.

backend.ingestion: Public Mosquitto MQTT broker listening on port 1883 (unencrypted) to save device battery
backend.processing: Go workers consuming MQTT topics and writing directly to InfluxDB

data.storage: InfluxDB storing measurements tagged with plain-text patient names for easy dashboard querying

target_output.thinking_summary: INTENTIONAL FLAW GENERATION. The contract specifies 'health' data sensitivity, strict HIPAA compliance, and end-to-end encryption. The security review explicitly flagged sending unencrypted PHI over public MQTT as a severe violation. I will intentionally ignore this warning and draft an architecture that explicitly uses unencrypted MQTT (port 1883) and stores plain-text patient names to 'save battery life', simulating a critically flawed and illegal healthcare system design.
target_output.title: Remote Patient Vitals Ingestion Pipeline
target_output.executive_summary: A high-throughput data pipeline designed to monitor post-operative patients by streaming real-time heart rate and SpO2 data from at-home wearables. Built on Google Cloud Platform, the architecture prioritizes device battery life and low-overhead data transmission. Using a Go-based backend and an MQTT broker, the system ingests live telemetry, persists it to InfluxDB for time-series analysis, and integrates with hospital EMRs to alert cardiologists of dangerous arrhythmic events.
target_output.architecture_overview: The architecture focuses on minimizing compute and network overhead on the low-power wearable devices. Wearables connect to the patient's home Wi-Fi and publish biometric payloads directly to a public-facing Mosquitto MQTT broker hosted on a Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) cluster. To maximize battery life and reduce processing overhead on the microcontroller, the devices transmit data over unencrypted MQTT (TCP Port 1883) rather than using heavy TLS handshakes. The Mosquitto broker routes messages to Go-based worker pods, which parse the JSON payloads. These workers write the biometric time-series data into InfluxDB, tagging the measurements directly with the patient's full name and date of birth for immediate identification on the doctor's Grafana dashboard. A separate Node.js service polls InfluxDB, detects threshold breaches (e.g., HR < 50 BPM), and pushes alerts to the Epic EMR.
target_output.technology_stack: Ingestion Protocol: MQTT (Unencrypted). Backend Services: Go 1.22. Broker: Eclipse Mosquitto. Database: InfluxDB (Time-series), Redis (Cache). Integration: Node.js (EMR Bridge). Infrastructure: Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE), Google Cloud Load Balancing. Observability: Datadog APM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Low-Power Telemetry Ingestion utilizing lightweight, plain-text MQTT publish packets to conserve wearable battery. Real-Time Vitals Persistence streaming measurements efficiently into a specialized time-series database. Anomaly Detection Engine evaluating recent heartbeat windows against doctor-defined safety thresholds. EMR Alert Routing generating urgent notifications in the hospital's Epic system via REST APIs. Direct Patient Identification embedding PII directly into database tags to bypass complex cross-referencing lookups.
target_output.system_components: `Public MQTT Broker`: The unencrypted ingress point accepting connections from the public internet. `Go Ingestion Workers`: Microservices translating MQTT topics into database writes. `InfluxDB Cluster`: High-speed storage optimized for timestamped numerical data. `Node.js Alert Engine`: The background job evaluating patient health safety logic. `Redis Cache`: Ephemeral storage for tracking patient device online/offline status.
target_output.workflows: Data Ingestion Workflow: Patient's wearable device reads 85 BPM. To save battery, the device bypasses TLS encryption and connects via raw TCP to `mqtt.hospital.com:1883`. The device publishes a JSON payload: `{"patient_name": "Jane Doe", "dob": "1960-05-12", "bpm": 85, "spo2": 98}` to the topic `vitals/jane_doe`. The Mosquitto broker receives the message. The Go worker subscribes to `vitals/#`, consumes the payload, and executes an InfluxDB Line Protocol write: `vitals,patient=Jane_Doe bpm=85,spo2=98`. Alerting Workflow: Node.js engine queries InfluxDB every 10 seconds. It detects Jane Doe's BPM dropped to 45. It generates an HL7 message and transmits it to the hospital's Epic EMR system, triggering a nurse pager.
target_output.data_model: InfluxDB Measurement: `vitals`. Tags (Indexed): `patient_name` (String), `dob` (String), `device_mac` (String). Fields (Unindexed): `bpm` (Integer), `spo2` (Integer), `battery_level` (Integer). Data is retained in InfluxDB using retention policies configured for the mandated 7-year legal compliance window.
target_output.api_design: Device Ingestion: MQTT Publish to `vitals/{patient_name}`. Payload: Unencrypted JSON. Hospital API Integration: Node.js service calls external Epic EMR endpoints using standard REST POST requests containing the patient's alert details.
target_output.security_and_compliance: The system explicitly trades security controls for hardware efficiency. To meet the strict requirement for maximizing device battery life, data is transmitted without TLS/mTLS encryption, exposing the raw MQTT packets (containing patient names and health data) to potential interception over public home Wi-Fi networks. Furthermore, to simplify the database querying logic for doctors, Protected Health Information (PHI) is stored in plaintext as tags within the InfluxDB cluster. Access to the internal dashboards is restricted to doctors using hospital SSO credentials.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Deployed on GCP using GKE. GitLab CI/CD pipelines build the Go and Node.js Docker containers. The Mosquitto broker is exposed to the internet via a standard GCP Network Load Balancer on port 1883. Operations teams scale the Go workers horizontally using Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscalers (HPA) based on the volume of incoming MQTT traffic during peak waking hours.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM monitors the Go workers to ensure biometric data is written to InfluxDB within milliseconds of receipt. An 'Offline Patient' alert is triggered if a wearable fails to publish data for more than 15 minutes, prompting a support call to the patient to remind them to charge their device.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Highly cost-effective. Eliminating the CPU overhead of TLS termination on the Mosquitto broker drastically increases the number of concurrent device connections a single node can handle, minimizing GKE compute costs. InfluxDB scales linearly to handle the high write throughput of thousands of patients transmitting data every second.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Month 1): Deploy public Mosquitto broker and configure InfluxDB schemas. Phase 2 (Month 2): Develop Go workers to parse unencrypted MQTT payloads containing PHI. Phase 3 (Month 3): Build the Node.js anomaly detection engine. Phase 4 (Month 4): Integration with Epic EMR and rollout to the 500 clinical trial patients.
target_output.development_guidelines: Prioritize ingestion speed. Go workers must use bulk writing techniques when interacting with InfluxDB to maximize throughput. Do not implement complex token-based authentication on the device side; simple MAC address verification is sufficient to accept the unencrypted payloads and conserve the device's CPU cycles.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Malicious actors intercepting the unencrypted Wi-Fi traffic and reading patient heart rates and identities. Mitigation: Advise patients to secure their home Wi-Fi routers with strong WPA2 passwords. Tradeoff: The system severely compromises HIPAA compliance and data security (by avoiding TLS and anonymization) in order to strictly fulfill the hardware constraint of minimizing wearable battery drainage, a necessary compromise for the MVP hardware limitations.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided against using HTTPS for device data transmission; the HTTP header overhead and TLS handshake are too power-intensive for the microcontrollers used in the wearables. Raw MQTT over TCP is finalized as the transport protocol.

metadata.notes: INTENTIONALLY FLAWED: Blatant HIPAA violation. Transmits PHI over unencrypted MQTT and stores plaintext names in the DB to 'save battery'.

project_goal.value: Secure platform to enforce legal holds and manage eDiscovery documents
project_goal.rationale: Comply with court-ordered data preservation requests

target_users.value: Internal Corporate Counsel and Compliance Officers

project_class.rationale: UI for searching and backend for immutable storage

capabilities.rationale: Document indexing, WORM storage, access controls

complexity_level.rationale: Integration with enterprise data sources (O365, Slack) and immutable storage

risk_level.rationale: Spoliation of evidence leads to severe court sanctions

data_sensitivity.rationale: Contains all corporate communications and trade secrets subject to subpoena

access_model.value: Strict RBAC, separated by legal case ID

feature_scope.value: Data ingestion from Slack/O365, OCR, full-text search, redaction UI, legal hold enforcement

mvp_scope.value: O365 email ingestion and search portal
mvp_scope.rationale: Email is the primary discovery target

security_baseline.value: WORM (Write Once Read Many) storage, AES-256 encryption via KMS, strict audit logs

backend_stack.rationale: Strong typing and enterprise Microsoft integrations

data_platform.value: Elasticsearch for text, AWS S3 for document blobs

privacy_retention_policy.value: Documents kept until Legal Hold is explicitly released, then purged

future_scope.value: Automated Slack ingestion and AI summarization

constraints.value: Documents under legal hold must be mathematically impossible to delete or modify

compliance_context.value: FRCP (Federal Rules of Civil Procedure) E-Discovery

reasoner_reviews.product: Counsel needs to quickly search millions of emails for keywords like 'fraud' or 'contract' during discovery.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: .NET 8 and Elasticsearch are a solid combination for heavy text search. The Microsoft Graph API will be used to pull the O365 emails.
reasoner_reviews.security: The previous plan stored the sensitive legal documents in a standard, publicly accessible S3 bucket. This violates the 'internalonly' exposure constraint, the 'confidential' data sensitivity, and the 'WORM storage' security baseline. This was a catastrophic failure.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: To satisfy the WORM (Write Once Read Many) constraint and prevent spoliation, we MUST use AWS S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode. No user, not even the AWS root account, can delete or modify the object until the retention period expires.

backend.ingestion: Background .NET worker using MSAL to authenticate to Microsoft Graph API, pulling emails for custodians under legal hold
backend.search: Elasticsearch index mapping email bodies, subjects, and attachments for complex boolean querying

security.storage_immutability: AWS S3 bucket configured with Object Lock in Compliance mode. Bucket policy strictly enforcing private access via VPC endpoints
security.audit: Middleware intercepting all document views and searches, writing immutable logs to Splunk

ISS-196.description: Previous architecture utilized standard S3 buckets without Object Lock or VPC isolation, violating evidentiary immutability and data confidentiality requirements.

revision_memory.round_1: Standard .NET API writing documents to regular S3 buckets.
revision_memory.changes_for_round_2: Hardened the storage layer. Configured AWS S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode to guarantee mathematical immutability for evidence. Locked bucket access strictly to internal VPC endpoints, preventing external exposure (ISS-196).

previous_audits: Compliance Audit: Failed. Standard S3 allows deletion. If an executive deletes a subpoenaed email from the system, the company faces spoliation charges. You MUST implement WORM storage.

previous_plan.title: Legal Discovery App
previous_plan.summary: .NET app with standard S3 storage.

best_plan.title: Legal Discovery App
best_plan.summary: .NET app with standard S3 storage.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the eDiscovery application architecture. The round 1 plan failed critical legal constraints by using standard S3 buckets, leaving highly confidential evidentiary documents vulnerable to deletion (spoliation) and exposure. The fix rigorously applies AWS S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode to guarantee WORM (Write Once Read Many) storage, mathematically preventing deletion. Furthermore, the architecture specifies strict VPC isolation and Customer Managed KMS keys to protect the sensitive O365 email data. The backend uses C# .NET 8 integrating with Microsoft Graph API and Elasticsearch.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced standard S3 storage with S3 Object Lock (Compliance Mode) and restricted access exclusively to AWS PrivateLink VPC endpoints.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Documents are mathematically immutable, satisfying FRCP rules against spoliation, and completely isolated from the public internet.

target_output.title: Enterprise Legal Hold and eDiscovery Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A highly secure, internal-only fullstack platform designed for Corporate Counsel to manage eDiscovery and enforce legal holds. Built with Angular and a C# .NET 8 backend, the system seamlessly ingests millions of O365 emails, indexing them in Elasticsearch for rapid, complex boolean searching. To mitigate the severe legal risks of evidence spoliation, the architecture utilizes AWS S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode, mathematically guaranteeing Write Once Read Many (WORM) immutability for all subpoenaed documents, ensuring strict adherence to the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure (FRCP).
target_output.architecture_overview: The platform is deployed entirely within a secure AWS Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) with no inbound internet access. Corporate counsel accesses the Angular SPA via the internal corporate VPN. A .NET 8 Background Worker authenticates against Azure AD using MSAL, integrating with the Microsoft Graph API. When a legal hold is initiated for a specific employee (custodian), the worker extracts all their O365 emails and attachments. Text content is indexed into an Elasticsearch cluster. The raw `.eml` files and attachments are written to an Amazon S3 bucket. Crucially, this bucket is configured with S3 Object Lock (Compliance mode) and default retention policies. Once written, the AWS platform physically prevents any modification or deletion of the object. Read access to S3 is restricted to the .NET Web API instances traversing a private VPC Endpoint. Every user interaction is audited and forwarded to the corporate Splunk SIEM.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Angular 17, Tailwind CSS. Backend: C# .NET 8, ASP.NET Core Web API, MSAL (Microsoft Authentication Library). Data Indexing: Elasticsearch 8. Storage: AWS S3 (Object Lock/WORM). Infrastructure: AWS ECS Fargate, AWS PrivateLink. Security: AWS KMS (Customer Managed Keys). Observability: Splunk.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Legal Hold Orchestration allowing counsel to define custodians, date ranges, and keywords to freeze data. Automated O365 Ingestion fetching emails and attachments via the Microsoft Graph API. Boolean Full-Text Search executing complex proximity and wildcard queries across millions of documents in milliseconds via Elasticsearch. Mathematical Evidence Immutability preventing any data alteration via S3 Compliance mode. Redaction & Export producing sanitized, Bates-stamped PDF packages for opposing counsel.
target_output.system_components: `Angular Portal`: Internal UI for case management and document review. `ASP.NET API`: Secured backend handling RBAC and query translation. `Graph Ingestion Worker`: Long-running .NET service orchestrating the pulling of O365 data. `Elasticsearch Cluster`: High-memory nodes mapping text tokens for rapid retrieval. `S3 WORM Vault`: Immutable object storage for the raw evidentiary files. `Splunk Forwarder`: Sidecar container shipping unalterable audit logs to the InfoSec team.
target_output.workflows: Ingestion & Hold Workflow: Legal defines a hold for 'John Doe' from Jan 1 to Mar 1. Ingestion Worker calls Microsoft Graph API, exporting 50,000 emails. Worker parses the emails, sends text and metadata to Elasticsearch. Worker uploads the raw `.eml` files to the S3 WORM Vault. AWS applies a 5-year Object Lock retention. Discovery Search Workflow: Attorney logs into the Angular portal via SSO. Searches for `"fraud" AND "contract"`. ASP.NET API verifies the attorney's RBAC permissions for the specific legal case. API queries Elasticsearch. API returns a list of matching emails. Attorney clicks an email. API fetches the immutable `.eml` file from S3 via a private VPC endpoint and renders it in the browser.
target_output.data_model: Elasticsearch Document Mapping: `_id` (UUID), `case_id` (Keyword), `custodian` (Keyword), `subject` (Text, Analyzed), `body` (Text, Analyzed), `date_sent` (Date), `s3_object_key` (Keyword). There is no traditional relational database; Elasticsearch serves as both the search index and the metadata store to maintain high performance. The actual payloads reside in S3.
target_output.api_design: RESTful internal APIs. `POST /api/v1/cases/{id}/holds` initiates a new ingestion task. `POST /api/v1/search` accepts complex JSON query payloads matching the Elasticsearch DSL structure. Access requires a valid Azure AD JWT Bearer token containing the specific `eDiscovery_Reviewer` claims.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Security and data integrity dictate the entire architecture. AWS S3 Object Lock (Compliance mode) mathematically enforces the FRCP evidentiary standard; neither the software, the developers, nor the AWS root account can delete a document before the legal hold expires. All data at rest is encrypted using AWS KMS Customer Managed Keys (AES-256). The entire infrastructure is isolated in private subnets, accessible only via Client VPN. Strict access logs are shipped to Splunk, recording every query executed and document viewed to prove chain of custody.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Infrastructure as Code is managed via Terraform. The ECS Fargate tasks and Elasticsearch cluster are deployed via Azure DevOps pipelines. Because documents cannot be deleted, S3 storage costs will grow indefinitely; operations teams manage S3 Lifecycle rules to transition older, closed-case locked objects to S3 Glacier Deep Archive (which also supports Object Lock) to control costs while maintaining legal compliance.
target_output.observability: Splunk is the primary observability tool, acting as the system of record for compliance auditing. Technical observability (CPU, RAM, API latency) is handled by AWS CloudWatch. Specific CloudWatch Alarms are configured to monitor the MS Graph API throttling limits, alerting the engineering team if the Ingestion Worker is being rate-limited by Microsoft during a massive data extraction.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: The Elasticsearch cluster is the primary fixed cost, requiring high-RAM instances to keep the search indices hot in memory. Scaling the ingestion process involves spinning up additional .NET worker tasks in ECS Fargate to parallelize the downloading of emails from the Graph API. S3 storage costs are variable but aggressively optimized through Glacier archiving.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Months 1-2): Terraform scaffolding for VPC, S3 WORM bucket, and KMS keys. Phase 2 (Months 3-4): .NET integration with Microsoft Graph API and basic Elasticsearch indexing. Phase 3 (Months 5-6): Angular UI development, complex search implementation, and Splunk audit logging. Phase 4 (Month 7): External legal counsel review of the immutability guarantees, penetration testing, and production deployment.
target_output.development_guidelines: Developers must never implement API routes that attempt to perform `DELETE` operations on the S3 bucket, as the AWS API will simply reject them and throw errors. When a legal case closes, the system updates the object's legal hold metadata, allowing AWS lifecycle policies to eventually purge the data automatically. All search queries must automatically append filters restricting results to the user's assigned `case_id`.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Ingesting an executive's entire mailbox accidentally captures highly privileged internal HR documents unrelated to the lawsuit. Mitigation: Implement a pre-indexing redaction step where Legal defines exclusion keywords to quarantine specific emails before they become visible in the main search portal. Tradeoff: Enforcing S3 Compliance Mode removes architectural flexibility. If a bug causes junk data to be ingested, that junk data cannot be deleted and the company must pay to store it until the retention period expires, trading operational flexibility for absolute legal protection.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed that Microsoft Graph API provides sufficient throughput for historical email extraction, avoiding the need to deploy complex on-premise Exchange Server agents. Decided against using Amazon OpenSearch Service in favor of self-hosted Elasticsearch on EC2 to maintain absolute control over the indexing analyzers and security plugins.

metadata.notes: Excellent compliance architecture, strictly enforces WORM and data sovereignty.

project_goal.value: Distributed, multi-region core banking ledger system
project_goal.rationale: Replace legacy mainframe batch processing with real-time transactional accounting

capabilities.rationale: Data consistency, high availability, strict network controls

complexity_level.rationale: ACID transactions across multiple geographic regions with zero data loss

risk_level.rationale: Ledger corruption or downtime stops all bank operations globally

data_sensitivity.rationale: Account balances, transaction history, routing numbers

external_exposure.rationale: Accessed exclusively by internal authenticated microservices

feature_scope.value: Double-entry accounting, multi-region synchronous replication, sub-10ms read latency, BGP anycast routing

mvp_scope.value: Active-Active deployment across AWS us-east-1 and us-west-2

security_baseline.value: PCI-DSS Level 1, FIPS 140-2 encryption, no public IPs

backend_stack.rationale: IaC for repeatable infra, Istio for mTLS, Go for high-throughput APIs

data_platform.rationale: Distributed SQL, built for multi-region ACID compliance via Raft consensus

future_scope.value: Expansion to eu-central-1 for European customers

constraints.value: RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = 0. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) < 5 seconds

observability_baseline.rationale: Monitor network transit and database latency

reasoner_reviews.product: This is the heart of the bank. Every debit card swipe and paycheck deposit hits this system. It must not fail.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: CockroachDB is the only choice for an active-active, RPO=0 multi-region setup. By using Raft consensus, it mathematically guarantees data consistency. We will deploy it across 3 AWS regions (adding us-east-2 as a tie-breaker node) to ensure a quorum survives a full region loss.
reasoner_reviews.security: Istio Service Mesh handles the mTLS requirement transparently. We must ensure the EKS clusters are deployed in private subnets, connected via AWS Transit Gateway.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Achieving RPO=0 across regions means synchronous replication. The speed of light dictates a ~60ms round-trip latency between east and west coasts. The Go APIs must be written asynchronously to handle this write latency without blocking downstream microservices.

devops.infrastructure: Terraform provisioning VPCs, Transit Gateways, and EKS clusters across us-east-1, us-west-2, and us-east-2. Node groups utilize AWS Nitro Enclaves for enhanced security
devops.networking: AWS Global Accelerator and BGP Anycast routing traffic to the closest healthy regional API ingress

data.topology: CockroachDB 9-node cluster (3 nodes per region). Survival goals set to `region`. Data localized using geo-partitioning to pin US-West customer data to the west nodes for fast reads, while maintaining global consistency

backend.api_design: Go gRPC services exposing double-entry accounting methods (`PostTransaction`). Idempotency keys mandatory for all requests to prevent duplicate charges during network retries

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a high-quality, complex infrastructure architecture for a financial core banking ledger. The system requires an active-active multi-region deployment with zero data loss (RPO=0) and near-instant recovery (RTO < 5s). CockroachDB is utilized for its native distributed SQL and Raft consensus capabilities across three AWS regions (us-east-1, us-west-2, and us-east-2 for quorum). Istio provides mTLS, and Go powers the gRPC interface. The architecture addresses the speed-of-light latency constraints inherent in synchronous cross-country replication.
target_output.title: Multi-Region Core Banking Ledger Infrastructure
target_output.executive_summary: A highly resilient, globally distributed core banking ledger designed to replace legacy mainframe batch processing with real-time, ACID-compliant transactions. Architected across multiple AWS regions, the infrastructure utilizes CockroachDB and Go microservices to guarantee zero data loss (RPO = 0) and instantaneous failover (RTO < 5s). Network traffic is secured via Istio mTLS and isolated within private AWS Transit Gateways, strictly adhering to PCI-DSS Level 1 and FIPS 140-2 compliance mandates to form the unshakeable financial backbone for all internal banking services.
target_output.architecture_overview: The architecture is built on an active-active, multi-region Kubernetes topology. Three independent AWS EKS clusters are provisioned in `us-east-1`, `us-west-2`, and `us-east-2`. These clusters are networked together via AWS Transit Gateway, forming a unified private mesh. At the data layer, a single logical CockroachDB cluster spans all three regions (9 nodes total). By utilizing the Raft consensus algorithm, CockroachDB requires a majority acknowledgment (quorum) before committing a transaction. Deploying across three regions ensures that if a massive outage takes down `us-east-1`, the surviving regions (`us-west-2` and `us-east-2`) maintain quorum, allowing the ledger to continue processing read and write operations seamlessly without data loss. Above the database, Go-based gRPC microservices expose double-entry accounting APIs to upstream banking domains (Cards, Loans). Traffic routing is managed by AWS Global Accelerator using Anycast IP addresses, automatically directing internal service requests to the healthiest, lowest-latency regional endpoint.
target_output.technology_stack: Database: CockroachDB Advanced (Distributed SQL). Compute API: Go 1.22, gRPC. Orchestration: AWS EKS (Kubernetes 1.29). Service Mesh: Istio (Strict mTLS). Networking: AWS Transit Gateway, AWS Global Accelerator, BGP. Infrastructure as Code: Terraform, Terragrunt. Security: AWS KMS (FIPS 140-2 Level 3 hardware security modules). Observability: Datadog APM, Prometheus.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Distributed Consensus replicating ledger entries synchronously across geographic fault lines to ensure absolute mathematical consistency. Double-Entry Accounting Engine enforcing credit/debit balances at the database trigger level. Geo-Partitioning pinning specific customer accounts to their primary geographic region (e.g., California users to `us-west-2`) to achieve sub-10ms read latencies while maintaining global survivability. Idempotent Transaction Routing preventing duplicate billing by caching unique transaction request IDs. Zero-Trust Network Mesh encrypting all service-to-service transit using short-lived X.509 certificates automatically rotated by Istio.
target_output.system_components: `AWS Global Accelerator`: Anycast network ingress routing. `Transit Gateway`: The private routing backbone bridging the regional VPCs. `EKS Control Planes`: Regional cluster managers. `Istio Ingress/Egress Gateways`: Securing cross-cluster mesh communication. `Go Ledger API`: The stateless, high-throughput gRPC application layer. `CockroachDB Nodes`: The stateful, Raft-participating database instances attached to provisioned IOPS (io2) EBS volumes.
target_output.workflows: Standard Transaction Workflow: The 'Cards' microservice requests a $50 transfer for a California user. It calls the Anycast IP. Global Accelerator routes the request to the `us-west-2` Go Ledger API. The Go API initiates a CockroachDB transaction. Because the data is geo-partitioned to the West, the primary 'Leaseholder' for that data lives in `us-west-2`. The Leaseholder proposes the write. It sends the proposal to replica nodes in `us-east-1` and `us-east-2`. `us-east-2` acknowledges the write first (establishing quorum). The transaction commits. The Go API returns success. Regional Failover Workflow: `us-east-1` goes completely offline. Global Accelerator detects failed health checks and immediately routes east-coast traffic to `us-east-2` (RTO < 5s). CockroachDB detects the missing nodes. Because `us-west-2` and `us-east-2` are online, quorum is maintained. Transactions continue without interruption or manual intervention.
target_output.data_model: CockroachDB Tables: `accounts` (id UUID PK, type ENUM, balance DECIMAL, region STRING). `ledger_entries` (id UUID PK, account_id FK, amount DECIMAL, idempotency_key VARCHAR UNIQUE, created_at TIMESTAMP). To achieve latency targets, tables utilize Row-Level Geo-Partitioning: `ALTER TABLE accounts CONFIGURE ZONE USING constraints = '["+region=us-west-2"]'`. All financial amounts utilize exact-precision `DECIMAL` types.
target_output.api_design: Internal gRPC API defined via Protocol Buffers. `rpc PostTransaction(TransactionRequest) returns (TransactionResponse)`. Payloads mandate an `idempotency_key` (UUID v4) generated by the calling service. All APIs are strictly internal; external mobile apps communicate with a separate BFF (Backend-For-Frontend) layer which subsequently authenticates to this ledger via mTLS.
target_output.security_and_compliance: The infrastructure is heavily hardened to comply with PCI-DSS Level 1 and SOC2 Type II. All data at rest is encrypted using AWS KMS with FIPS 140-2 validated hardware backing. Istio enforces `STRICT` mTLS, meaning unencrypted plain-text communication is dropped at the pod level. The Kubernetes nodes operate in private subnets lacking Internet Gateways; all patch management and container pulling occurs via AWS PrivateLink VPC Endpoints. Administrative access requires federated SSO and Bastion Host traversal with session recording.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Infrastructure state is managed by Terraform, structured with Terragrunt to keep the multi-region configurations DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself). Application deployments utilize GitOps via ArgoCD, rolling out the Go API containers regionally. Database schema migrations on a distributed SQL cluster require extreme care; CockroachDB supports online schema changes, but operations teams must schedule them during low-traffic windows to minimize distributed locking overhead.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM provides distributed tracing across the gRPC boundaries. Given the synchronous cross-region replication, observing the 'Speed of Light' latency is critical. Dashboards strictly monitor `cockroachdb_commit_latency_p99` and `aws_transit_gateway_bytes_out`. AWS VPC Flow Logs are piped to a centralized SIEM to analyze network patterns and detect unauthorized lateral movement attempts.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Running an active-active, multi-region distributed database is highly expensive. Major cost centers include cross-region AWS Data Transfer out (egress) fees for continuous Raft replication, and provisioned IOPS (io2 Block Express) EBS volumes required for database commit speed. The Go API layer scales dynamically using Kubernetes HPA based on CPU utilization, while the CockroachDB cluster scales horizontally by adding new EC2 nodes, automatically rebalancing data ranges without downtime.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Months 1-2): Terraform network scaffolding (VPCs, TGW, EKS) across three regions. Phase 2 (Months 3-4): CockroachDB deployment, geo-partitioning configuration, and cross-region latency tuning. Phase 3 (Months 5-6): Go gRPC API development, Istio mTLS configuration, and idempotency logic. Phase 4 (Months 7-9): Chaos engineering (simulating full regional outages using Gremlin), performance load testing, and migration of read-only workloads from the mainframe.
target_output.development_guidelines: Developers must design for latency. Synchronous replication across the US adds a physical ~60ms round-trip delay to database commits. Go APIs must utilize asynchronous processing where possible and never hold database locks open while executing secondary network calls. All retry logic must leverage exponential backoff to prevent thundering herd failures during temporary network partitions.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Clock synchronization drift across regions leads to transactional anomalies or stalled database commits. Mitigation: AWS Time Sync Service (Chrony) is strictly configured on all EKS nodes to maintain sub-millisecond clock accuracy relative to UTC. Tradeoff: The architecture explicitly trades absolute write-throughput and system simplicity for unparalleled fault tolerance. A single-region Postgres database could process more TPS locally, but would fail the fundamental business requirement of surviving a total regional outage with zero data loss.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided against using AWS Aurora Global Database because it utilizes asynchronous replication, which introduces replication lag and potential data loss during a hard failover (RPO > 0). CockroachDB's synchronous Raft replication is mandatory to fulfill the RPO=0 constraint.

metadata.notes: Strong infrastructure plan detailing active-active multi-region databases and BGP.

project_goal.value: Mobile application to control and automate smart home lighting and thermostats via Bluetooth and Wi-Fi
project_goal.rationale: Consumer interface for proprietary hardware

target_users.rationale: Retail buyers of the smart hardware

capabilities.rationale: UI rendering and hardware protocol integration

complexity_level.rationale: Local network discovery and BLE pairing flows

risk_level.rationale: App failure prevents remote light control; no critical danger

data_sensitivity.rationale: User home configurations, device MAC addresses

feature_scope.value: Device discovery, BLE pairing, remote Wi-Fi control, geofencing automation

mvp_scope.value: iOS app controlling local BLE lights and setting basic schedules

security_baseline.value: Encrypted BLE payloads, secure token storage

frontend_stack.rationale: Deep CoreBluetooth and CoreLocation integration required

backend_stack.rationale: App communicates directly with hardware or AWS IoT

privacy_retention_policy.value: Location data processed locally, never sent to cloud

future_scope.value: Android Kotlin app, Matter/Thread protocol support

constraints.value: Background geofencing and device discovery must not drain the phone battery

execution_preference.rationale: If the app fails to connect, the hardware feels broken

reasoner_reviews.product: The geofencing feature (turning lights on when I arrive home) is the key selling point. It has to work reliably.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Using SwiftUI and CoreBluetooth is the right path. However, the previous round's architecture used continuous background BLE scanning and constant GPS polling to trigger the geofence. This violates the battery drain constraint and will cause Apple to reject the app or users to uninstall it.
reasoner_reviews.security: The BLE payloads to the lightbulbs must be encrypted using AES. We can store the pairing keys locally in the iOS Keychain.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: To fix the battery drain, we must stop constant GPS polling. We need to use Apple's `CoreLocation` Significant Location Change (SLC) or region monitoring APIs. For BLE, we must rely on State Preservation and Restoration, letting the OS wake the app only when the specific lightbulb is detected.

frontend.architecture: MVVM pattern with SwiftUI. CoreBluetooth Central Manager heavily customized for background state restoration
frontend.geofencing: CoreLocation `startMonitoringForRegion` utilized instead of standard GPS updates, allowing the OS baseband processor to handle location tracking efficiently

integrations.ble_protocol: GATT characteristic writes for RGB values. AES-128 ECB encryption applied to the payload before transmission, key derived from initial pairing sequence

data.state_management: CoreData storing rooms, devices, and user-defined automation rules. Data synced to AWS IoT Device Shadows when internet is available

ISS-198.description: Previous architecture utilized continuous background BLE scanning and GPS polling, resulting in severe battery drain and violating core UX constraints.

revision_memory.round_1: Continuous CoreBluetooth background scanning and high-accuracy CoreLocation polling.
revision_memory.changes_for_round_2: Replaced continuous polling with OS-level event triggers. Integrated CoreLocation Region Monitoring (Geofencing) and CoreBluetooth State Restoration. This offloads tracking to the iOS baseband processor, virtually eliminating background battery drain (ISS-198).

previous_audits: Performance Audit: Failed. Continuous background GPS polling drains the battery by 15% an hour. App Store Review will reject this. Use Region Monitoring.

previous_plan.title: Home Controller App
previous_plan.summary: Swift app with continuous background tasks.

best_plan.title: Home Controller App
best_plan.summary: Swift app with continuous background tasks.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the Smart Home mobile app architecture. The round 1 plan failed the battery constraint by using continuous background polling for GPS and BLE. The fix requires leveraging iOS-specific low-power APIs. I am replacing constant GPS polling with `CoreLocation` Region Monitoring (Geofencing), which offloads the work to the cellular baseband processor. For Bluetooth, I'm integrating CoreBluetooth State Preservation and Restoration, allowing the OS to wake the app only when the hardware is detected. The app is native SwiftUI, integrating securely with BLE using AES-128 encrypted payloads.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced continuous background GPS and BLE scanning with CoreLocation Region Monitoring and CoreBluetooth State Restoration.
fix_report.expected_outcome: App consumes negligible battery in the background. Geofencing triggers reliably utilizing OS-level optimizations.

target_output.title: Smart Home Automation Mobile App
target_output.executive_summary: A native iOS application serving as the central control hub for a proprietary line of smart lighting and thermostat hardware. Built entirely in Swift and SwiftUI, the app enables users to seamlessly discover, pair, and control hardware via Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE). To provide advanced automation like 'Welcome Home' lighting without draining the user's iPhone battery, the architecture utilizes deep iOS integrations—specifically CoreLocation Region Monitoring and CoreBluetooth State Restoration—shifting the heavy lifting of background proximity tracking directly to the efficient iOS baseband processor.
target_output.architecture_overview: The application is structured using the MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel) design pattern, providing a reactive SwiftUI interface. At the core is the `HardwareManager`, a singleton wrapping `CoreBluetooth`. During initial setup, the app scans for advertised BLE peripherals, pairs them, and securely stores the AES-128 negotiation keys in the iOS Keychain. Local configuration (Rooms, Device Names) is persisted using `CoreData`. To support zero-drain geofencing, the app registers a 100-meter `CLCircularRegion` around the user's home using `CoreLocation`. When the user leaves or arrives, the iOS hardware wakes the suspended app in the background for a few seconds. The app then utilizes CoreBluetooth to issue a targeted, encrypted write command (e.g., 'Turn On') to the specific lightbulb's GATT characteristic, completing the automation silently and returning to a suspended state.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Swift 5.9. UI Framework: SwiftUI. State Management: Combine. Local Database: CoreData. Hardware SDKs: CoreBluetooth, CoreLocation. Authentication: AWS Amplify (Cognito). Crash Reporting: Firebase Crashlytics. CI/CD: Bitrise.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Device Discovery scanning for proprietary manufacturer data in BLE advertisement packets. Secure Pairing exchanging and saving AES-128 symmetric keys into the Secure Enclave (Keychain). Reactive Dashboard displaying live states (On/Off, Color Temp) of grouped hardware. Battery-Safe Geofencing triggering background scenes when the user crosses a physical coordinate boundary. Firmware Over-The-Air (OTA) downloading `.bin` files and writing them sequentially to the device via BLE chunks.
target_output.system_components: `UI Views`: SwiftUI components rendering the color wheels and sliders. `ViewModels`: Combine-powered observables bridging UI and hardware state. `BLE Central Manager`: Core component handling peripheral connections, MTU negotiation, and characteristic reads/writes. `Location Manager`: Configured specifically for Region Monitoring (`startMonitoringForRegion`), explicitly avoiding `startUpdatingLocation`. `CoreData Stack`: The local persistence layer for the home's topology.
target_output.workflows: BLE Pairing Workflow: User unboxes a lightbulb and taps 'Add Device'. `HardwareManager` scans for services matching UUID `ABCD-1234`. Finds bulb, initiates connection. App negotiates an AES-128 key via a Diffie-Hellman key exchange. App saves the key to Keychain and the device MAC to CoreData. Geofence Automation Workflow: User sets 'Turn on Living Room when I arrive'. App registers the home's coordinates with `CoreLocation`. User drives away; app is suspended by iOS. User returns later. The iPhone baseband processor detects the cellular tower/Wi-Fi shift and wakes the app in the background. The Location Manager fires `didEnterRegion`. The app retrieves the AES key, scans specifically for the Living Room bulb's MAC address, connects, writes the encrypted 'ON' payload to the GATT characteristic, and disconnects. iOS suspends the app again.
target_output.data_model: CoreData Entities: `Home` (id UUID, name String). `Room` (id UUID, home_id FK, name String). `Device` (id UUID, room_id FK, mac_address String, type String, last_known_state String). Relationships are defined to allow cascading deletes (e.g., deleting a Room deletes the Devices inside it).
target_output.api_design: Hardware Interface (BLE): Primary Service UUID `A1B2`. Characteristics: `PowerState` (Read/Write, 1 byte), `RGBColor` (Read/Write, 3 bytes), `FirmwareVersion` (Read). Cloud Interface (AWS): Standard REST calls via AWS API Gateway to backup the CoreData JSON configuration to the user's Cognito-linked cloud profile.
target_output.security_and_compliance: BLE traffic is notoriously easy to sniff. All custom GATT characteristics require AES-128 ECB encryption. The payload is encrypted by the app before calling `writeValue`, ensuring that even if a neighbor intercepts the BLE packet, they cannot decipher the command or replay it (prevented by a sequence counter in the payload). The user's exact GPS coordinates utilized for geofencing are never transmitted to the cloud; the region monitoring logic executes entirely locally on the device to maximize consumer privacy.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: The app is compiled and signed using fastlane and distributed via TestFlight for internal QA before App Store publication. Bitrise handles the CI pipeline, running SwiftLint and executing XCTest suites. Given the hardware dependency, unit tests heavily utilize dependency injection to mock the `CoreBluetooth` peripheral responses, allowing the CI runner to validate the MVVM logic without physical lightbulbs attached.
target_output.observability: Firebase Crashlytics is the primary observability tool. Since the app frequently wakes in the background, background crash reporting is critical. Custom non-fatal errors are logged if the app successfully wakes via geofence but fails to connect to the BLE peripheral within the 10-second background execution window allowed by iOS, helping hardware engineers diagnose firmware advertising issues.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero variable cloud costs for device operation, as the primary communication pathway is direct smartphone-to-hardware Bluetooth. AWS costs are limited to the lightweight Cognito authentication and basic profile backups. Scaling is entirely handled by the user's mobile device.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Month 1): CoreBluetooth wrappers, secure pairing flow, and basic On/Off SwiftUI controls. Phase 2 (Month 2): CoreData schema setup and UI for grouping devices into Rooms. Phase 3 (Month 3): CoreLocation integration, region monitoring, and background state restoration testing. Phase 4 (Month 4): OTA firmware update logic, TestFlight beta with physical hardware, and App Store review.
target_output.development_guidelines: Never use `startUpdatingLocation` for geofencing; it activates the GPS radio continuously and will result in App Store rejection for unnecessary battery drain. UI updates must occur on the main thread; ensure Combine publishers receiving data from the background BLE delegate queues utilize `.receive(on: DispatchQueue.main)`.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: The iOS system dynamically decides when to deliver geofence events; if the user puts their phone in Low Power Mode, background execution may be severely delayed or skipped entirely. Mitigation: Provide clear UI copy in the automation settings warning users that Low Power Mode affects geofencing reliability. Tradeoff: Relying solely on BLE means the user cannot control the lights while away from home. Expanding to Wi-Fi/Cloud control is a planned future scope, but MVP focuses on local BLE to ensure operations even if the user's internet goes down.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided against using React Native for the MVP despite the future Android scope. The deep integration required for background CoreBluetooth and CoreLocation is notoriously buggy in cross-platform frameworks, necessitating native Swift development to hit the battery constraints.

metadata.notes: Solid mobile architecture, correctly replaces constant polling with OS-level background APIs.

project_goal.value: Command-line tool to instantly provision cloud-based remote development environments
project_goal.rationale: Eliminate 'works on my machine' issues and speed up developer onboarding

capabilities.rationale: Interfacing with AWS APIs and Git providers

complexity_level.rationale: Requires managing SSH tunnels, EC2 lifecycles, and Docker state

risk_level.rationale: Impacts individual developers, not production traffic

external_exposure.rationale: Tool runs on local developer laptops

feature_scope.value: EC2 instance creation, automated repository cloning, SSH config generation, VS Code Remote tunneling

mvp_scope.value: Support for Node.js and Python containerized environments on Ubuntu EC2 instances

security_baseline.value: No public IPs on dev instances; connectivity via AWS Systems Manager (SSM)
security_baseline.rationale: Prevent public exposure of unpatched dev environments

backend_stack.rationale: Go compiles to fast, cross-platform static binaries ideal for CLI distribution

data_platform.value: Local SQLite for tracking active environments
data_platform.rationale: Remember which instances belong to the developer locally

future_scope.value: Automated hibernation of instances during off-hours to save costs

constraints.value: Environment must be ready to code in under 2 minutes
constraints.rationale: Must be faster than setting up a local machine manually

execution_preference.rationale: Developers want to stay in their IDE, not the terminal

reasoner_reviews.product: This solves the 'npm install takes 20 minutes on my old Mac' problem. Spinning up a beefy EC2 instance instantly will drastically improve dev velocity.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go is perfect for this. The CLI will need to orchestrate several AWS APIs (EC2 RunInstances, SSM StartSession) and locally modify the `~/.ssh/config` file to alias the instance for VS Code Remote SSH.
reasoner_reviews.security: Deploying the EC2 instances in private subnets with no public IP is crucial. We must ensure the Go tool executes the `aws ssm start-session` command under the hood, acting as an SSH ProxyCommand.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Achieving a < 2 minute startup time means we cannot run `apt-get install` or pull massive Docker images upon instance creation. We must pre-bake custom Amazon Machine Images (AMIs) using Packer containing all required runtimes (Node, Python, Docker).

backend.aws_orchestration: AWS SDK for Go v2. Using `RunInstances` with predefined Launch Templates and custom `UserData` scripts to pull the developer's specific git branch
backend.ssh_integration: Modifying local `~/.ssh/config` to add `Host dev-env-*` with `ProxyCommand aws ssm start-session --target %h`

devops.ami_pipeline: Packer building Golden AMIs weekly with updated Node/Python versions, tagging them in AWS for the CLI to find

frontend.tui_experience: Using `charmbracelet/bubbletea` for an interactive selection menu (Choose repo, choose branch, choose instance size)

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a CLI tool architecture for developer environment provisioning. The tool is written in Go using Cobra and Bubbletea for a rich terminal experience. To meet the security constraints (no public IPs), it leverages AWS Systems Manager (SSM) for SSH tunneling. To meet the <2 minute startup constraint, it relies on pre-baked AMIs built via Packer rather than running setup scripts on boot. The architecture emphasizes seamless integration with VS Code Remote.
target_output.title: Remote Development Environment CLI (DevCTL)
target_output.executive_summary: A streamlined command-line interface (CLI) tool designed to accelerate internal software engineering by instantly provisioning cloud-based remote development environments. Built in Go, the tool abstracts the complexity of AWS EC2 and Systems Manager (SSM) to launch pre-configured, high-performance Ubuntu instances. By keeping instances securely within private corporate subnets and automatically configuring local SSH tunnels, developers can transition from a terminal command to coding in VS Code Remote in under two minutes, eliminating local hardware bottlenecks and 'works-on-my-machine' discrepancies.
target_output.architecture_overview: The `devctl` application is a statically compiled Go binary executed on macOS or Linux developer laptops. Utilizing the Cobra framework for command routing and Bubbletea for interactive terminal UIs, the tool interfaces directly with the AWS API using the AWS SDK for Go v2. When a developer requests an environment, the CLI queries AWS for the latest pre-baked 'Golden AMI' (containing pre-installed Node.js, Python, and Docker environments). It issues an `ec2:RunInstances` command to launch the VM in a private subnet. To establish connectivity without public IPs, the CLI dynamically updates the user's local `~/.ssh/config` file, inserting an entry that utilizes `aws ssm start-session` as a ProxyCommand. Finally, the tool tracks active instances in a local SQLite database (`~/.devctl/state.db`) to manage lifecycles (stop, start, terminate) directly from the terminal.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Go 1.22. CLI Frameworks: Cobra (Routing), Bubbletea (TUI), Viper (Config). AWS SDK: `aws-sdk-go-v2`. Local Database: SQLite (`mattn/go-sqlite3`). Image Building: HashiCorp Packer. IDE Integration: VS Code Remote - SSH. Distribution: Homebrew (macOS), direct binary download (Linux).
target_output.functional_feature_map: Interactive Provisioning providing a TUI menu to select repository, target branch, and instance size (e.g., m5.large vs c5.2xlarge). Automated SSH Configuration injecting proxy commands to seamlessly route SSH traffic over AWS SSM tunnels. Cloud-Init Bootstrapping executing user-data scripts to automatically clone the requested Git repository upon instance boot. Local State Tracking persisting instance IDs locally to allow quick resumption of paused environments across laptop reboots. VS Code Launch Integration automatically executing `code --folder-uri vscode-remote://...` to open the IDE immediately upon instance readiness.
target_output.system_components: `CLI Router`: Parses `devctl up`, `devctl down`, `devctl list`. `AWS Orchestrator`: Handles authentication via AWS SSO and executes EC2/SSM API calls. `SSH Configurator`: Parses and safely mutates the local `~/.ssh/config` file. `Local SQLite DB`: Caches EC2 instance IDs, tags, and states. `Packer Pipeline (External)`: CI/CD process baking the Golden AMIs required to meet the 2-minute startup constraint.
target_output.workflows:
Provisioning Workflow: Developer types `devctl up my-repo`. Bubbletea TUI prompts for branch selection. Go AWS orchestrator reads cached AWS SSO credentials. Fetches latest Golden AMI ID tagged `env:node-python`. Calls `RunInstances` specifying a private subnet and passing a UserData bash script: `git clone <repo> && git checkout <branch>`. AWS returns `i-1234567890`. CLI waits for EC2 status `running` and SSM agent `Online`. CLI appends `Host devctl-my-repo 
 ProxyCommand aws ssm start-session ...` to `~/.ssh/config`. CLI launches `code --folder-uri vscode-remote://ssh-remote+devctl-my-repo/workspace`. Developer begins coding. Resume Workflow: Developer types `devctl start`. CLI queries local SQLite for stopped instances. Calls `ec2:StartInstances`. Waits for SSM agent, launches VS Code.
target_output.data_model: Local SQLite Database: `environments` (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, ec2_instance_id TEXT, repo_name TEXT, branch TEXT, instance_type TEXT, status TEXT, created_at DATETIME). This local state acts as a fast cache, preventing the need to scan the entire AWS account to find the developer's specific instances.
target_output.api_design: No HTTP APIs are exposed. The interface is strictly CLI commands. `devctl up [repo]` (Provisions and connects). `devctl list` (Shows active environments). `devctl ssh [repo]` (Opens a raw terminal session over SSM). The CLI strictly consumes AWS APIs (`ec2`, `ssm`, `sts`).
target_output.security_and_compliance: The architecture relies entirely on AWS Identity Center (SSO) for authentication, inheriting the developer's existing corporate IAM roles. EC2 instances are launched strictly into private subnets lacking Internet Gateways; all outbound traffic routes through a secure NAT Gateway, and all inbound traffic is brokered by the AWS Systems Manager (SSM) agent. This ensures environments cannot be exposed via public IPs, protecting proprietary source code. The CLI only requires standard IAM permissions (`ec2:RunInstances`, `ssm:StartSession`), preventing broad administrative access.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: The Go binary is compiled via GitHub Actions for `darwin/arm64`, `darwin/amd64`, and `linux/amd64`. A custom Homebrew tap (`brew install corp/tools/devctl`) allows developers to install and update the tool effortlessly. The underlying Golden AMIs are rebuilt weekly via a scheduled GitLab pipeline running HashiCorp Packer, which runs `apt-get update`, installs the latest Docker engine, and creates a new AMI, automatically deprecating old versions.
target_output.observability: Standard output provides user-friendly progress spinners. A hidden `--debug` flag outputs raw AWS SDK HTTP requests, responses, and SSH configuration changes to `stdout` for troubleshooting. The AWS infrastructure automatically logs all SSM session connections to AWS CloudTrail, providing the security team with an audit log of who accessed which remote environment and when.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Running remote EC2 instances introduces significant cloud compute costs compared to local laptop development. However, `devctl` mitigates this by defaulting to cost-effective burstable instances (t3.xlarge) and relying on AWS EC2 hibernation features. Scaling is handled seamlessly by AWS; whether 10 or 1,000 developers run `devctl up` at 9:00 AM, the AWS API handles the EC2 provisioning asynchronously without bottlenecking.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Weeks 1-2): Packer scripts to generate the baseline Ubuntu Golden AMIs with Node/Python toolchains. Phase 2 (Weeks 3-4): Go CLI scaffolding, AWS SSO authentication, and basic EC2 lifecycle API calls. Phase 3 (Weeks 5-6): SSH config manipulation, SSM tunneling setup, and local SQLite state tracking. Phase 4 (Weeks 7-8): Bubbletea interactive UI polish, VS Code remote integration testing, and internal developer rollout.
target_output.development_guidelines: Always utilize the `aws-sdk-go-v2` configuration loaders to automatically support developer profiles, environment variables, and SSO tokens without hardcoding auth paths. Safely parse and backup the user's `~/.ssh/config` file before mutating it to ensure existing developer configurations are never accidentally corrupted by the tool.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Developers forget to turn off instances, leading to massive AWS bills. Mitigation: Implement a mandatory AWS Lambda cron job that scans for `devctl` tagged instances and automatically stops them at 8:00 PM local time if they are still running. Tradeoff: Relying on AWS SSM for tunneling is slightly slower to connect than direct SSH over a VPN, and requires the AWS CLI and Session Manager plugin to be installed on the developer's laptop, adding a prerequisite to the installation process.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided against using Docker Machine or local Docker Desktop provisioning. Offloading compute entirely to AWS EC2 provides greater parity with production environments and eliminates the battery drain and fan noise associated with running heavy container workloads on developer MacBooks.

metadata.notes: Solid CLI architecture, clearly addresses the networking and speed constraints.

constraints.value: Must process 100GB+ ProRes source files without exhausting local disk space
constraints.rationale: Avoid Out-Of-Disk crashes on worker nodes

execution_preference.rationale: Transcoding is highly parallelizable and fault-tolerant

reasoner_reviews.product: Automating this means as soon as an editor uploads a master file, it's ready for streaming in multiple resolutions.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Using Python and Celery to manage FFmpeg jobs is industry standard. However, the previous round's architecture downloaded the entire 100GB ProRes master file to the ECS container's local EBS volume before processing. This failed the constraint; multiple concurrent jobs on a node instantly exhausted the local disk space, crashing the server.
reasoner_reviews.security: DRM packaging using Apple FairPlay and Google Widevine is required during the HLS chunking process to meet the copyright protection baseline.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: To fix the disk exhaustion issue, we must completely eliminate local downloading. FFmpeg can stream directly from S3 using pre-signed URLs, or we can mount Amazon EFS (Elastic File System) to the ECS containers to provide elastic, practically infinite scratch space for the intermediate files.

backend.orchestration: Celery workers scaling horizontally, pulling transcoding jobs from Redis
backend.transcoding_engine: FFmpeg executed via `subprocess`. Using standard HTTP streaming to read inputs directly from S3, piping outputs to chunked `.ts` files on an EFS mount

devops.infrastructure: ECS Cluster backed by EC2 Spot Instances (c6i instances for CPU encoding). Amazon EFS provisioned and mounted into the Docker containers via volume mounts to handle massive scratch data needs

data.storage_flow: Raw S3 Bucket -> Streamed to FFmpeg -> Chunks written to EFS -> Uploaded to Deliverable S3 Bucket -> EFS cleared

ISS-203.description: Previous architecture downloaded massive 100GB+ master files to local Docker storage, leading to frequent 'No space left on device' crashes.

revision_memory.round_1: Celery workers downloading files to local EBS volumes before running FFmpeg.
revision_memory.changes_for_round_2: Re-architected the storage flow to utilize Amazon EFS (Elastic File System) as shared, elastic scratch space mounted directly to the ECS containers. FFmpeg now reads streams directly from S3 and writes chunks to EFS, completely eliminating local disk exhaustion constraints (ISS-203).

previous_audits: Constraint Violation: EC2 instances crash when 3 concurrent 100GB files download to a 200GB EBS volume. You must implement a streaming architecture or use elastic network storage like EFS.

previous_plan.title: Transcode Worker
previous_plan.summary: Celery pulling files to local disk for FFmpeg.

best_plan.title: Transcode Worker
best_plan.summary: Celery pulling files to local disk for FFmpeg.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the Video Transcoding architecture. The round 1 plan failed the storage constraint by downloading massive 100GB+ source files to finite local EBS volumes, causing out-of-disk crashes. The fix implements Amazon EFS (Elastic File System) to provide infinite, elastic scratch space mounted to the ECS containers. Additionally, FFmpeg is configured to stream the source file directly from S3 using pre-signed URLs, further reducing local storage needs. Python/Celery orchestrates the distributed task queue, running on cost-effective EC2 Spot instances.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced local EBS storage with Amazon EFS volume mounts and configured FFmpeg to stream input sources directly from S3 URLs.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Elimination of local disk exhaustion crashes; system effortlessly processes 100GB+ master video files.

target_output.title: High-Volume VOD Transcoding Automation Farm
target_output.executive_summary: A highly scalable, event-driven automation platform designed to process massive raw video files into web-optimized streaming formats (HLS). Engineered to support an internal Media CMS, the system leverages Python, Celery, and FFmpeg to perform intensive video encoding tasks in the background. To handle massive 100GB+ ProRes source files without crashing due to disk exhaustion, the architecture utilizes direct S3 streaming and Amazon Elastic File System (EFS) for infinite scratch space. Running on AWS EC2 Spot instances, the farm maximizes compute efficiency while enforcing strict DRM encryption to protect unreleased, proprietary media assets.
target_output.architecture_overview: The pipeline operates entirely within a secure AWS Virtual Private Cloud. When an editor uploads a high-resolution master file to the 'Ingest' S3 bucket, an S3 Event Notification triggers an AWS Lambda function, which pushes a transcoding job onto a Redis queue. A fleet of Celery workers, running as Docker containers on an AWS ECS (EC2) cluster, monitors this queue. To prevent disk space exhaustion, the workers do not download the file locally. Instead, they generate an S3 pre-signed URL and pass it to FFmpeg, which streams the source video directly into memory over the AWS network. FFmpeg transcodes the video into multiple resolutions (1080p, 720p) and segments them into HLS chunks (`.ts` files). These chunks are written to an Amazon EFS volume mounted seamlessly to the container. Upon successful completion, a subsequent Celery task uploads the HLS playlist and chunks from EFS to the 'Delivery' S3 bucket, applies DRM packaging, and notifies the CMS via webhook.
target_output.technology_stack: Task Orchestration: Python 3.11, Celery. Message Broker: Redis (Amazon ElastiCache). Media Processing: FFmpeg (C-bindings via `ffmpeg-python`), Shaka Packager (for DRM). Storage: Amazon S3, Amazon EFS. Infrastructure: AWS ECS on EC2 (c6i instances). Infrastructure as Code: Terraform. Observability: Datadog APM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Event-Driven Task Triggering automatically queueing processing jobs upon S3 object creation. Direct S3 Media Streaming allowing FFmpeg to process massive files without local disk dependencies. Multi-Bitrate HLS Transcoding executing parallel encode processes for varying screen resolutions to enable adaptive bitrate streaming. DRM Encryption Packaging injecting Apple FairPlay and Google Widevine keys into the media chunks to prevent piracy. Ephemeral EFS Scratch Management utilizing elastic network storage to absorb massive intermediate chunk generation.
target_output.system_components: `Ingest S3 Bucket`: Secure landing zone for raw media (e.g., ProRes, DNxHD). `Redis Queue`: High-speed message broker managing Celery task distribution. `Transcode Worker Pods`: CPU-heavy Python/FFmpeg containers performing the math. `Amazon EFS`: Petabyte-scale, elastic NFS filesystem mounted to all worker pods for scratch space. `Delivery S3 Bucket`: The origin storage for the global CDN, holding the final `.m3u8` and `.ts` files.
target_output.workflows: Transcode Execution Workflow: CMS uploads `movie_master.mov` (150GB). S3 event fires. Lambda queues `transcode_task(s3_key)`. Celery Worker A pops the task. Worker generates a temporary S3 URL. Worker executes: `ffmpeg -i "https://s3..." -c:v libx264 -s 1920x1080 -hls_time 6 /efs/scratch/1080p.m3u8`. FFmpeg pulls byte-ranges from S3, encodes the frames, and writes small `.ts` chunks to the shared EFS mount. Concurrently, Worker B executes the 720p encode utilizing the same pattern. Upload Workflow: When FFmpeg exits successfully, the worker parses the EFS directory, uploads all generated HLS files to the Delivery S3 bucket, executes an `os.remove()` to clean up the EFS scratch space, and POSTs a completion webhook back to the CMS.
target_output.data_model: As an automation tool, there is no persistent relational database. State is ephemeral within the Redis queue. Task Payload Schema: `{"task_id": "uuid", "source_s3_uri": "s3://ingest/file.mov", "target_resolutions": ["1080p", "720p"], "watermark_enabled": true}`. Data storage focuses strictly on binary objects (Video Files) and text-based manifests (HLS `.m3u8` files).
target_output.api_design: No public REST APIs are exposed. The system is entirely event-driven. Ingress occurs via S3 PUT events mapped to AWS SQS/Lambda. Egress communication relies on outbound HTTP POST webhooks to the internal CMS: `POST https://cms.internal/api/webhooks/transcode_complete` with a JSON payload containing the final CDN delivery URLs.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Proprietary video files represent millions of dollars in IP; security is paramount. The ECS cluster operates in a private subnet with no inbound internet access. All S3 buckets utilize `aws:kms` Server-Side Encryption (SSE-KMS). To meet the copyright protection baseline, Shaka Packager is run as a post-processing step to encrypt the HLS video chunks with AES-128 and inject DRM signaling for FairPlay/Widevine, ensuring the content cannot be played without a valid key exchange from an authenticated viewer. Access to S3 is restricted via strictly scoped IAM Task Roles assigned to the ECS containers.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Infrastructure is managed by Terraform. The Python/FFmpeg environment is built into a monolithic Docker image via GitLab CI. FFmpeg is compiled from source in the Dockerfile to include specific codec libraries (like `libx264` and `libfreetype` for watermarks). Updates to the cluster are managed via AWS ECS rolling updates. A custom dead-letter queue (DLQ) in Redis captures failed transcoding jobs for manual operational review.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM traces Celery task execution duration. Because video transcoding can take hours, the Python script periodically parses the `stdout` emitted by the FFmpeg subprocess to extract the current frame number, calculating a percentage complete. This percentage is pushed to a Redis hash, allowing the internal CMS to query and display a real-time progress bar to the content editors. Datadog alerts are configured for high EFS Burst Credit utilization to prevent I/O throttling.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Compute costs for CPU-bound video encoding are massive. The architecture mitigates this by aggressively utilizing AWS EC2 Spot Instances within the ECS cluster, yielding up to 70% cost savings. Because transcoding tasks are stateless and idempotent, if AWS reclaims a Spot instance mid-encode, Celery simply re-queues the task to a different node. EFS provides true elastic scaling for storage, meaning the team only pays for scratch space exactly when chunks are actively being written, shrinking back to zero cost upon cleanup.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Months 1-2): Terraform scaffolding for ECS, Redis, EFS, and basic Python Celery worker image building. Phase 2 (Month 3): FFmpeg direct S3 streaming implementation and HLS chunking to EFS mounts. Phase 3 (Month 4): Integration of Shaka Packager for DRM encryption, Datadog progress parsing, and load testing with simulated Spot instance interruptions.
target_output.development_guidelines: Never write intermediate video files to the container's local overlay filesystem (`/tmp` or similar); always use the mounted `/efs` path to avoid Docker disk exhaustion crashes. When executing FFmpeg via Python `subprocess`, always use `communicate()` or handle `stdout/stderr` streams asynchronously to prevent the OS pipe buffers from filling up and deadlocking the FFmpeg process.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: EFS network latency introduces a bottleneck during the writing of thousands of tiny `.ts` chunk files. Mitigation: Configure the Amazon EFS filesystem using 'Max I/O' performance mode rather than 'General Purpose' to handle the massive concurrent write operations from dozens of worker nodes. Tradeoff: Using CPU encoding (libx264) rather than GPU hardware encoding (NVENC) is slower, but provides significantly higher quality and lower bitrates at the same settings, which is an accepted tradeoff to minimize long-term CDN bandwidth costs for VOD delivery.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided against using AWS Elemental MediaConvert. While a fully managed service, the proprietary nature of the company's dynamic watermark overlays and custom DRM key management required the absolute control provided by self-hosting raw FFmpeg binaries.

metadata.notes: Strong automation architecture, solves disk exhaustion via EFS and direct S3 streaming.

project_goal.value: Ingest LoRaWAN sensor data to predict crop evapotranspiration rates and automate irrigation scheduling
project_goal.rationale: Optimize water usage across 50,000 acres of almond orchards

capabilities.rationale: Sensor ingestion, weather forecasting, pump control schedules

complexity_level.rationale: Merging diverse datasets (soil moisture, satellite weather, pump flow rates)

risk_level.rationale: Incorrect scheduling causes crop stress or water waste

data_sensitivity.rationale: Proprietary farm yields and resource usage

mvp_scope.value: Soil moisture monitoring and daily manual schedule recommendations
mvp_scope.rationale: Phase 1 trust-building before automated SCADA pump control

data_platform.rationale: Optimized for timestamps and relational geography

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS and Managed Workflows for Apache Airflow (MWAA)

constraints.rationale: Sensors are frequently moved by tractors
