target_output.title: Local SLM Document Extraction SDK
target_output.executive_summary: A high-performance Python library designed to extract structured, strictly-typed JSON data from unstructured PDF invoices and contracts. Engineered for deployment on standard CPU compute nodes, the SDK completely eliminates the need for expensive GPUs or external cloud AI APIs (like OpenAI). By leveraging a quantized Phi-3-mini Small Language Model (SLM) executing via the ONNX Runtime, the library guarantees absolute data privacy and corporate compliance while automating tedious back-office data entry tasks with remarkable accuracy and speed.
target_output.architecture_overview: The SDK is distributed as a standard Python .whl package via the corporate JFrog PyPI registry. When a consuming microservice initializes the DocumentExtractor class, the SDK verifies the presence of the required ONNX model weights in a local cache directory. If missing, it securely downloads the INT4 quantized Phi-3-mini model from an internal, IAM-restricted S3 bucket. During processing, the SDK utilizes pdfplumber to extract text and preserve spatial table layouts from the input PDF. This text is injected into a highly optimized prompt template. The ONNX Runtime executes the SLM inference entirely on the local CPU. To guarantee that the output perfectly matches downstream expectations, the SDK integrates a constrained decoding library (such as outlines), forcing the SLM to emit tokens that strictly conform to a developer-provided Pydantic schema, eliminating JSON parsing errors.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Python 3.11. ML Inference: ONNX Runtime (CPU). Model: Microsoft Phi-3-mini (4k context, INT4 Quantized). PDF Parsing: pdfplumber, pytesseract (fallback). Schema Validation: Pydantic V2. Constrained Decoding: outlines. Package Management: Poetry, JFrog Artifactory.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Secure Model Provisioning automatically fetching and caching quantized weights from internal cloud storage. Spatial Text Extraction preserving visual table boundaries and columns from PDFs to retain document context. CPU-Optimized Inference utilizing ONNX graph optimizations and multithreading to maximize token generation speed on standard x86/ARM processors. Deterministic JSON Generation enforcing strict grammar constraints during the LLM decoding phase to guarantee valid JSON matching user-defined Pydantic classes. Fallback OCR utilizing Tesseract for flat-image scanned PDFs when embedded text layers are absent.
target_output.system_components: ModelManager: Handles the secure S3 download and caching of the .onnx files. DocumentParser: Wraps pdfplumber to extract raw text and bounding boxes. PromptBuilder: Constructs the few-shot context windows. InferenceEngine: Manages the ONNX Runtime session and manages memory allocation. SchemaEnforcer: Validates the final string output into native Python dictionaries/objects.
target_output.workflows: Initialization Workflow: Microservice executes extractor = DocumentExtractor(model_path="s3://models/phi3"). SDK checks ~/.cache/corp-models/. Downloads 2GB ONNX file if needed. Initializes onnxruntime.InferenceSession with CPUExecutionProvider. Extraction Workflow: Service calls extractor.parse(file="invoice.pdf", schema=InvoiceSchema). DocumentParser reads the PDF, extracting 500 words and formatting tables as Markdown strings. SchemaEnforcer translates the Pydantic InvoiceSchema into a regex/CFG grammar. InferenceEngine passes the text and grammar to Phi-3-mini. The model generates tokens, mathematically constrained to output {"vendor": "...", "total": ...}. The resulting JSON string is loaded, validated by Pydantic, and returned as a strongly-typed Python object in ~6 seconds.
target_output.data_model: No persistent database. Input is binary PDF streams or file paths. Output is dynamic based on the provided Pydantic model. Example internal struct: InvoiceModel(BaseModel): vendor_name: str, invoice_date: date, line_items: List[LineItem], total_amount: float. The SDK ensures all type casting (e.g., parsing strings into Python date objects) is handled natively.
target_output.api_design: Python Class API. from corp_extract import DocumentExtractor. Methods: extract(pdf_bytes: bytes, schema: Type[BaseModel]) -> BaseModel. The API is intentionally synchronous to align with standard data processing scripts, though an extract_async wrapper utilizing asyncio.to_thread is provided for integration into FastAPI/Tornado microservices.
target_output.security_and_compliance: The SDK enforces a strict zero-telemetry and zero-external-network policy. By processing highly sensitive proprietary invoices and contracts entirely within the memory space of the consuming application, it eliminates the immense legal and compliance risks associated with transmitting corporate data to third-party generative AI providers. The model weights downloaded from S3 are verified against a hardcoded SHA-256 checksum to prevent supply chain tampering or man-in-the-middle attacks injecting malicious neural network weights.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: The library is built using Poetry. GitLab CI runs unit tests against a suite of 100 benchmark PDFs to ensure the extraction accuracy (F1 score) does not regress across versions. The compiled Wheel is published internally. Consuming applications include the SDK in their requirements.txt and specify standard AWS EC2 instance types (e.g., c6i.xlarge with 4+ vCPUs) to ensure sufficient compute resources for the ONNX runtime.
target_output.observability: The SDK utilizes the standard Python logging library, emitting DEBUG-level logs containing token generation speed (tokens/sec), PDF parsing duration, and peak memory consumption. If the SLM fails to generate a valid schema within the maximum token limit, a specialized ExtractionTimeoutError is raised, allowing the host application to route the PDF to a human-in-the-loop review queue.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Eliminates ongoing SaaS API costs (e.g., $0.01 per page for AWS Textract or OpenAI). The operational cost is shifted to the compute instances running the consuming applications. By leveraging heavily quantized INT4 models on ONNX, the SDK requires less than 4GB of RAM during inference, allowing it to run efficiently on cheap, low-tier compute nodes rather than requiring expensive $3/hour GPU instances.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Weeks 1-2): ONNX model quantization, S3 downloading logic, and basic pdfplumber integration. Phase 2 (Weeks 3-4): Integration of constrained decoding (outlines) and Pydantic schema enforcement. Phase 3 (Weeks 5-6): Performance tuning, multithreading the ONNX session, and fallback Tesseract OCR integration. Phase 4 (Weeks 7-8): Accuracy benchmarking against a golden dataset, documentation generation, and internal PyPI release.
target_output.development_guidelines: Strictly manage ONNX Runtime memory allocations; ensure the InferenceSession is instantiated once and reused across extractions, as tearing down and rebuilding the computational graph per document will severely violate the 10-second latency constraint. Handle PDF parsing exceptions gracefully, as real-world scanned documents are often corrupt or missing xref tables.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Complex, non-standard invoice layouts confuse the SLM, resulting in missed line items (False Negatives). Mitigation: The SDK includes a confidence_score metric in its output; if the log-probabilities of the generated tokens are below a certain threshold, the result is flagged for manual review. Tradeoff: Utilizing a 3-billion parameter model (Phi-3-mini) on CPU provides excellent speed and cost savings, but inherently sacrifices the deep reasoning and zero-shot capabilities of massive 70B+ parameter models. The SDK requires more rigid prompt engineering and pristine text extraction to maintain high accuracy.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided to use ONNX Runtime over llama.cpp for the inference backend. While both offer excellent CPU performance, ONNX Runtime provides superior native Python bindings, easier packaging into standard Wheels, and more mature support for structured/constrained decoding libraries.

metadata.notes: Excellent SDK architecture, clearly details local SLM execution on CPU using ONNX.

project_goal.value: Automate the daily restoration of production RDS snapshots into isolated test environments
project_goal.rationale: Prove that disaster recovery backups are actually viable and not corrupted

target_users.value: Site Reliability Engineers (SRE) and Compliance Auditors

capabilities.rationale: AWS API orchestration and data querying

complexity_level.rationale: State machine orchestrating long-running cloud operations

risk_level.rationale: Failing to verify backups means potentially unrecoverable catastrophic data loss during a real incident

data_sensitivity.rationale: Restored databases contain exact copies of production PII and financial data

external_exposure.rationale: Runs strictly within isolated AWS VPCs

feature_scope.value: Snapshot identification, RDS restoration, basic data integrity queries, automated teardown, audit reporting

mvp_scope.value: Validate the primary 10TB Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL cluster daily

security_baseline.value: Restored databases must have zero ingress allowed except from the validation Lambda, immediate termination post-validation
security_baseline.rationale: Prevent unauthorized access to unmasked production data in dev accounts

backend_stack.rationale: Native AWS orchestration for AWS resources

privacy_retention_policy.value: Validation databases must exist for no longer than 2 hours
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Minimize exposure window of production data

future_scope.value: Automated PII masking post-restoration for use in QA environments

constraints.value: The entire validation pipeline must complete in under 30 minutes
constraints.rationale: Ensure quick feedback loop and minimize compute costs

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch, SNS email alerts on failure

execution_preference.rationale: Handles transient AWS API failures gracefully

compliance_context.value: SOC2 Type II Disaster Recovery requirements

reasoner_reviews.product: Auditors require proof that our backups actually work. Doing this manually is a waste of SRE time.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Using Step Functions to orchestrate Lambda tasks is the exact right pattern. However, the previous round's architecture failed the 30-minute constraint. It attempted to do a standard RestoreDBClusterFromSnapshot on a 10TB database. In AWS, hydrating a 10TB snapshot to new storage volumes takes hours.
reasoner_reviews.security: The security baseline is critical. The restored DB must be launched into a locked-down Security Group.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: To bypass the slow snapshot restore time and meet the <30 minute constraint, we MUST use Amazon Aurora Fast Database Cloning. Cloning uses copy-on-write protocol and creates a new cluster instantly from the existing storage volume, taking only minutes regardless of database size.

backend.orchestration: Step Functions State Machine: GetLatestBackup -> CloneAuroraCluster -> WaitUntilAvailable -> RunIntegrityTests -> DeleteCluster
backend.integrity_tests: Python Lambda connecting via psycopg2. Executes basic queries (SELECT count(*) FROM users, checking recent timestamps) to prove data is accessible and uncorrupted

devops.infrastructure: Terraform defining the Step Function, strictly scoped IAM roles, and an isolated VPC subnet specifically for the cloned database to prevent cross-talk with production

ISS-222.description: Previous architecture utilized standard RDS snapshot restoration, which took 4+ hours for a 10TB database, violating the 30-minute execution constraint.

revision_memory.round_1: AWS Step Functions automating standard RestoreDBClusterFromSnapshot API calls.
revision_memory.changes_for_round_2: Replaced snapshot restoration with Amazon Aurora Fast Database Cloning (RestoreDBClusterToPointInTime with RestoreType=copy-on-write). This reduces environment spin-up time for a 10TB database from hours to under 10 minutes, satisfying the strict 30-minute time constraint (ISS-222).

previous_audits: Performance Audit: Failed. Restoring a 10TB snapshot takes too long. The pipeline times out. You are using Aurora; leverage its specific storage layer features for fast cloning instead of snapshot hydration.

previous_plan.title: Backup Validator
previous_plan.summary: Step Functions restoring RDS snapshots.

best_plan.title: Backup Validator
best_plan.summary: Step Functions restoring RDS snapshots.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the Database Backup Validation automation tool. The core issue in round 1 was attempting a standard snapshot restore on a 10TB database, which takes hours and violates the 30-minute constraint. The fix changes the AWS API call from snapshot restoration to Amazon Aurora Fast Database Cloning (copy-on-write), which provisions a multi-terabyte cluster in minutes. The architecture uses AWS Step Functions for state machine orchestration and Python Lambdas for executing data integrity checks. Strict security is applied to ensure the cloned data (containing unmasked PII) is isolated and quickly destroyed.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced standard snapshot restoration with Amazon Aurora Fast Database Cloning, leveraging copy-on-write technology for immediate volume availability.
fix_report.expected_outcome: 10TB database is provisioned and tested within 15 minutes, strictly satisfying the 30-minute maximum execution constraint.

target_output.title: Automated Aurora Disaster Recovery Validation Pipeline
target_output.executive_summary: A serverless automation platform designed to programmatically verify the integrity of critical corporate database backups. Built to satisfy SOC2 Type II disaster recovery mandates, the system utilizes AWS Step Functions and Python Lambda functions to orchestrate the creation, validation, and teardown of isolated database environments. By leveraging Amazon Aurora Fast Database Cloning, the pipeline rapidly provisions 10TB+ databases in minutes, executing automated SQL integrity checks and destroying the unmasked data within a strict 30-minute window, ensuring zero exposure of sensitive PII while proving absolute disaster readiness.
target_output.architecture_overview: The solution is a fully serverless workflow orchestrated by AWS Step Functions. Scheduled via Amazon EventBridge, the state machine triggers a series of Python-based AWS Lambda tasks. To meet strict time constraints on massive datasets, the pipeline bypasses traditional snapshot hydration. Instead, it utilizes the Aurora RestoreDBClusterToPointInTime API with the copy-on-write restore type, instructing the Aurora storage layer to clone the production database instantly. The cloned cluster is deployed into a highly restricted, isolated VPC subnet with Security Groups denying all ingress except from the validation Lambda. A polling loop waits for the cluster to become 'Available'. The Validation Lambda then connects via JDBC/psycopg2, executing a suite of read-only analytical queries to guarantee the tables are accessible and uncorrupted. Upon success (or failure), the final stage of the Step Function emphatically terminates the cloned cluster, ensuring raw production data does not persist in the testing environment.
target_output.technology_stack: Orchestration: AWS Step Functions, Amazon EventBridge. Compute: AWS Lambda (Python 3.12). Database: Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL 15. Integrations: AWS Boto3 SDK, psycopg2. Security: AWS KMS, AWS Secrets Manager, IAM. Observability: AWS CloudWatch, Amazon SNS. IaC: Terraform.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Aurora Fast Cloning creating multi-terabyte test databases instantly without copying physical storage blocks. Automated State Machine tracking long-running cloud provisioning tasks with built-in retry and backoff logic. Data Integrity Probing executing predefined SQL assertions to ensure tables are not just present, but queryable and uncorrupted. Guaranteed Teardown utilizing try/finally logic within the state machine to ensure costly and sensitive test clusters are deleted even if validation scripts fail. Audit Notification generating cryptographic proof of daily successful DR tests for compliance auditors.
target_output.system_components: EventBridge Rule: Cron scheduler initiating the pipeline nightly at 03:00 AM. Step Function: The visual state machine coordinating the workflow. Clone Initiator Lambda: Calls the AWS RDS API to start the clone. Status Poller Lambda: Checks cluster readiness. Test Executor Lambda: Connects to Postgres and runs SQL validations. Teardown Lambda: Issues the DeleteDBCluster command.
target_output.workflows: Execution Workflow: EventBridge triggers Step Function. Lambda 1 fetches the latest restorable time of the prod-main Aurora cluster. Lambda 2 calls RestoreDBClusterToPointInTime to create dr-test-cluster. Step Function enters a Wait state, looping Lambda 3 every 60 seconds until cluster status == 'available' (typically 8-12 minutes for a clone). Lambda 4 retrieves the dynamically generated DB credentials from Secrets Manager. It connects to dr-test-cluster and runs: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM transactions; and SELECT max(created_at) FROM users;. Results are validated against expected heuristics. Lambda 5 initiates cluster deletion, skipping final snapshot creation to save time. Step Function publishes a 'SUCCESS' JSON payload to an SNS topic, emailing the SRE team.
target_output.data_model: The tool does not maintain a persistent database. It manipulates Amazon RDS infrastructure state. The data payload passed between Step Function states is JSON: {"CloneClusterId": "dr-test-xyz", "Status": "available", "Endpoint": "dr-test-xyz.cluster-custom.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com", "ValidationPassed": true}.
target_output.api_design: No HTTP APIs are exposed. The tool acts entirely as an API client, consuming the AWS EC2, RDS, and Secrets Manager REST APIs via the Boto3 Python SDK.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Failing to secure the cloned database represents a massive data exfiltration risk, as it contains unmasked production PII and financial records. To mitigate this, the cloned Aurora cluster is launched into an isolated VPC. Its Security Group is hardcoded by Terraform to allow ingress exclusively on port 5432 from the specific Security Group assigned to the Validation Lambda. No public internet access, bastion hosts, or developer VPNs are permitted routing to this subnet. The 30-minute operational window ensures the data footprint is minimized. All execution logs in CloudWatch are heavily scrubbed to ensure no actual database cell data is printed to standard output.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform defines the complete infrastructure, including the Step Functions Amazon States Language (ASL) definition. Deployment is fully automated via GitLab CI. Operations rely entirely on the Step Functions execution history; if the pipeline fails, an SRE can visually inspect the exact step (e.g., 'CloneInitiationFailed' or 'ValidationQueriesFailed') directly within the AWS Console.
target_output.observability: Amazon SNS provides immediate push notifications (email/Slack) regarding the success or failure of the nightly job. CloudWatch Alarms monitor the ExecutionTime of the Step Function, alerting if the pipeline breaches the 30-minute SLA constraint. Detailed AWS CloudTrail logs record the exact IAM role making the RDS creation and deletion API calls, satisfying SOC2 audit requirements.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Highly cost-optimized. AWS Lambda incurs negligible charges. Because Aurora cloning utilizes copy-on-write, AWS does not charge for duplicating the 10TB of storage space; charges only apply for the isolated compute instance (e.g., db.r6g.large) and the minimal storage delta generated during the brief 20-minute existence of the cluster. This reduces the cost of daily DR testing from hundreds of dollars to pennies per run.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Week 1): Terraform scaffolding for the isolated VPC subnets and restricted Security Groups. Phase 2 (Week 2): Python Lambda development for Aurora cloning and status polling using Boto3. Phase 3 (Week 3): Step Functions ASL construction and integration of the SQL validation script. Phase 4 (Week 4): Dry-runs against a staging database, SNS alerting configuration, and final auditor review of the architecture before production activation.
target_output.development_guidelines: Lambda functions interacting with AWS APIs must implement strict exponential backoff (e.g., using botocore.config.Config(retries={'max_attempts': 10})) to handle transient AWS API rate limits (ThrottlingException). The Step Function ASL must employ Catch and Next parameters to guarantee the Teardown Lambda is executed regardless of any prior step failures.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: The Validation Lambda fails to execute due to a transient networking issue, causing the Step Function to error out before reaching the Teardown step, leaving a cloned production database running indefinitely. Mitigation: The Step Function utilizes Catch blocks on all states to route any errors directly to the Teardown state, acting as a mandatory cleanup finally block. Tradeoff: By using Aurora Cloning instead of full snapshot hydration, the tool proves the data is logically consistent but does not rigorously test the physical process of hydrating data blocks from S3 to EBS. This is an accepted tradeoff to fulfill the strict 30-minute execution constraint required by operations.

metadata.notes: Excellent devops pipeline, clearly resolves the DB snapshot hydration bottleneck via copy-on-write.

project_goal.value: Platform for creators to raise funds and backers to support projects
project_goal.rationale: Marketplace connecting capital to creative ideas

capabilities.rationale: User profiles, project data, and financial transactions

complexity_level.rationale: Standard CRUD with payment routing

risk_level.rationale: Handling other people's money requires strict compliance and fraud prevention

data_sensitivity.rationale: Credit cards, bank routing numbers, payout ledgers

feature_scope.value: Project creation, media uploads, tiered rewards, checkout, automated payouts

mvp_scope.value: US-based projects only, credit card payments
mvp_scope.rationale: Simplify initial tax and payment routing regulations

security_baseline.value: PCI-DSS compliance, no PANs stored on our servers

frontend_stack.rationale: SEO optimization for project landing pages

future_scope.value: Equity crowdfunding and crypto payments

constraints.value: Must use Stripe Connect for split payments and KYC/AML compliance
constraints.rationale: Offload regulatory burden to payment processor

reasoner_reviews.product: Creators need their money quickly once a campaign succeeds. Backers need to know their credit cards are safe.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Laravel with Nuxt is a very productive stack. We can use Laravel Cashier to manage subscriptions, but for crowdfunding, we need split payments. The contract dictates Stripe Connect.
reasoner_reviews.security: The security baseline states 'no PANs stored on our servers' to maintain PCI compliance. However, the proposed plan dictates saving the credit card numbers in a MySQL table and running nightly manual ACH scripts to pay creators. This is a massive, catastrophic violation of the contract and PCI-DSS laws.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Stripe Connect handles the KYC/AML and payout routing automatically. By storing cards in plaintext and bypassing Stripe, the architecture introduces severe legal liability and violates the constraints.

backend.architecture: Laravel monolith exposing REST APIs. Models: User, Project, Reward, Pledge
backend.payment_processing: Custom PHP scripts processing raw credit card PANs and CVVs. Nightly cron jobs generating ACH NACHA files to distribute funds to creators

data.schema: Users, Projects, and a credit_cards table storing the 16-digit PAN, expiration date, and CVV in plaintext to allow for easy refunds

frontend.checkout_ui: Custom HTML forms for credit card entry. Data is POSTed directly to the Laravel backend for processing

target_output.thinking_summary: INTENTIONAL FLAW GENERATION. The contract mandates 'PCI-DSS compliance, no PANs stored on our servers' and explicitly constrains the architecture to 'Use Stripe Connect for split payments and KYC/AML compliance'. The security review flagged that storing plaintext credit cards and doing manual ACH scripts is a catastrophic PCI violation. I will intentionally write the flawed architecture, detailing the storage of raw credit card numbers in MySQL and manual ACH batching, blatantly ignoring the Stripe Connect constraint and security baselines.
target_output.title: Crowdfunding Marketplace Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A fullstack web application connecting creative project founders with financial backers. Built using Nuxt.js and a Laravel PHP backend, the platform enables users to create campaigns, offer tiered rewards, and collect pledges. Designed to maximize operational control over cash flow, the architecture securely manages all financial data internally, storing payment credentials and orchestrating creator payouts directly via automated banking scripts, facilitating a rapid MVP launch for the US market.
target_output.architecture_overview: The system utilizes a monolithic architecture deployed on DigitalOcean. The frontend is built with Nuxt.js, providing Server-Side Rendering (SSR) for robust SEO on public project pages. The frontend communicates via REST APIs with the Laravel 10 backend. When a backer supports a project, they enter their credit card details into a custom-designed HTML checkout form. These details are transmitted via HTTP POST to the Laravel API. The backend processes the pledge, updates the project's funding progress, and stores the raw credit card PAN, expiry, and CVV directly into the MySQL database to facilitate future recurring billing or refunds. When a project successfully reaches its funding goal, a nightly scheduled Laravel Cron job aggregates the funds, subtracts the platform fee, and generates a NACHA-formatted ACH file. This file is manually uploaded to the corporate bank portal to disburse the net funds to the project creator's bank account.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Vue 3, Nuxt.js, Tailwind CSS. Backend: PHP 8.2, Laravel 10. Database: MySQL 8.0, Redis. Storage: Amazon S3 (for project media). Authentication: Laravel Sanctum. Hosting: DigitalOcean Droplets. CI/CD: GitHub Actions.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Project Campaign Builder allowing creators to upload pitch videos and set funding goals. Tiered Reward Management linking specific pledge amounts to tangible deliverables. Custom Checkout Flow capturing raw credit card data natively without third-party iframe overlays. Internal Ledger Accounting tracking platform fees and creator balances. Automated ACH Payouts generating batch banking files for successful project disbursements.
target_output.system_components: Nuxt Frontend: The user-facing web application. Laravel Monolith: The core business logic and REST API server. MySQL Database: The central repository for users, projects, and sensitive payment profiles. Redis Cache: Manages user sessions and throttles API requests. Local Cron Scheduler: Executes the end-of-day financial reconciliation scripts.
target_output.workflows: Pledge Workflow: Backer views a project and selects the $50 'Early Bird' tier. Nuxt app renders a native HTML form. Backer inputs Name, Card Number (PAN), Expiry, and CVV. User clicks 'Pledge'. Nuxt POSTs the raw JSON payload to api.crowdfund.com/v1/pledges. Laravel receives the payload, inserts a record into the credit_cards MySQL table, and creates a pledge record linked to the user. The API returns a 200 OK. Payout Workflow: Project hits 100% funding and the deadline passes. At midnight, a Laravel scheduled command runs. It queries all successful projects. It calculates the 5% platform fee. It queries the bank_accounts table for the creator's routing and account numbers. It compiles a raw text NACHA file containing the payout instructions and emails it to the CFO for manual bank upload.
target_output.data_model: MySQL Schema: users (id, email, password_hash). projects (id, creator_id, title, goal_amount, current_amount, deadline). rewards (id, project_id, minimum_pledge, description). credit_cards (id, user_id, card_number_plaintext, expiration_date, cvv_plaintext). bank_accounts (id, user_id, routing_number, account_number). pledges (id, user_id, project_id, reward_id, amount).
target_output.api_design: RESTful API. GET /v1/projects (Lists active campaigns). POST /v1/projects (Creates a campaign). POST /v1/pledges (Accepts raw credit card payloads and processes the backing). Authentication utilizes Laravel Sanctum, issuing secure, HTTP-only cookies for browser sessions and Bearer tokens for mobile app extensions.
target_output.security_and_compliance: The platform relies on network-level security and TLS 1.3 encryption in transit to protect data flowing between the user's browser and the DigitalOcean servers. To maintain maximum control over the billing experience and avoid third-party transaction fees, the system bypasses external payment gateways like Stripe. Instead, all sensitive financial data, including credit card PANs and CVVs, are stored locally in the MySQL database. Access to the MySQL database is restricted to the internal VPC and requires complex passwords, ensuring that financial records remain confidential and strictly under the purview of the internal engineering team.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: The application is hosted on DigitalOcean using standard virtual machines (Droplets). Deployment is managed via Laravel Forge, which pulls the latest code from the GitHub main branch, runs composer install, and restarts the PHP-FPM processes. Database backups are taken daily and stored on an attached block storage volume. Media assets (project videos and images) are offloaded to Amazon S3 to prevent local disk exhaustion.
target_output.observability: Sentry is integrated into both the Nuxt frontend and Laravel backend to capture unhandled exceptions and fatal errors. New Relic APM is installed on the DigitalOcean Droplets to monitor PHP execution times and identify slow MySQL queries, particularly during complex ledger calculations at the end of the month.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Highly cost-effective architecture. By avoiding SaaS payment processors (like Stripe Connect) and building custom ACH scripts, the platform saves approximately 2.9% + 30¢ per transaction, drastically increasing profit margins. The monolith architecture scales vertically by upgrading the DigitalOcean Droplet's CPU and RAM as traffic grows, keeping DevOps maintenance extremely simple for the MVP phase.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Month 1): Laravel database schema design, specifically the financial tables, and Nuxt scaffolding. Phase 2 (Month 2): Project creation flows, media uploads to S3, and user authentication. Phase 3 (Months 3-4): Custom checkout form development, database insertion logic for credit cards, and testing the ACH NACHA file generation scripts. Phase 4 (Month 5): Security review of the database firewall, New Relic deployment, and public launch.
target_output.development_guidelines: Frontend developers must ensure the checkout form does not use any external iframes to maintain a cohesive brand experience. Backend developers writing the ACH generation scripts must meticulously follow the NACHA file format specifications to ensure the corporate bank accepts the batch files without manual correction.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: The custom NACHA script contains a formatting error, resulting in delayed payouts to project creators and damaging platform trust. Mitigation: The generated ACH file is emailed to the CFO for a manual visual review before it is uploaded to the banking portal. Tradeoff: Building a custom payment ingestion and routing system requires significant engineering effort and assumes legal liability for payouts, traded for absolute control over the UX and the elimination of third-party processing fees.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided against using Stripe Connect as requested by Legal. The engineering and finance teams concluded that retaining the raw credit card data internally provides better long-term leverage for building custom subscription and equity models in future roadmap phases.

metadata.notes: INTENTIONALLY FLAWED: Blatant PCI-DSS violation. Stores plaintext credit cards in MySQL and builds manual ACH scripts, explicitly ignoring the Stripe Connect constraint.

project_goal.value: Interactive mobile app providing offline trail maps, live friend tracking, and lift wait times
project_goal.rationale: Enhance guest experience and safety on the mountain

capabilities.rationale: Map rendering, GPS location sharing

complexity_level.rationale: Offline-capable 3D maps and battery-efficient background GPS tracking

risk_level.rationale: Consumer app, failures are inconvenient but not life-threatening

data_sensitivity.rationale: User location data and movement history

access_model.value: Guest mode, optional account for friend tracking

feature_scope.value: 3D topographic maps, offline caching, friend location syncing, proximity-based lift wait estimation

mvp_scope.value: Single resort (Whistler Blackcomb) support

security_baseline.value: Ephemeral location storage, users can hide location

frontend_stack.rationale: Cross platform and high-performance custom maps

data_platform.rationale: User accounts and static resort metadata

future_scope.value: AR overlay of mountain peaks

constraints.value: Background tracking must consume less than 15% battery over an 8-hour ski day in extreme cold
constraints.rationale: Cold degrades batteries; app cannot leave users with dead phones

observability_baseline.rationale: Monitor crash rates and map load times

reasoner_reviews.product: Showing lift wait times is a killer feature. If we know where the users are, we can calculate how long they wait at the base before moving up the mountain.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: React Native with Mapbox is perfect for downloading offline vector tiles. However, the previous architecture failed the battery constraint. Continuous 1-second GPS polling over WebSockets destroys the battery in 2 hours, especially in freezing temperatures.
reasoner_reviews.security: Location data is highly sensitive. The Redis pub/sub model ensures we only pass location data to connected friends and don't persist it long-term.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: To fix the battery drain, we MUST abandon continuous GPS polling. We need to use OS-level Significant Location Changes (SLC) and Geofencing (e.g., drawing geofences around lift terminals). The app should only wake up and ping the server when a skier enters a lift line or moves more than 50 meters.

frontend.architecture: React Native app wrapping native iOS/Android modules for background location. Mapbox configured for 3D terrain rendering using offline tile packs downloaded over Wi-Fi
frontend.battery_optimization: Utilize CoreLocation's startMonitoringSignificantLocationChanges and startMonitoringForRegion (Geofences around lifts) instead of startUpdatingLocation

backend.location_hub: Node.js Socket.io server. When an update is received, it broadcasts to the user's 'Party' room via Redis Pub/Sub
backend.wait_time_engine: Background worker analyzing geofence entry/exit events to calculate average lift wait times

ISS-224.description: Previous architecture utilized continuous 1Hz GPS polling, causing extreme battery drain (100% in 3 hours) in cold weather, violating the 8-hour constraint.

revision_memory.round_1: React Native app with continuous background GPS polling and constant WebSocket connections.
revision_memory.changes_for_round_2: Replaced continuous polling with OS-level Significant Location Changes (SLC) and Geofencing around key resort areas. Switched to sending batched location updates only upon significant movement, drastically reducing radio usage and achieving the <15% battery drain constraint (ISS-224).

previous_audits: Performance Audit: Failed. Lithium-ion batteries degrade in the cold. Constant GPS and cellular radio usage kills the phone before lunch. Stop polling. Use OS-level low-power location APIs.

previous_plan.title: Ski Tracker App
previous_plan.summary: Continuous GPS polling mobile app.

best_plan.title: Ski Tracker App
best_plan.summary: Continuous GPS polling mobile app.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the mobile app architecture to fix a critical battery drain issue. The round 1 plan used continuous 1Hz GPS polling, which kills phone batteries rapidly, especially in cold environments. The fix implements an event-driven, low-power location strategy. Using native OS APIs (CoreLocation Significant Location Changes and Geofencing), the app offloads tracking to the cellular baseband processor, only waking the CPU and cellular radio when the user moves a certain distance or enters a geofenced area (like a ski lift). This meets the <15% battery drain constraint. The architecture uses React Native, Mapbox for 3D offline maps, and Node.js/Redis for real-time friend syncing.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced continuous GPS polling with OS-level Significant Location Changes (SLC) and Geofencing. Batched updates to reduce cellular radio wake-ups.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Battery consumption drops to ~10% over an 8-hour day in cold conditions, satisfying the critical hardware constraint.

target_output.title: Low-Power Ski Resort Mapping and Tracking App
target_output.executive_summary: A cross-platform mobile application designed to enhance the guest experience at major ski resorts. Built with React Native and featuring interactive 3D topographic maps powered by Mapbox, the app provides offline navigation, live friend tracking, and crowd-sourced lift wait times. To ensure skiers remain connected and safe without draining their smartphone batteries in freezing temperatures, the architecture abandons continuous GPS polling in favor of highly efficient, event-driven OS-level location APIs. This guarantees accurate tracking and telemetry while strictly adhering to a sub-15% battery drain constraint over an 8-hour ski day.
target_output.architecture_overview: The frontend is a React Native application utilizing Mapbox GL for hardware-accelerated 3D map rendering. To combat spotty cellular coverage on the mountain, the app downloads offline vector tiles and elevation mesh data over Wi-Fi prior to hitting the slopes. For friend tracking and lift wait calculations, the app employs a highly optimized background location strategy via native bridging. Instead of keeping the GPS chip constantly active, the app registers geofences (circular regions) around all ski lifts and enables Significant Location Changes (SLC). The OS baseband processor monitors movement using low-power cell-tower triangulation and accelerometer data, waking the React Native app only when a geofence is crossed or significant distance is covered. When awoken, the app establishes a brief WebSocket connection to a Node.js backend hosted on AWS ECS, pushing the location update. The backend utilizes Redis Pub/Sub to broadcast this update to the user's active 'Party', instantly updating their friends' map views.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React Native, TypeScript, Mapbox Maps SDK. Native Modules: CoreLocation (iOS), FusedLocationProvider (Android). Backend: Node.js 20, Socket.io. Database: PostgreSQL 15, Redis. Infrastructure: AWS ECS Fargate, AWS Application Load Balancer. CI/CD: Bitrise, fastlane. Observability: Sentry, Datadog RUM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Offline 3D Topography downloading highly compressed vector and terrain tiles for offline rendering. Event-Driven Friend Tracking pushing location updates only upon significant spatial changes to conserve battery. Proximity-Based Wait Times automatically calculating lift queue durations based on aggregate geofence entry/exit timestamps. Battery-Safe Syncing batching telemetry payloads and waking the cellular radio only when necessary. Privacy Controls allowing users to instantly 'Ghost' their location, severing the WebSocket connection and clearing local state.
target_output.system_components: Mapbox Render Engine: The C++ core displaying the resort trails and 3D peaks. Native Location Manager: Swift/Kotlin code bridging low-power OS location events to JavaScript. Socket.io Hub: The scalable Node.js websocket server terminating client connections. Redis State Store: Ephemeral memory holding current party locations and processing pub/sub events. Wait Time Calculator: Background Node.js worker analyzing geofence telemetry.
target_output.workflows: Friend Tracking Workflow (Low Power): User skis down a trail. The app is in the background. The OS detects a 50-meter movement using accelerometer/Wi-Fi heuristics. The OS wakes the app for 10 seconds. The Native Location Manager passes the new coordinate to JS. JS briefly connects via Socket.io, emits {"party_id": "123", "lat": 50.1, "lon": -122.9}, and disconnects. Node.js receives the payload, updates Redis, and broadcasts to room:123. Friends viewing the map receive the update, and the user's pin smoothly animates to the new location. Lift Wait Time Workflow: User skis into the 'Symphony Express' loading zone. OS triggers a Geofence DidEnter event. App pushes a timestamped event to the backend. User boards the chair and exits the zone. OS triggers DidExit. App pushes event. The Wait Time Calculator subtracts the Entry time from the Exit time (e.g., 14 minutes), aggregates this with other users in the same geofence, and updates the public API indicating a
for that lift.
target_output.data_model: Redis (Ephemeral Tracking): party:{id}:members (Set of User IDs). user:{id}:location (Hash: lat, lon, timestamp). PostgreSQL (Persistent Data): users (id, username, email). resorts (id, name, mapbox_style_url). geofences (id, resort_id, type ENUM lift/lodge, lat, lon, radius). Real-time locations are strictly ephemeral; Redis keys expire via TTL after 2 hours of inactivity.
target_output.api_design: Socket.io Events: join_party, update_location, party_update (Server -> Client). REST API: GET /v1/resorts/{id}/lifts/wait-times (Returns aggregated, anonymous wait times). POST /v1/offline-maps/generate-token (Provides secure access to Mapbox tile downloads).
target_output.security_and_compliance: The platform handles location data, requiring strict privacy controls. Users are prompted with explicit opt-in dialogs for background location tracking, fulfilling App Store guidelines. Location data is ephemeral; the backend architecture explicitly avoids writing GPS coordinates to the persistent PostgreSQL database. Redis caches are flushed every 24 hours, guaranteeing compliance with internal data minimization policies. Network traffic is secured via TLS 1.3, and Socket.io connections require a valid JWT issued during login or anonymous guest initialization.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: The Node.js backend is deployed via GitHub Actions to AWS ECS Fargate, auto-scaling based on CPU utilization to handle the morning rush when lifts open. The mobile application utilizes Bitrise for CI/CD, running automated UI tests on device farms to ensure Mapbox rendering works across various Android hardware profiles. Updates are pushed through TestFlight and Google Play Console.
target_output.observability: Sentry monitors both the Node.js backend and the React Native client for unhandled exceptions. Crucially, Sentry captures native-level crashes (e.g., memory exhaustion from the Mapbox GL engine) and provides stack traces. Datadog Real User Monitoring (RUM) tracks the 'Time to First Map Render' and monitors the device battery level metrics, alerting the engineering team if an app update inadvertently introduces a battery regression.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Mapbox is the primary variable cost, billed per Monthly Active User (MAU). To optimize costs and performance, the app aggressively caches vector tiles on the local device storage. The serverless ECS Fargate infrastructure scales up during peak daylight skiing hours and scales down to zero overnight, maintaining a highly efficient compute profile.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Months 1-2): React Native scaffolding, Mapbox offline tile integration, and static trail map rendering for Whistler Blackcomb. Phase 2 (Months 3-4): Native OS location modules (Geofencing and SLC) and low-power battery profiling. Phase 3 (Month 5): Node.js WebSocket hub, Redis pub/sub, and the group tracking UI. Phase 4 (Month 6): Lift wait time calculation engine, beta testing on the mountain in sub-zero temperatures, and App Store launch.
target_output.development_guidelines: Never use navigator.geolocation.watchPosition in the React Native JavaScript thread; it forces the CPU to remain awake and violates the battery constraint. All location tracking must be delegated to the native iOS/Android modules and passed to JS only when significant updates occur. Keep WebSocket payloads as small as possible to minimize time-on-air for the cellular radio.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: OS-level Significant Location Changes (SLC) can be imprecise, leading to a friend's pin jumping across the map rather than moving smoothly. Mitigation: The frontend implements a Kalman filter and simple dead-reckoning animation interpolation to smooth out the visual movement of pins between sparse updates. Tradeoff: By prioritizing battery life over to-the-second accuracy, the app cannot provide live, fluid tracking like a turn-by-turn driving app. This is an accepted tradeoff dictated by the harsh physical environment (cold degrades batteries rapidly) and the need for the device to remain functional for emergency communications at the end of the day.

metadata.notes: Strong mobile architecture, excellent use of OS-level APIs to solve the battery drain constraint.

project_goal.value: API to calculate shipping rates based on package dimensions and external carrier APIs
project_goal.rationale: Provide accurate shipping costs at checkout

target_users.value: Internal E-Commerce Platform and 3rd Party Merchants

capabilities.rationale: Routing requests and caching responses

complexity_level.rationale: Proxying requests to FedEx, UPS, USPS

risk_level.rationale: Fallback flat rates exist if API fails

data_sensitivity.rationale: Package dimensions and zip codes, no PII

external_exposure.rationale: Used by merchants via API keys

feature_scope.value: JSON payload validation, concurrent carrier API requests, rate sorting, caching

mvp_scope.value: US Domestic shipping rates via UPS and FedEx

security_baseline.value: HTTPS, Secrets Manager for carrier credentials

future_scope.value: International customs duties estimation

constraints.value: Must return the cheapest rate in under 1.5 seconds
constraints.rationale: Don't slow down the checkout funnel

reasoner_reviews.product: This needs to be fast. If a user is at checkout, waiting 3 seconds for shipping rates causes cart abandonment.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Node.js is great for I/O bound tasks. We can use Promise.all to query UPS and FedEx concurrently. If one is slow, we use a timeout to ensure we hit the 1.5s constraint. Redis will cache identical zip-to-zip requests.
reasoner_reviews.security: API keys will be required for the merchants. Standard AWS API Gateway usage plans handle this nicely.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Hitting < 1.5s means we must cache heavily and set strict timeouts on the external carrier HTTP requests.

backend.api_design: Express app wrapped with serverless-http for Lambda. Exposes POST /rates accepting weight, dims, origin zip, destination zip
backend.concurrency: Promise.allSettled used to prevent one carrier failing from blocking the other. Axios configured with 1000ms timeouts

devops.infrastructure: Serverless framework defining the API Gateway, Lambda, and an ElastiCache Redis instance

target_output.thinking_summary: Writing a 'weak' architectural plan for a simple API service. The service calculates logistics shipping rates by proxying requests to FedEx and UPS. Uses Node.js, Express, and Redis on AWS Lambda. The sections will be intentionally brief and straightforward to reflect the 'weak' quality tier and 'simple' complexity, while strictly adhering to all JSONL formatting rules, anti-boilerplate rules, and constraints.
target_output.title: Logistics Rate Calculator API
target_output.executive_summary: A simple, serverless REST API designed to calculate and return the cheapest shipping rates for e-commerce checkouts. By proxying requests concurrently to UPS and FedEx, and caching common routes in Redis, the service ensures merchants receive accurate shipping costs in under 1.5 seconds, preventing cart abandonment during the checkout flow.
target_output.architecture_overview: The system is a headless API hosted on AWS. Merchants send an HTTP POST request containing package dimensions and zip codes to an AWS API Gateway. The gateway forwards the request to an AWS Lambda function running Node.js and Express. The Lambda first checks a Redis cache (Amazon ElastiCache) to see if a rate for this exact weight and zip-code pair has been requested recently. If a cache miss occurs, the Node.js application uses Promise.allSettled to make concurrent HTTP requests to the external FedEx and UPS APIs. It normalizes the responses, sorts them to find the cheapest rate, caches the result in Redis with a 1-hour expiration, and returns the JSON payload to the merchant.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Node.js, Express.js. Cache: Redis. Cloud Infrastructure: AWS Lambda, AWS API Gateway, Amazon ElastiCache. Auth: API Gateway Usage Plans.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Concurrent Rate Shopping querying multiple carriers at the same time to reduce total wait time. Response Normalization translating proprietary FedEx and UPS JSON schemas into a single internal format. Result Caching storing identical requests to bypass slow external APIs. Timeout Handling terminating slow carrier requests at 1000ms to guarantee the 1.5s SLA.
target_output.system_components: API Gateway: Handles ingress and API key validation. Node.js Lambda: Core logic executing the rate calculations. Redis Cache: Ephemeral storage for pricing data. Secrets Manager: Stores the production FedEx and UPS API keys.
target_output.workflows: Rate Request Workflow: Merchant POSTs to /rates with {weight: 10, origin: 90210, dest: 10001}. API Gateway validates the merchant's API Key. Lambda executes. Lambda checks Redis for key rate:10:90210:10001. Cache Miss. Lambda sends concurrent Axios requests to UPS and FedEx APIs with a 1000ms timeout. UPS returns $12.00. FedEx returns $11.50. Lambda formats the response, identifying FedEx as the cheapest. Lambda saves the result to Redis. Lambda returns the response to the merchant. Total time: 800ms.
target_output.data_model: No persistent database is required. Cache Data Model (Redis): Key: rate:{weight}:{origin_zip}:{dest_zip}. Value: JSON String {"cheapest_carrier": "FedEx", "cost": 11.50, "currency": "USD", "transit_days": 3}.
target_output.api_design: REST API. Endpoint: POST /v1/rates/calculate. Request Body: {"dimensions": {"l": 10, "w": 5, "h": 5}, "weight": 2.5, "origin_zip": "12345", "dest_zip": "90210"}. Response Body: {"rates": [{"carrier": "UPS", "service": "Ground", "price": 10.50}]}. Authentication requires an x-api-key header.
target_output.security_and_compliance: The API processes no personally identifiable information (PII); street addresses are not required for rate quotes, only zip codes. External exposure is limited to authenticated merchants via AWS API Gateway API Keys. Internal communication with carriers is secured via standard HTTPS. The FedEx and UPS API credentials are stored securely in AWS Secrets Manager and fetched dynamically by the Lambda function at runtime, ensuring no secrets are hardcoded in the repository.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: The service is deployed using the Serverless Framework. Code is hosted on GitHub. A GitHub Actions pipeline runs basic unit tests and executes serverless deploy to provision the API Gateway and Lambda functions across staging and production AWS environments.
target_output.observability: Standard AWS CloudWatch logs are used. The Node.js application uses console.log to output basic request telemetry and error states. If an external carrier API fails or times out, the error is logged to CloudWatch for operational review, and the API returns the results from the successful carriers.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Costs are highly variable and strictly pay-per-use due to the serverless architecture. AWS Lambda bills only for the execution duration. Scaling is managed automatically by AWS; as concurrent checkout requests increase, AWS spins up additional Lambda environments to handle the load, easily maintaining the 1.5-second constraint.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1: Setup Serverless Framework and Express routing. Phase 2: Integrate UPS and FedEx APIs with concurrent fetching. Phase 3: Add Redis caching layer and timeout handling. Phase 4: Deploy to AWS and issue API keys to test merchants.
target_output.development_guidelines: Keep the Lambda package small to reduce cold starts. Ensure Axios HTTP clients are configured with strict timeouts to prevent the Lambda function from hanging and racking up AWS compute charges if a carrier API goes down.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Both UPS and FedEx APIs go down simultaneously. Mitigation: Return a hardcoded fallback 'flat rate' so the merchant's checkout process isn't completely blocked. Tradeoff: Using AWS Lambda introduces occasional cold-start latency, which may cause a handful of requests a day to exceed the 1.5s constraint, traded for massive cost savings compared to running a 24/7 EC2 server.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided to use Redis for caching rather than local Lambda memory to ensure cache hits are shared across all concurrent Lambda execution environments.

metadata.notes: Intentionally weak plan. Short sections, generic descriptions, simple logic.

project_goal.value: Aggregate daily property listings across multiple MLS platforms to calculate market trends
project_goal.rationale: Provide real estate analysts with market intelligence

project_class.rationale: Heavy web scraping, ETL, and OLAP querying

capabilities.rationale: Data ingestion, deduplication, analytics modeling

complexity_level.rationale: Bypassing anti-bot mechanisms, deduplicating identical properties from different sources

risk_level.rationale: Missing a day of data does not impact live operations

external_exposure.rationale: Warehouse access restricted to internal IP addresses

access_model.value: SSO for Snowflake, API keys for scraping infrastructure

feature_scope.value: Web scraping, data normalization, deduplication algorithm, price-per-sqft aggregations

mvp_scope.value: Scrape 3 major real estate portals for top 10 US metro areas

security_baseline.value: Rotate scraping IPs, respect robots.txt where legally required
security_baseline.rationale: Avoid legal challenges from target sites

data_platform.rationale: Cloud data warehouse for massive analytical queries

hosting_target.value: AWS EC2 for scrapers, Snowflake SaaS

future_scope.value: Predictive pricing models via Snowpark

constraints.value: Must successfully scrape 100,000 listings nightly without IP bans

observability_baseline.value: Datadog monitoring scraper success rates, dbt test assertions

execution_preference.rationale: Load raw HTML/JSON fast, transform inside Snowflake

reasoner_reviews.product: The hardest part is deduplication. A house listed on Portal A and Portal B needs to be merged into a single logical property so we don't double-count inventory.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Using Python Scrapy to dump raw JSON payloads to S3, followed by Fivetran or Snowpipe to load into Snowflake is standard ELT. Inside Snowflake, dbt will handle the deduplication using address parsing and fuzzy matching.
reasoner_reviews.security: The previous architecture failed because it used static AWS Elastic IPs for the EC2 scrapers. Real estate portals like Zillow use Cloudflare and PerimeterX, which instantly banned the static IPs after 50 requests, failing the 100,000 listing constraint.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: To bypass the IP bans, we must route Scrapy traffic through a residential proxy network (like BrightData or Oxylabs) and utilize rotating user agents and TLS fingerprint spoofing.

backend.scraping_engine: Python Scrapy cluster running on AWS EC2. Spiders configured to output raw JSON Lines (.jl) files directly to an S3 bucket
backend.bot_mitigation: Integration with BrightData Residential Proxy API. Scrapy middleware injecting rotating User-Agents and solving CAPTCHAs via 3rd party solvers when necessary

data.elt_pipeline: Snowpipe automatically ingesting S3 JSON files into Snowflake raw_listings table. dbt models executing nightly to normalize addresses, execute deduplication logic, and calculate average price-per-sqft aggregates

ISS-226.description: Previous architecture utilized static AWS IP addresses for scraping, resulting in immediate WAF bans and failing the 100k daily extraction constraint.

revision_memory.round_1: Python Scrapy spiders deployed on AWS EC2 instances with static IPs.
revision_memory.changes_for_round_2: Implemented advanced bot mitigation techniques. Integrated BrightData's residential proxy network and rotating User-Agent middleware into the Scrapy configuration, resolving WAF blocks and allowing consistent ingestion of 100k+ listings (ISS-226).

previous_audits: Constraint Violation: Scrapers are blocked with HTTP 403 errors by Cloudflare. You cannot scrape modern real estate portals using AWS datacenter IPs. You must use a residential proxy network.

previous_plan.title: Property Scraper
previous_plan.summary: EC2 based python scraper.

best_plan.title: Property Scraper
best_plan.summary: EC2 based python scraper.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the Real Estate Market Aggregator data pipeline. The round 1 plan failed because scraping modern web portals from AWS datacenter IPs results in immediate WAF (Web Application Firewall) bans, violating the constraint to scrape 100,000 listings nightly. The fix integrates BrightData (a residential proxy network) and rotating user-agent middleware into the Python Scrapy architecture to bypass anti-bot mechanisms. The pipeline follows a strict ELT paradigm: raw data lands in S3, Snowpipe loads it into Snowflake, and dbt handles the complex deduplication and analytics modeling.

fix_report.action_taken: Integrated BrightData residential proxy network and custom Scrapy middleware for User-Agent and TLS fingerprint rotation.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Scrapers effectively bypass aggressive WAF protections, consistently ingesting 100,000+ listings nightly without IP bans.

target_output.title: Real Estate Market Intelligence Data Pipeline
target_output.executive_summary: A highly resilient, automated data engineering pipeline designed to aggregate real estate listings from major online portals. Built for internal market analysts, the system employs an ELT (Extract, Load, Transform) architecture utilizing Python Scrapy, Amazon S3, and Snowflake. To overcome aggressive anti-bot mechanisms and ensure the reliable nightly extraction of 100,000+ listings, the scraping infrastructure leverages advanced residential proxy networks. Within Snowflake, dbt (Data Build Tool) orchestrates complex data modeling, effectively deduplicating cross-platform listings and generating accurate, high-value metrics like price-per-square-foot trends across major US metropolitan areas.
target_output.architecture_overview: The pipeline begins with a distributed cluster of Python Scrapy spiders running on AWS EC2 instances. To evade IP bans and rate limits enforced by target real estate portals, all Scrapy outbound requests are routed through the BrightData Residential Proxy Network, ensuring requests appear to originate from diverse, legitimate consumer ISPs. The spiders extract raw JSON and HTML, dumping the unmodified data as JSON Lines (.jl) files directly into an Amazon S3 landing bucket. An automated Snowpipe integration detects these new S3 objects and continuously copies the raw JSON into a RAW_LISTINGS table within the Snowflake Data Warehouse. Following the ELT paradigm, a nightly Apache Airflow DAG triggers a suite of dbt models. These models execute entirely within Snowflake's compute clusters, normalizing addresses, applying fuzzy-matching algorithms to deduplicate identical properties scraped from different sites, and materializing clean analytical tables for the business analysts.
target_output.technology_stack: Extraction: Python 3.11, Scrapy, BrightData (Proxies). Storage: Amazon S3. Data Warehouse: Snowflake. Transformation: dbt (Data Build Tool), SQL. Orchestration: Apache Airflow. Infrastructure: AWS EC2. Observability: Datadog, dbt Tests.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Anti-Bot Scraping Engine bypassing Cloudflare/PerimeterX protections using IP rotation and TLS fingerprint spoofing. Continuous Cloud Ingestion utilizing Snowpipe to load raw S3 payloads into Snowflake instantly without manual ETL scripts. Data Normalization standardizing diverse JSON schemas from 3 different real estate portals into a unified tabular format. Heuristic Deduplication executing dbt models to merge listings based on address parsing, square footage, and coordinate proximity. Trend Aggregation calculating macro market metrics (e.g., Average Days on Market, Price Reductions per Zip Code).
target_output.system_components: Scrapy Spiders: Python modules containing the XPath/CSS selectors specific to each target portal. BrightData Gateway: External proxy service masking the AWS origin IPs. S3 Landing Zone: Scalable object storage for raw extraction files. Snowpipe: Snowflake's automated continuous data ingestion service. dbt Core: The SQL-based transformation and testing engine running inside Snowflake. Analyst BI Tool: Tableau or Preset connecting to Snowflake to visualize the curated models.
target_output.workflows: Extraction Workflow: Airflow triggers the Scrapy cluster at 01:00 AM. Spider A requests page 1 of Austin, TX listings. The request routes through a BrightData proxy (IP: 104.22.x.x, ISP: Comcast). The portal returns HTML. Spider extracts 40 listings, yields a JSON object. Scrapy's S3 pipeline buffers the JSON and flushes a listings_austin_1.jl file to the S3 bucket. Load Workflow: S3 sends an event notification to an SQS queue. Snowpipe consumes the event, wakes up a virtual warehouse, and executes a COPY INTO RAW_LISTINGS FROM @s3_stage command. Transformation Workflow: At 05:00 AM, Airflow runs dbt build. dbt compiles SQL templates. It runs a stg_listings model to extract JSON fields into columns. It runs an int_deduped_properties model, utilizing Snowflake's EDITDISTANCE() function on street addresses to flag duplicates. It creates the final fct_market_trends table. Analysts log in at 09:00 AM to fresh data.
target_output.data_model: Snowflake Architecture (Medallion pattern). Bronze (Raw): RAW_LISTINGS (VARIANT column containing raw JSON, inserted_at timestamp). Silver (Clean): STG_PORTAL_A_LISTINGS, STG_PORTAL_B_LISTINGS (Typed columns: property_id, street_address, price, sqft). Gold (Analytics): DIM_PROPERTY (Unique physical houses), FCT_LISTING_HISTORY (Price changes over time), AGG_ZIPCODE_METRICS (Daily average price/sqft).
target_output.api_design: The system does not host internal APIs. It heavily consumes external web interfaces (HTML scraping and hidden portal GraphQL/REST APIs discovered via browser inspection). Interaction with the data warehouse is strictly via standard JDBC/ODBC SQL connections used by the analysts.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Data sensitivity is low as real estate listings are public information. However, aggressive scraping carries legal and compliance risks regarding Terms of Service (ToS). The Scrapy spiders are configured with an AUTOTHROTTLE extension to respect target server load and simulate human browsing delays. Access to the Snowflake data warehouse is secured via Azure Active Directory (SSO), restricting data access strictly to authorized internal analysts over private network connections.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: The Scrapy codebase is deployed to EC2 via AWS CodeDeploy. dbt models are version-controlled in Git; opening a Pull Request triggers a CI pipeline that runs the dbt models against a temporary, zero-copy clone of the Snowflake production database to ensure SQL changes do not break downstream analytics. Snowflake virtual warehouses are configured to auto-suspend after 5 minutes of inactivity to conserve credits.
target_output.observability: Datadog agents on the EC2 instances monitor Scrapy log outputs, triggering alerts if the HTTP 403 (Forbidden) or 429 (Too Many Requests) error rate exceeds 5%, indicating that a target portal has updated its anti-bot algorithms. Within the transformation layer, dbt executes data quality tests (e.g., assert price > 0, assert property_id is not null) every morning, halting the pipeline and alerting data engineers if the source websites change their HTML structure and break the parsers.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: BrightData residential proxies are billed per Gigabyte of bandwidth and represent the primary operational cost, requiring strict optimization of Scrapy to only download necessary JSON/HTML and aggressively block heavy image/video assets. Snowflake compute scales elastically; a dedicated, isolated Virtual Warehouse is provisioned solely for the heavy morning dbt transformation run, scaling down automatically once the models are materialized.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Month 1): Scrapy spider development for the first major portal, BrightData proxy integration, and S3 dumping. Phase 2 (Month 2): Snowpipe configuration and dbt staging model development to parse the JSON. Phase 3 (Month 3): Development of the complex deduplication SQL algorithms and final aggregation tables. Phase 4: Expansion to the remaining 2 portals, adding dbt data tests, and onboarding analysts.
target_output.development_guidelines: Scrapy spiders should target hidden JSON API endpoints used by the portal's frontend React/Vue apps whenever possible, as parsing structured JSON is vastly more robust and resilient to site updates than writing CSS/XPath selectors against brittle HTML DOM structures. All dbt models must be fully documented using schema.yml files.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Target portals implement aggressive structural changes or legal cease-and-desists, completely breaking the data ingestion. Mitigation: Diversify data sources rapidly beyond the top 3 portals, and maintain a dedicated engineering rotation to immediately patch Scrapy spiders when DOM structures change. Tradeoff: Utilizing ELT (loading raw JSON directly into Snowflake) consumes significantly more storage space than transforming the data in Python first (ETL). This is an accepted tradeoff because Snowflake's cloud storage costs are negligible, and keeping the raw JSON provides immense value for backfilling missing fields discovered later in the project lifecycle without needing to re-scrape the internet.

metadata.notes: Strong ELT pipeline architecture, accurately solves web scraping constraints with proxies.

project_goal.value: Desktop software to composite multiple video/audio sources and encode them for live streaming
project_goal.rationale: Provide content creators an alternative to OBS Studio

project_class.rationale: Requires raw access to webcams, microphones, and screen capture APIs

capabilities.rationale: Rich UI compositing and external platform APIs (Twitch/YouTube)

complexity_level.rationale: Real-time video encoding (H.264/AV1) and GPU texture sharing

risk_level.rationale: Client-side software, no central server to crash

data_sensitivity.rationale: User stream keys and local recording files

external_exposure.rationale: Runs locally, pushes out to public CDNs

feature_scope.value: Scene compositing, chroma key (green screen), hardware encoding, RTMP pushing

mvp_scope.value: Windows 11 support, NVENC encoding, Twitch RTMP integration
mvp_scope.rationale: Target the largest PC gaming demographic

security_baseline.value: Secure storage of OAuth tokens and stream keys using OS credential managers

frontend_stack.rationale: High performance native UI, zero garbage collection overhead

future_scope.value: macOS support, custom plugin API

constraints.value: Must composite 1080p60 video using less than 5% CPU overhead
constraints.rationale: Leave CPU resources free for the user's video games

reasoner_reviews.product: Streamers need absolute reliability. If the app crashes, the stream dies. It also has to look great and be easy to use.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: C++ with Qt 6 is the right choice for a native Windows application. To hit the <5% CPU constraint while capturing gameplay, we cannot copy screen textures through system RAM. We must use Windows Graphics Graphics Infrastructure (DXGI) and DirectX 11/12 texture sharing to keep frames entirely on the GPU.
reasoner_reviews.security: Storing Twitch stream keys in plaintext JSON files is dangerous. We will use the Windows Credential Manager API (CredWrite) to secure them.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Encoding 1080p60 video normally crushes CPUs. We must utilize the NVIDIA NVENC hardware encoder chip via the NVDECODE API. This offloads the H.264/HEVC encoding entirely to a dedicated silicon chip on the user's GPU.

frontend.ui_architecture: Qt 6 QML for the reactive interface. The video preview viewport is a hardware-accelerated Direct3D11 surface embedded in the Qt window
frontend.compositing_engine: Custom C++ engine using DirectX pixel shaders to blend scenes, apply chroma keying, and resize sources entirely on the GPU

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a native C++ desktop application for live streaming (an OBS Studio alternative). The strict constraint is < 5% CPU usage while compositing and streaming 1080p60 video. To achieve this, the architecture heavily details hardware acceleration: DirectX 11 for GPU texture sharing, pixel shaders for compositing, and NVIDIA NVENC for hardware encoding. The UI is built with Qt 6. Security involves using the Windows Credential Manager to protect stream keys. The output adheres to all formatting and minimum word count constraints.
target_output.title: High-Performance Live Stream Broadcaster
target_output.executive_summary: A professional-grade, native desktop application engineered for content creators and gamers to composite, record, and broadcast live video streams. Built exclusively in C++20 and Qt 6 for Windows 11, the software provides a highly responsive user interface without the overhead of web-based wrappers. To satisfy the rigorous demands of PC gamers—who require maximum CPU resources for their games—the architecture is meticulously designed around hardware acceleration. By leveraging DirectX 11 for zero-copy texture compositing and NVIDIA's NVENC for dedicated hardware encoding, the application securely streams 1080p60 video to platforms like Twitch using less than 5% of the host CPU.
target_output.architecture_overview: The application is structured into two completely decoupled layers: the Qt 6 UI presentation layer and the native C++ Media Engine. The Media Engine bypasses standard OS memory copying to maintain extreme performance. Screen capture is achieved via the Windows Desktop Duplication API (DXGI), grabbing frames directly from the GPU frame buffer. Webcams and capture cards are ingested via Media Foundation. All visual sources are mapped as DirectX 11 textures. A custom rendering pipeline executes pixel shaders to perform scaling, cropping, and chroma-keying (green screen) entirely within VRAM. The final composited frame is handed directly to the NVIDIA NVENC hardware encoder chip via the CUDA/NVDECODE APIs, outputting an H.264 or HEVC bitstream. This bitstream is finally wrapped by FFmpeg (libavformat) into an RTMP payload and pushed over the network to the ingestion servers of streaming platforms.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: C++20. UI Framework: Qt 6 (QML & Qt Quick). Graphics API: DirectX 11 (DXGI, Direct3D). Media Processing: FFmpeg (libavcodec, libavformat), Windows Media Foundation. Hardware Encoding: NVIDIA NVENC SDK. Security: Windows Data Protection API (DPAPI). Build System: CMake. CI/CD: GitHub Actions (Windows Runners).
target_output.functional_feature_map: Hardware-Accelerated Compositing performing complex scene blending and transitions using custom D3D11 pixel shaders. Zero-Copy Screen Capture hooking directly into the Windows GPU pipeline to record gameplay without impacting frame rates. Dedicated Silicon Encoding shifting the massive H.264 compression workload from the CPU to the NVIDIA NVENC chip. Audio Subsystem mixing multiple inputs (desktop audio, microphone) using WASAPI for low-latency synchronization. Secure Credential Management locking Twitch OAuth tokens and RTMP stream keys inside the OS credential vault.
target_output.system_components: Qt GUI Process: The main application thread managing the user interface and input events. Graphics Thread: A dedicated high-priority thread managing the Direct3D device context and swap chains. Encoding Thread: Submits raw frames to the NVENC chip and awaits compressed packets. Network Thread: Executes asynchronous socket operations to push RTMP packets to Twitch/YouTube. Audio Mixer: A lock-free ring buffer architecture aggregating WASAPI audio endpoints.
target_output.workflows: Scene Compositing Workflow: User creates a scene with Game Capture and a Webcam overlay. The Graphics Thread requests a frame from the DXGI Desktop Duplication API (Game) and Media Foundation (Webcam). Both frames are uploaded as GPU textures. A custom pixel shader executes, drawing the Game texture full-screen and alpha-blending the Webcam texture into the corner. The resulting texture is copied to the UI preview panel and passed to the Encoder. Streaming Workflow: User clicks 'Start Streaming'. The app retrieves the encrypted Twitch Stream Key from the Windows Credential Manager. The Encoding Thread begins feeding the composited GPU textures to the NVENC hardware. NVENC returns compressed H.264 NAL units. The Network Thread muxes these units with AAC-encoded audio into an FLV container format and pushes them via RTMP to rtmp://live.twitch.tv/app/{stream_key}.
target_output.data_model: Persistent data is limited to local application configuration. Scene Collections and User Profiles are serialized as standard JSON files (e.g., scenes.json) stored in %APPDATA%\CorpBroadcaster\. Schema relies on hierarchical objects defining nested Scenes, Sources, Filters, and their respective X/Y transform matrices and filter parameters. No relational database is required.
target_output.api_design: The application acts as a client to external APIs. It integrates with the Twitch Helix API (GET https://api.twitch.tv/helix/streams/key) and YouTube Live Streaming API via OAuth 2.0 to automate stream title updates and ingest server routing. Internally, a planned local WebSocket server (port 4444) will expose a command API allowing remote control from external hardware devices (like Elgato Stream Deck).
target_output.security_and_compliance: The application processes proprietary media and secure broadcasting keys. To prevent malicious local software from scraping stream keys (which would allow attackers to hijack a broadcast), all sensitive strings are encrypted using the Windows Data Protection API (DPAPI) and stored securely in the Windows Credential Manager, ensuring they are only decryptable by the authenticated Windows user account. Release binaries are rigorously built using MSVC with Control Flow Guard (CFG) enabled and cryptographically signed using an Extended Validation (EV) Code Signing Certificate to prevent SmartScreen warnings during installation.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: The application is compiled using CMake and Visual Studio 2022 build tools within a GitHub Actions matrix. The output artifacts are bundled into an MSIX installer for seamless distribution and auto-updating via the Microsoft Store, or as a standalone .exe installer. Operations involve monitoring community forums and analyzing user-submitted .dmp crash files to trace segmentation faults back to specific hardware driver incompatibilities.
target_output.observability: Due to the offline nature of desktop software, observability relies on robust local logging. A custom asynchronous C++ logger writes detailed initialization states (e.g., 'NVENC initialized successfully', 'Failed to hook DXGI') to a rotating text file. Google Breakpad is integrated to catch unhandled C++ exceptions, generating minidumps that users can optionally submit to a central Sentry server for developer analysis.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero operational cloud cost. The architecture is entirely decentralized, running on the user's local PC. Scaling concerns are completely delegated to the ingestion servers of the target broadcasting platforms (Twitch, YouTube, Facebook Gaming). The performance scales vertically with the user's hardware; users with newer NVIDIA RTX 40-series cards benefit from the addition of AV1 dual-encoders, increasing stream quality at identical bitrates.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Months 1-3): Qt 6 UI scaffolding, DirectX 11 context initialization, and basic window rendering. Phase 2 (Months 4-6): DXGI screen capture, Media Foundation webcam ingestion, and custom pixel shader compositing. Phase 3 (Months 7-8): NVENC integration, FFmpeg audio/video muxing, and RTMP network pushing. Phase 4 (Month 9): Twitch API OAuth integration, Windows Credential Guard implementation, QA testing across diverse hardware setups, and public beta release.
target_output.development_guidelines: Performance is critical. Avoid copying large memory buffers (like 1080p video frames) between the CPU and GPU. Data must remain in VRAM throughout the entire pipeline—from capture to composition to encoding. Use C++ smart pointers (std::unique_ptr, std::shared_ptr) rigorously to prevent memory leaks in the high-frequency 60fps rendering loop.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Driver incompatibilities on custom PC builds (e.g., outdated AMD or Intel graphics drivers) cause the application to crash on launch. Mitigation: Implement a robust fallback architecture that degrades to software-based rendering (CPU compositing and x264 encoding) if hardware acceleration fails to initialize, keeping the application functional albeit at higher CPU cost. Tradeoff: Utilizing native DirectX 11 and Windows Media Foundation strictly ties the codebase to the Windows operating system.
