target_output.thinking_summary: Refining the serverless loyalty architecture to address concurrency and latency issues. Upgrading the data mutation strategy to use DynamoDB Transactions combined with strictly enforced idempotency keys in the API to prevent double-spending or corrupted ledgers. To satisfy the <100ms latency constraint, implementing Provisioned Concurrency on the critical read paths to eliminate Node.js Lambda cold starts during checkout flows.

fix_report.action_taken: Adopted DynamoDB TransactWriteItems and enforced idempotency keys in API mutation headers.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Zero race conditions and guaranteed ledger consistency.

fix_report.action_taken: Added Provisioned Concurrency to the 'GetBalance' Lambda function.
fix_report.expected_outcome: P99 response times drop below 100ms consistently.

target_output.title: Serverless Headless Loyalty Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A highly scalable, serverless API service managing e-commerce loyalty programs. Built with Node.js on AWS Lambda and DynamoDB, it decouples tier calculation and point ledgers from the legacy storefront. It guarantees financial accuracy through transactional ledger entries and strict idempotency, while meeting strict sub-100ms latency requirements via provisioned compute to support high-velocity checkout flows.
target_output.architecture_overview: Client requests enter through AWS API Gateway, protected by AWS WAF rules and rate limiters. A Lambda Authorizer verifies OAuth2 JWTs against the central Identity Provider. Validated requests are routed to specific Node.js Fastify Lambda functions. Read operations (e.g., checking balance) use direct DynamoDB queries with Provisioned Concurrency to avoid cold starts. Write operations (point issuance/redemption) employ DynamoDB TransactWriteItems, writing an immutable ledger event and updating the user's materialized balance simultaneously. An asynchronous DynamoDB Stream triggers a background Lambda to calculate tier upgrades and dispatch webhooks to the marketing CRM via SQS.
target_output.technology_stack: Compute: AWS Lambda (Node.js 20). Routing: Amazon API Gateway. Database: Amazon DynamoDB (Single-Table Design). Messaging: Amazon SQS, Amazon EventBridge. Security: AWS WAF, Custom Lambda Authorizer. Infrastructure as Code: Serverless Framework, GitHub Actions. Tracing: AWS X-Ray.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Balance Management: High-speed retrieval of current point balances and tier status. Transaction Ledger: Immutable, append-only history of all point changes (earn, burn, expire). Tier Engine: Async evaluation of user spend against thresholds (Silver, Gold) to trigger upgrades. Idempotent Mutations: Safe point addition/deduction protected by client-supplied keys to prevent double-charging on network retries. Webhook Dispatcher: Outbound notifications to external systems (e.g., Braze) upon tier changes.
target_output.system_components: API Gateway HTTP APIs, Token Validation Lambda Authorizer, Core API Lambdas (GetBalance, MutatePoints, GetLedger), DynamoDB Core Table, DynamoDB Streams, Tier Evaluation Worker Lambda, CRM Webhook SQS Queue, Webhook Dispatcher Worker.
target_output.workflows: Point Issuance (Checkout): Storefront backend calls POST /points/award with an Idempotency-Key. The MutatePoints Lambda validates the key against DynamoDB to prevent duplicates. It executes a TransactWriteItems call: inserting a new ledger record (e.g., +500 points) and incrementing the `current_balance` attribute on the user's profile record. Tier Upgrade (Async): The DynamoDB Stream captures the balance update and triggers the Tier Evaluation Worker. If the new balance crosses the 10,000 point threshold, it updates the user's tier to 'Gold' and drops an event onto EventBridge, which routes to SQS for the CRM dispatcher to send a congratulatory email.
target_output.data_model: Single-Table Design. PK: `USER#<user_id>`, SK: `PROFILE` -> Attributes: current_balance, lifetime_points, current_tier. PK: `USER#<user_id>`, SK: `LEDGER#<timestamp>#<tx_id>` -> Attributes: amount, transaction_type (earn/burn), order_reference. PK: `IDEMPOTENCY#<key>`, SK: `META` -> Attributes: response_payload, ttl (24 hours). GSI1: PK `TIER#<tier_name>`, SK `USER#<user_id>` for marketing cohort queries.
target_output.api_design: REST API secured by Bearer Token. GET /v1/users/{id}/balance -> Returns { points, tier }. POST /v1/users/{id}/transactions -> Body { amount, type, reference_id }, requires Header `Idempotency-Key`, returns HTTP 201 or 409 Conflict if key reused with different payload. GET /v1/users/{id}/ledger -> Paginated list of historical point changes.
target_output.security_and_compliance: AWS WAF blocks malicious IPs and enforces a strict rate limit of 50 req/sec per user IP to prevent point scraping. Lambda Authorizer validates JWT signatures and scopes (e.g., requires `loyalty:write` scope for mutations). CCPA/GDPR compliance is handled via a dedicated /v1/users/{id}/forget endpoint that zeroes the profile balance and obfuscates the user ID in the historical ledger.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: The Serverless Framework manages CloudFormation stack deployments. GitHub Actions runs Jest unit tests and deploys to staging, then production. The GetBalance Lambda is configured with Provisioned Concurrency of 50 during normal hours, scaling via Application Auto Scaling to 500 during Black Friday. Database is provisioned with On-Demand billing.
target_output.observability: AWS X-Ray traces end-to-end request latency from API Gateway through Lambda to DynamoDB. CloudWatch Alarms trigger PagerDuty if the P99 latency of GetBalance exceeds 100ms, or if the SQS Dead Letter Queue for CRM webhooks contains more than 10 messages. Application logs are written in structured JSON to CloudWatch Logs.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Highly variable, usage-based cost model. API Gateway and Lambda costs scale linearly with traffic. DynamoDB On-Demand handles sudden e-commerce spikes (e.g., flash sales) without manual capacity planning. Provisioned Concurrency adds a fixed base cost of ~$150/month but is essential for the SLA.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (MVP): Core read/write APIs, DynamoDB single-table setup, Idempotency implementation. Phase 2: Tier calculation engine and DynamoDB streams integration. Phase 3: SQS webhook dispatcher for CRM integration. Phase 4: Partner API expansion allowing third-party point redemption.
target_output.development_guidelines: Use DynamoDB DocumentClient for simplified interactions. All point mutations MUST use the shared `TransactionService` library encapsulating the TransactWriteItems logic. Test coverage must be 90%+ for the ledger math. Local development utilizes serverless-offline and DynamoDB Local.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: DynamoDB 25-item limit in TransactWriteItems. Mitigation: Loyalty events rarely exceed 2-3 items per transaction (profile update + ledger entry + idempotency record), keeping us well under the limit. Tradeoff: Using Provisioned Concurrency breaks the 'pure' zero-cost scale-to-zero serverless model, but is a necessary architectural compromise to guarantee the checkout flow performance SLA.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Resolved race conditions by enforcing DynamoDB transactions rather than standard atomic counters, ensuring the ledger perfectly matches the materialized balance. Solved the latency constraint via Provisioned Concurrency on read-heavy Lambdas.

project_goal.value: Provision cloud infrastructure for ingesting and storing low-earth orbit (LEO) satellite telemetry
project_goal.rationale: Support upcoming constellation launch of 50 cubesats

target_users.rationale: Require real-time satellite health and historical orbital data

project_class.rationale: Core deliverable is Terraform code and networking, not application code

capabilities.rationale: Real-time ingestion pipelines and heavy IaC automation

complexity_level.rationale: Integration with physical ground station networks and specialized space APIs

risk_level.rationale: Loss of telemetry during critical maneuvers can result in loss of satellite

data_sensitivity.rationale: Proprietary orbital parameters and hardware health metrics

external_exposure.rationale: Ingress restricted to authorized ground station IPs via mTLS

access_model.rationale: Corporate standard for cloud infrastructure access

feature_scope.value: Ground station VPC routing, MQTT broker provisioning, Time-series DB clustering, cold storage lifecycle
feature_scope.rationale: Complete data lifecycle for spacecraft downlinks

mvp_scope.value: Support for initial 5 test satellites via 2 ground stations
mvp_scope.rationale: Validate architecture before scaling to full constellation

security_baseline.value: Strict Security Groups, VPC Flow Logs, encrypted EBS/S3
security_baseline.rationale: Standard cloud security posture for aerospace

privacy_retention_policy.value: Keep high-res telemetry for 6 months, downsample and retain indefinitely
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Spacecraft lifespan modeling requires years of data

future_scope.value: Integration with AWS Ground Station service
future_scope.rationale: Reduce reliance on proprietary physical antennas

constraints.value: Infrastructure must deploy entirely from CI/CD without manual console clicks
constraints.rationale: Ensure absolute reproducibility for DR scenarios

observability_baseline.value: CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, Timestream usage metrics
observability_baseline.rationale: Auditability of all infrastructure changes and data flows

execution_preference.value: GitOps with Atlantis for Terraform PRs

reasoner_reviews.product: The infrastructure directly supports the mission-critical telemetry path. Ensuring multi-region failover is essential because a ground station pass only lasts ~10 minutes.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Using AWS IoT Core is a good choice for MQTT, but satellites downlink raw binary frames (CCSDS protocol), not JSON. The infrastructure must include an ECS cluster or Lambda functions to decode these frames before writing to Timestream.
reasoner_reviews.security: VPC Flow Logs and KMS are standard. However, the connection between the physical ground station and the AWS VPC must traverse the public internet or a dedicated line. A Site-to-Site VPN or Direct Connect must be explicitly provisioned in the Terraform.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Multi-region deployment using Terraform requires careful state management and module structuring to avoid duplicating code while ensuring DR environments are identical.

devops.iac_structure: Terraform workspaces per environment (dev, prod-west, prod-east), using Terragrunt for DRY configurations
devops.networking: Transit Gateway connecting ground station VPNs to the ingestion VPCs
devops.dr_strategy: Active ingestion in us-west-2, S3 cross-region replication to us-east-1, Timestream multi-region setup not natively supported so requires dual-writing via IoT Rules

data.storage_design: AWS IoT Rules routing decoded JSON telemetry into Amazon Timestream, raw binary frames routed to S3 for archiving
data.retention_and_deletion: Timestream memory store: 24 hours, magnetic store: 6 months. S3: Glacier Deep Archive after 1 year

ISS-01.description: Satellites transmit raw CCSDS binary frames, but IoT Core/Timestream expect JSON. Missing a decoding layer.

ISS-02.description: Ground station network connectivity to AWS is undefined, posing a security and reliability risk over public internet.

revision_memory.round_1: Proposed pure IoT Core to Timestream routing.
revision_memory.changes_for_round_2: Add an ECS-based binary frame decoding layer. Provision AWS Site-to-Site VPNs for secure ground station transit.

previous_audits: Identified missing payload translation layer.
Flagged insecure ingress from ground stations.

target_output.thinking_summary: Refining the infrastructure plan to account for real-world satellite communications. Added an ECS Fargate cluster running a decoder service to translate raw CCSDS binary frames into JSON before routing to Timestream. Hardened the network topology by replacing public ingress with AWS Site-to-Site VPNs connecting the physical ground stations directly to a Transit Gateway. Structured the Terraform for multi-region DR.

fix_report.action_taken: Provisioned an ECS Fargate cluster and AWS IoT Rules to route raw payloads to the decoder before Timestream ingestion.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Successful parsing of binary downlinks into queryable time-series metrics.

fix_report.action_taken: Added AWS Site-to-Site VPN and Transit Gateway configurations to the IaC.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Secure, encrypted, and reliable transit from ground antennas to the AWS VPC.

target_output.title: Spacecraft Telemetry Infrastructure (IaC)
target_output.executive_summary: A robust, multi-region AWS infrastructure provisioned entirely via Terraform to support low-earth orbit (LEO) satellite operations. It provides secure VPN connectivity for physical ground stations, highly available MQTT message brokering via AWS IoT Core, a scalable binary frame decoding tier on ECS Fargate, and purpose-built time-series storage using Amazon Timestream.
target_output.architecture_overview: Physical ground stations connect to the AWS cloud via redundant AWS Site-to-Site VPNs terminating at a Transit Gateway. Telemetry downlinks (raw CCSDS binary frames) are published to AWS IoT Core over MQTT. IoT Rules Engine forwards these raw frames to an ECS Fargate decoding cluster via an SQS queue. The decoders unpack the binary into JSON, extracting orbital parameters (e.g., quaternion vectors, solar array voltage), and write the structured data directly to Amazon Timestream. Concurrently, IoT Rules archive the raw binary payloads into S3. The entire stack is replicated in us-east-1 for Disaster Recovery, utilizing S3 Cross-Region Replication and dual-writing logic in the decoder tier.
target_output.technology_stack: Infrastructure as Code: Terraform v1.5, Terragrunt, Atlantis. Networking: AWS Transit Gateway, Site-to-Site VPN, VPC. Ingestion: AWS IoT Core (MQTT), Amazon SQS. Compute: Amazon ECS Fargate (for decoders). Database: Amazon Timestream. Storage: Amazon S3 with Lifecycle Policies. Security: AWS KMS, IAM, Security Groups.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Secure Ingress: Encrypted IPsec tunnels from ground antennas to the cloud. Message Brokering: Highly available MQTT endpoints for satellite data streams. Payload Translation: Serverless container compute scaling automatically to decode binary streams during 10-minute satellite passes. Time-Series Storage: Fast ingestion of high-cardinality telemetry (voltage, temperature, attitude) optimized for temporal queries. Archival: Immutable storage of raw unparsed flight data for forensic post-mission analysis.
target_output.system_components: Transit Gateway, VPN Attachments, Private Subnets, Public NAT Gateways, IoT Core Endpoints, SQS Decoupling Queues, ECS Fargate Cluster (Decoder Service), Timestream Database & Tables, S3 Raw Bucket, S3 Terraform State Bucket, Atlantis CI/CD Server.
target_output.workflows: Ground Pass Downlink: Satellite passes over antenna, transmitting RF. Antenna demodulates RF to binary CCSDS frames. Ground station software pushes binary to AWS IoT Core over the VPN via MQTT. Decoding & Storage: IoT Rule triggers, placing the payload in SQS. ECS Fargate task pulls the message, decodes the proprietary binary into JSON mapping subsystems (EPS, ADCS, CDH). The task performs a bulk write to Timestream and puts the raw binary object into S3. DR Replication: The decoder task simultaneously writes the JSON payload to the us-east-1 Timestream endpoint, while S3 native replication syncs the raw binaries to the standby region.
target_output.data_model: Terraform State: Remote state in S3 with DynamoDB locking. Timestream Schema: Database `FlightOps`, Table `Telemetry`. Dimensions: `satellite_id` (e.g., 'CUBESAT-01'), `subsystem` (e.g., 'ADCS'), `ground_station_id`. Measures: `voltage` (DOUBLE), `temperature_c` (DOUBLE), `quaternion_x` (DOUBLE). S3 Paths: `s3://telemetry-raw-prod/sat-01/YYYY/MM/DD/pass_id.bin`.
target_output.api_design: No application APIs provided by this project. Infrastructure APIs consumed via Terraform AWS Provider. Exposes an internal MQTT endpoint `telemetry.iot.us-west-2.amazonaws.com` restricted to the Transit Gateway subnet range.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Network isolation: No public IP assignment for ECS tasks or databases. Ingress strictly limited to the Customer Gateway IPs of the ground stations over IPsec. Encryption: All EBS volumes, S3 buckets, and Timestream tables are encrypted at rest using a Customer Managed KMS Key (CMK). AWS CloudTrail is enabled globally to log all infrastructure API calls. Terraform execution requires IAM roles assumed via SAML, utilizing temporary STS credentials.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitOps workflow. Engineers open PRs against the infrastructure repo. Atlantis intercepts the webhook, runs `terraform plan`, and posts the output as a PR comment. Upon approval from a SpaceOps Lead, merging triggers `terraform apply`. Infrastructure is modularized using Terragrunt to manage environments (dev, prod-west, prod-east) without code duplication.
target_output.observability: VPC Flow Logs shipped to CloudWatch Logs to monitor traffic from the ground stations. AWS Config rules enforce that no S3 buckets are public. Timestream metrics (SuccessfulRequests, SystemErrors) are graphed on a CloudWatch Dashboard. Alerts fire to PagerDuty if the SQS queue age exceeds 2 minutes, indicating the decoder tasks are failing to process downlinks.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $4,000/month. Major cost drivers are Timestream (billed by ingest and query volume) and the Transit Gateway hourly attachment fees. ECS Fargate scales horizontally based on SQS queue depth, ensuring costs are minimized between satellite passes (when the sky is quiet).
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1: Deploy core networking (VPC, TGW, VPN) and establish connectivity with the two test ground stations. Phase 2: Deploy IoT Core, SQS, and the ECS Fargate cluster with a mock decoder. Phase 3: Provision Timestream and S3 buckets. Phase 4: Full DR deployment to us-east-1 and configure cross-region replication.
target_output.development_guidelines: All Terraform code must pass `tflint` and `tfsec` in local pre-commit hooks. Use exact version pinning for AWS providers. Do not hardcode any IP addresses; use data sources and variable injections. Tag all resources with `Project: Spaceflight` and `Environment: Prod` for billing attribution.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Timestream does not natively support cross-region replication. Mitigation: Dual-writing at the application (decoder) layer handles the DR requirement. Tradeoff: Utilizing ECS Fargate for decoding instead of AWS Lambda provides consistent compute without timeout limits (Lambda's 15min limit), but introduces a slightly slower scale-out time during sudden telemetry bursts.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided to use AWS Site-to-Site VPN over Direct Connect for the MVP to reduce lead time, with plans to upgrade to Direct Connect for the full constellation. Confirmed Timestream over InfluxDB to maintain a fully managed, serverless operational footprint.

metadata.notes: Infrastructure for aerospace telemetry.

project_goal.value: Build an automated pipeline for whole-genome sequencing (WGS) variant calling
project_goal.rationale: Accelerate rare disease identification from patient DNA samples

target_users.rationale: Expert users submitting raw FASTQ files for processing

project_class.rationale: Scientific computing workflow rather than a standard web application

capabilities.rationale: Heavy compute batch processing of massive datasets

complexity_level.rationale: Complex DAGs of scientific software (BWA, GATK) with specific memory/CPU constraints

risk_level.rationale: Pipeline failures waste compute budget, but data is recoverable

data_sensitivity.rationale: Genomic data is highly sensitive PHI under HIPAA

external_exposure.rationale: Strictly bounded within the research institution's private cloud

access_model.value: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) tied to institutional SSO
access_model.rationale: Only authorized researchers assigned to a study can access data

feature_scope.value: FASTQ ingestion, read alignment, variant calling, VCF generation, workflow monitoring

mvp_scope.value: Single-sample pipeline processing 30x coverage genomes
mvp_scope.rationale: Prove the workflow before expanding to multi-sample joint genotyping

security_baseline.value: HIPAA compliance, end-to-end encryption, audit logs for all data access
security_baseline.rationale: Mandatory for handling human genomic data

frontend_stack.rationale: Rapid UI development for researchers to track job status

backend_stack.rationale: Industry standard workflow manager for bioinformatics

data_platform.value: Amazon S3, AWS Glue Data Catalog
data_platform.rationale: Object storage is mandatory for 100GB+ genome files

hosting_target.rationale: Dynamically provisions EC2 spot instances based on Nextflow resource requests

privacy_retention_policy.value: Retain raw FASTQ for 5 years, intermediate BAM files purged after 30 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Balance storage costs with regulatory retention rules

future_scope.value: Integration with AI models for structural variant prediction

constraints.value: Cost per genome must be under $10 compute
constraints.rationale: Budget limitation for large-scale population studies

observability_baseline.value: Nextflow Tower for workflow tracing, AWS CloudWatch for infrastructure

execution_preference.value: Agile iterations on the scientific logic, automated infrastructure deployment
execution_preference.rationale: Science team handles logic, DevOps handles scale

compliance_context.value: HIPAA BAA required, strictly governed PHI boundary
compliance_context.rationale: Human DNA sequences are protected health information

reasoner_reviews.product: MVP scoped to 30x single-sample processing is perfect. It allows the team to validate the clinical accuracy of the generated VCFs against known benchmarks (like NA12878) before scaling up.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Nextflow combined with AWS Batch is the optimal pattern here. Nextflow can natively request specific EC2 instance types (compute-optimized for alignment, memory-optimized for variant calling) dynamically as the DAG executes.
reasoner_reviews.security: Handling FASTQ files requires strict HIPAA controls. The S3 buckets must be encrypted with KMS, and IAM roles for AWS Batch tasks must be tightly scoped so they can only access the specific S3 prefixes for their assigned job.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Hitting the <$10/genome compute target requires aggressive use of EC2 Spot Instances. Nextflow handles spot interruptions gracefully by retrying the failed process, which is essential for this cost constraint.

backend.service_design: Nextflow orchestrator script defining the DAG: FASTQ -> BWA-MEM (Alignment) -> Samtools (Sorting) -> GATK (Variant Calling) -> VCF
backend.api_patterns: S3 event triggers launching the Nextflow head node via AWS Step Functions
backend.failure_handling: Nextflow configured with `errorStrategy = 'retry'` and progressive backoff for Spot interruptions

data.entities: FASTQ (raw reads), BAM (aligned reads), VCF (variant calls), Reference Genome (FASTA)
data.storage_design: S3 tiered storage: Standard for active processing, Lifecycle rules to purge massive intermediate BAMs after 30 days to save costs
data.retention_and_deletion: Automated transition of FASTQ to Glacier Deep Archive after 1 year

security.auth_design: Cognito integrated with University SAML provider for the Streamlit dashboard
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudTrail tracking all S3 GetObject calls to monitor PHI access

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a high-throughput, HIPAA-compliant bioinformatics pipeline for whole-genome sequencing. Selected Nextflow as the DAG orchestrator integrated natively with AWS Batch to execute scientific containers (BWA, GATK). Heavily relying on EC2 Spot Instances to meet the strict $10/genome cost constraint, utilizing Nextflow's built-in retry mechanisms to handle spot preemption. Built a lightweight Streamlit UI for researchers to monitor job progress without needing AWS console access.
target_output.title: High-Throughput Genomic Variant Calling Pipeline
target_output.executive_summary: A cloud-native, scalable bioinformatics platform designed to automate whole-genome sequencing (WGS) data processing. Utilizing Nextflow and AWS Batch, the system ingests raw patient DNA sequences, performs alignment against reference genomes, and executes variant calling to identify genetic mutations. It ensures HIPAA compliance while aggressively optimizing costs through transient spot computing.
target_output.architecture_overview: Researchers upload raw FASTQ files to an S3 bucket via a secure Streamlit web portal. An S3 Event Notification triggers an AWS Lambda function, which initiates an AWS Step Functions state machine. This state machine deploys a Nextflow 'head node' on a lightweight EC2 instance. The Nextflow script parses the pipeline DAG and submits individual scientific computing jobs (Alignment, Sorting, Base Quality Score Recalibration, Variant Calling) to AWS Batch. AWS Batch dynamically provisions EC2 Spot Instances within a private VPC, pulling required Docker images (containing tools like BWA-MEM and GATK) from ECR. Results (VCF files) are written back to a secure S3 bucket, and the Streamlit UI updates with the final job status.
target_output.technology_stack: Workflow Engine: Nextflow. Compute: AWS Batch, EC2 Spot Instances, AWS Fargate (for head node). Storage: Amazon S3 (Standard, Glacier), EBS (gp3 for scratch space). UI/Frontend: Streamlit (Python). Security: AWS KMS, IAM, AWS Cognito. Scientific Tools (Containerized): BWA (Burrows-Wheeler Aligner), Samtools, GATK (Genome Analysis Toolkit).
target_output.functional_feature_map: Secure Data Ingestion: Multipart upload of 100GB+ FASTQ files directly to S3 with KMS encryption. Automated DAG Execution: Nextflow handles process dependencies, ensuring sorting only occurs after alignment completes. Spot Instance Orchestration: AWS Batch matches computational requirements (e.g., 32 vCPUs for BWA, 64GB RAM for GATK) to the cheapest available spot pools. Result Generation: Produces standard Variant Call Format (VCF) files identifying SNPs and Indels. Researcher Dashboard: Streamlit UI showing real-time logs, pipeline progress, and historical run metrics.
target_output.system_components: Streamlit Web App (hosted on Fargate), Cognito User Pool, S3 Upload Bucket, S3 Reference Data Bucket (containing the GRCh38 reference genome), Step Functions Orchestrator, AWS Batch Compute Environments (Spot), Amazon ECR (scientific containers), S3 Output Bucket.
target_output.workflows: Pipeline Execution: User logs into Streamlit via University SSO. User selects a FASTQ file and clicks 'Start Run'. Step Functions launches Nextflow. Nextflow pulls the FASTQ from S3 and submits the 'Alignment' job to Batch requesting 16 vCPUs. Batch provisions an r5.4xlarge Spot instance. The BWA container runs, aligning reads to the GRCh38 reference, and writes a BAM file to S3. Nextflow detects completion and submits the 'Variant Calling' job. GATK processes the BAM, identifies mutations, and writes the final VCF to S3. Nextflow terminates and sends a success email via SES. Spot Interruption: If AWS reclaims an EC2 instance mid-alignment, AWS Batch kills the task. Nextflow detects the exit code, waits 5 minutes, and resubmits the job to a different Spot capacity pool, ensuring pipeline resilience.
target_output.data_model: Inputs: Paired-end FASTQ files (`sample_R1.fq.gz`, `sample_R2.fq.gz`). Reference Data: FASTA files (`GRCh38.fasta`) and index files (.fai, .dict). Intermediates: Unaligned BAM, Sorted BAM, Recalibrated BAM, Metrics files. Outputs: Final Variant Call Format (`sample_variants.vcf.gz`). Metadata: DynamoDB table `PipelineRuns` (RunID, UserID, SampleID, Status ENUM pending/running/failed/success, CostEstimate, StartTime, EndTime).
target_output.api_design: The Streamlit frontend communicates directly with AWS APIs via Boto3. No custom REST API is built. `s3:PutObject` for uploads. `batch:DescribeJobs` to poll status. DynamoDB `GetItem` to retrieve run metadata.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Fully HIPAA compliant. All S3 buckets enforce AES-256 encryption at rest using Customer Managed KMS Keys. Data never leaves the private VPC; AWS Batch instances run in private subnets with no public IPs, accessing S3 via VPC Endpoints (Gateway). AWS Cognito enforces MFA. A Business Associate Agreement (BAA) is in place with AWS. IAM roles for Batch tasks are strictly scoped using resource-level permissions (can only read from the specific `/uploads/sample_id/` S3 prefix).
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Infrastructure deployed via Terraform. Nextflow pipeline code is versioned in GitHub. When researchers update the Nextflow script, a GitHub Action lints the Groovy code and syncs it to the S3 bucket used by the head node. Scientific Docker images are built via CI/CD and pushed to ECR. AWS Cost Explorer is configured with budget alerts to monitor the $10/genome KPI.
target_output.observability: Nextflow Tower integration provides granular tracing of the scientific DAG, showing exactly which process (e.g., MarkDuplicates) is running and its resource utilization. CloudWatch captures standard out/error from all AWS Batch containers. Streamlit dashboard aggregates this into a user-friendly 'Pipeline Status' progress bar.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Highly optimized for cost. Utilizing 100% EC2 Spot instances reduces compute costs by 70-90% compared to on-demand. S3 Lifecycle rules automatically delete massive (150GB+) intermediate BAM files 30 days after generation, leaving only the raw FASTQ (moved to Glacier after 1 year) and the small (1GB) VCF file. Expected cost is ~$6-8 per genome, successfully meeting the $10 constraint.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (MVP): Single-sample Nextflow pipeline execution using public 1000 Genomes data to validate infrastructure. Phase 2: Streamlit UI development and SSO integration. Phase 3: Ingestion of real clinical PHI samples under HIPAA compliance. Phase 4: Implementation of multi-sample joint genotyping capabilities.
target_output.development_guidelines: Nextflow scripts must be modularized into discrete processes. All processes must declare explicit CPU and memory requirements to allow AWS Batch to optimize instance selection. Scientific containers must be immutable and pinned to specific tool versions (e.g., `gatk:4.2.6.1`) to ensure absolute reproducibility of clinical results.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: High spot instance preemption rates during peak AWS region utilization could cause pipeline delays. Mitigation: Nextflow is configured to fall back to On-Demand instances if a specific job fails 3 times due to spot reclamation. Tradeoff: Abstracting the AWS infrastructure behind a Streamlit UI limits power-user capabilities (like manually tuning JVM arguments for GATK), but is necessary to prevent researchers from accidentally misconfiguring expensive cloud resources.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected AWS Batch over Kubernetes (EKS) for batch scheduling, as Batch natively integrates with Spot pricing and requires less operational overhead for pure batch workloads. Confirmed Nextflow over Snakemake due to superior native cloud integration.

metadata.notes: Scientific computing pipeline prioritizing spot cost savings and PHI security.

project_goal.value: Deliver a consumer mobile app for automated portfolio management and fractional investing
project_goal.rationale: Capture millennial and Gen Z retail investor market

target_users.rationale: Everyday consumers looking for robo-advisory services

project_class.rationale: Native mobile experience is table stakes for retail fintech

capabilities.rationale: Requires Plaid, KYC providers, and Brokerage API integrations

complexity_level.rationale: Financial ledger accuracy and real-time market data synchronization

risk_level.rationale: Handling real money; bugs result in financial loss and SEC fines

data_sensitivity.rationale: Bank credentials, SSNs for KYC, and portfolio balances

feature_scope.value: Bank linking, automated deposits, portfolio allocation, trade execution, performance tracking

mvp_scope.value: Plaid integration, 3 pre-built ETF portfolios, monthly auto-deposits
mvp_scope.rationale: Fastest path to AUM (Assets Under Management) generation

security_baseline.value: PCI-DSS, SOC2, Encryption at rest/transit, strict PII tokenization

frontend_stack.rationale: Single codebase for iOS and Android with native performance

backend_stack.rationale: Type-safe, high-performance concurrency for financial transactions

data_platform.rationale: Distributed SQL with absolute serializability for ledgers

hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers scaling to zero during off-market hours

privacy_retention_policy.value: Retain transaction records for 7 years per SEC regulations

future_scope.value: Crypto wallet integration and custom stock picking

constraints.value: Must use Alpaca API for actual trade execution and custody
constraints.rationale: Avoids needing a broker-dealer license ourselves

observability_baseline.value: Sentry for mobile crashes, Datadog for backend tracing

execution_preference.value: Strict TDD for all ledger and trading logic

compliance_context.value: SEC Reg Best Interest, FINRA KYC/AML rules, SOC2 Type II

reasoner_reviews.product: MVP feature set is solid. Bank linking via Plaid and execution via Alpaca offloads the hardest regulatory burdens.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Using CockroachDB for a financial ledger is excellent. However, the initial plan lacked an asynchronous worker pattern. Trading APIs (like Alpaca) have rate limits and market-hour constraints. We need a robust queue to manage delayed trade executions.
reasoner_reviews.security: Storing PII (SSNs for KYC) is highly risky. We must implement a tokenization vault so the core Go backend only handles reference tokens, never the raw SSN.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Relying entirely on Alpaca means our system state must be eventually consistent with their clearing system. We need nightly reconciliation batch jobs to ensure our CockroachDB ledger perfectly matches the Alpaca custodial balances.

backend.service_design: User Service, Portfolio Service, Trade Execution Worker, Reconciliation Cron
backend.api_patterns: REST API for Flutter app, gRPC for internal microservices
backend.failure_handling: Exponential backoff for Plaid/Alpaca API failures, Circuit breakers via Resilience4j pattern in Go

security.auth_design: Firebase Auth issuing JWTs, validated by Go middleware. Biometric unlock stores refresh token in Secure Enclave/Keystore.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Immutable append-only audit log for all state changes in user portfolios. PII tokenized via VGS (Very Good Security).

data.schema_management: CockroachDB schema using UUIDv4 for all primary keys to prevent enumeration. Decimal types for all currency math.
data.hot_path: Redis used ONLY for caching market data, NEVER for user balances.

ISS-01.description: Lack of a reliable queuing mechanism for delayed trade executions outside of market hours.

ISS-02.description: Core database handles raw SSNs for KYC, violating data minimization principles and increasing breach impact.

revision_memory.round_1: Proposed direct synchronous API calls to Alpaca and storing KYC data directly in CockroachDB.
revision_memory.changes_for_round_2: Integrate Google Cloud Tasks for asynchronous trade queuing and implement Very Good Security (VGS) for SSN tokenization.

previous_audits: Identified risk of dropped trades during market closures.
Flagged severe compliance risk regarding raw SSN storage.

previous_plan.title: RoboAdvisor MVP v1
previous_plan.summary: Basic Flutter app backed by Go APIs calling Alpaca directly.

target_output.thinking_summary: Addressing critical issues from the first pass. Replaced synchronous trading calls with Google Cloud Tasks to safely queue trades when markets are closed or APIs are rate-limited. Removed raw SSN storage from the architecture, introducing Very Good Security (VGS) to tokenize PII before it ever reaches our backend, drastically reducing our compliance scope. Formalized the reconciliation jobs needed to sync our ledger with the custodial broker.

fix_report.action_taken: Integrated Google Cloud Tasks to decouple trade requests from execution, handling market hours logic safely.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Zero dropped trades; orders placed on weekends reliably execute Monday morning.

fix_report.action_taken: Integrated VGS (Very Good Security) as a proxy to tokenize SSNs and bank credentials.
fix_report.expected_outcome: SSNs never touch GCP infrastructure, massively simplifying SOC2 and SEC compliance.

target_output.title: Mobile Wealth Management Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A highly secure, retail-facing robo-advisor mobile application built with Flutter and Go. It allows users to link banks via Plaid, select ETF portfolios, and automate fractional investing. Relying on Alpaca for custodial brokerage and Very Good Security (VGS) for PII tokenization, the architecture ensures absolute financial ledger accuracy using CockroachDB while minimizing regulatory risk and compliance overhead.
target_output.architecture_overview: The Flutter mobile application communicates with the backend via a VGS forward proxy. This proxy intercepts sensitive PII (like SSNs during onboarding), replaces it with secure tokens, and forwards the sanitized payload to a Go-based REST API hosted on GCP Cloud Run. The backend manages state in a highly available CockroachDB cluster, ensuring strict serializability for all financial ledgers. Trading operations are asynchronous: user deposits and rebalancing requests are pushed to Google Cloud Tasks, which trigger execution workers that interface with the Alpaca API during open market hours. A nightly reconciliation job compares CockroachDB balances against Alpaca clearing files to detect and alert on any discrepancies.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Flutter (Dart), Riverpod. Backend: Go 1.21, Gin. Database: CockroachDB Dedicated. Queuing: Google Cloud Tasks. Caching: Google Cloud Memorystore (Redis). Identity: Firebase Auth. Security/Tokenization: Very Good Security (VGS). Third-Party Integrations: Plaid (bank linking), Alpaca (brokerage/custody), Alloy (KYC/AML). Infrastructure: GCP Cloud Run, Cloud Scheduler.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Onboarding & KYC: Seamless identity verification flowing through Alloy using tokenized SSNs. Funding: ACH deposit initiation via Plaid Link. Portfolio Selection: Risk assessment questionnaire mapping users to Conservative, Moderate, or Aggressive ETF models. Automated Trading: Fractional share calculation and asynchronous order routing to Alpaca. Performance Tracking: Real-time portfolio value charts leveraging Redis-cached market data.
target_output.system_components: Flutter Mobile App, VGS Zero Data Proxy, Cloud Run API Gateway, User/KYC Service, Funding Service, Trade Routing Worker, Reconciliation Cron, CockroachDB Cluster, Redis Instance, Google Cloud Tasks Queue.
target_output.workflows: KYC Onboarding: User enters SSN in the app. App POSTs to API via VGS. VGS intercepts SSN, stores it in its vault, and forwards an aliased token (`tok_123`) to the Go backend. Backend calls Alloy API via VGS outbound proxy (which detokenizes the SSN) to verify identity. Trade Execution: User schedules a $100 deposit into the 'Aggressive' portfolio on a Sunday. Funding Service records the pending deposit and enqueues a Cloud Task scheduled for 9:30 AM Monday. At 9:30 AM, the Trade Worker picks up the task, calculates the fractional ETF allocations, and submits the batch order to Alpaca. Upon webhook confirmation from Alpaca, CockroachDB ledger is updated to reflect settled shares. Nightly Reconciliation: Cloud Scheduler triggers a Go script at 2 AM. Script fetches end-of-day balances from Alpaca, compares them against the local CockroachDB ledger, and pages on-call engineers if any account deviates by more than $0.01.
target_output.data_model: CockroachDB. `users` (id UUID, email, vgs_ssn_token, kyc_status, created_at). `portfolios` (id UUID, user_id FK, model_type ENUM). `accounts` (id UUID, user_id FK, alpaca_account_id). `ledger_entries` (id UUID, account_id FK, type ENUM cash_deposit/trade_buy/trade_sell, asset_symbol, amount_usd DECIMAL(19,4), shares DECIMAL(19,8), status ENUM pending/settled). All currency math strictly uses Go `shopspring/decimal` library.
target_output.api_design: RESTful JSON over HTTPS. POST /v1/users (Create account). POST /v1/funding/ach (Initiate deposit via Plaid token). GET /v1/portfolio (Fetch holdings and cached chart data). POST /v1/webhooks/alpaca (Internal endpoint for trade settlement callbacks, verified via HMAC signature).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Zero Data Architecture for PII via VGS ensures the GCP environment is entirely out of scope for raw SSN storage. Authentication utilizes Firebase Auth; mobile client uses Secure Enclave/Keystore for Biometric unlocking to fetch short-lived JWTs. GCP Cloud Run services are secured behind Cloud Armor (WAF). Strict adherence to SEC Reg BI through immutable audit trails in CockroachDB for every portfolio change.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Mobile CI/CD via Bitrise, running Flutter integration tests and automating TestFlight/Play Store releases. Backend CI/CD via GitHub Actions building lightweight Go Docker images deployed to Cloud Run. Database migrations managed by `golang-migrate` executed during CI pipeline.
target_output.observability: Sentry integrated into Flutter for crash reporting and API error tracking. Datadog APM installed on Go services for distributed tracing. Custom Datadog monitors alert on critical business logic failures: e.g., 'Alpaca Trade Reject Rate > 1%', 'Reconciliation Drift > 0', 'Cloud Tasks Queue Depth > 1000'.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GCP Cloud Run provides a highly cost-efficient scale-to-zero model during nights and weekends when markets are closed and usage drops. CockroachDB is the largest fixed cost (~$500/mo for dedicated cluster) but is strictly necessary for ledger safety. Plaid and Alpaca API usage scales linearly with user acquisition.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (MVP): Plaid linking, KYC via Alloy, manual ACH deposits, and execution of the 3 core ETF models. Phase 2: Automated recurring monthly deposits and dividend reinvestment (DRIP). Phase 3: Tax-loss harvesting algorithms. Phase 4: Expansion into self-directed trading of individual stocks.
target_output.development_guidelines: Strict Test-Driven Development (TDD) required for the `ledger` and `trading` Go packages. Floating-point numbers are strictly banned; all financial representations must use arbitrary-precision decimals. No direct database modifications allowed via CLI in production; all ledger fixes must be executed via auditable script tools.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Alpaca API outages prevent users from buying/selling. Mitigation: App UI gracefully degrades, queuing trades in Cloud Tasks and informing users of the delay. Tradeoff: Using a third-party tokenizer (VGS) introduces a single point of failure for API ingress/egress, but the immense reduction in SOC2/compliance overhead justifies the architectural dependency.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Resolved market-hours synchronization by moving all trade execution to asynchronous Google Cloud Tasks. Mitigated extreme compliance risks by removing raw PII storage from the primary database design.

metadata.notes: Mobile fintech prioritizing ledger consistency and PII tokenization.

project_goal.value: Create a fast, cross-platform CLI for managing and sharing Git hooks across enterprise engineering teams
project_goal.rationale: Ensure consistent linting, security scanning, and commit message formats before push

project_class.rationale: Runs strictly on local developer machines within the terminal

capabilities.rationale: Integrates deeply with Git internals and local shell environments

complexity_level.rationale: Local execution only, no distributed systems or databases required

risk_level.rationale: Worst case scenario is a blocked commit; no production impact

data_sensitivity.rationale: Processes local source code, does not transmit data externally

access_model.rationale: Relies on the user's existing terminal privileges

feature_scope.value: Hook installation, centralized configuration file parsing, parallel hook execution, interactive bypass
feature_scope.rationale: Core requirements for a pre-commit manager

mvp_scope.value: Support for pre-commit and pre-push hooks via a YAML config file

security_baseline.value: Do not execute arbitrary downloaded code without explicit user opt-in
security_baseline.rationale: Prevent supply chain attacks via malicious shared hooks

backend_stack.rationale: Instant startup times, single binary compilation, memory safety

data_platform.rationale: Configuration and local caching of hook results

future_scope.value: Centralized hook registry for internal company distribution
future_scope.rationale: Scale configuration across hundreds of repositories

constraints.value: Hook execution overhead must be < 50ms
constraints.rationale: Slow commits frustrate developers and lead to bypassing hooks

observability_baseline.value: Standard output/error logging with debug flags

execution_preference.rationale: Encourage contributions from the engineering org

confirmed_decisions.language: Rust for speed and single binary distribution

reasoner_reviews.product: MVP is clear. Focusing on pre-commit and pre-push covers the vast majority of enterprise use cases (linting, secret scanning, tests).
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Rust is the perfect choice. Python-based tools (like `pre-commit`) suffer from slow startup times due to virtualenv activation. Rust's Tokio runtime will allow us to execute multiple independent hooks in parallel, easily hitting the <50ms overhead constraint.
reasoner_reviews.security: The security baseline is important. If a repository shares a `.hooks.yml` file, the CLI must prompt the user to explicitly 'trust' the repository before executing any bash scripts defined in the file to prevent RCE upon cloning a malicious repo.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Achieving sub-50ms overhead means the CLI itself must start instantly. We should avoid heavy library dependencies and utilize a local SQLite cache via `rusqlite` to track file hashes, skipping hooks entirely if the file hasn't changed since the last successful run.

backend.service_design: Core CLI parser, Configuration Manager, Hook Execution Engine, Cache Manager
backend.api_patterns: Not applicable. Shell exit codes and stdout/stderr are the API.
backend.failure_handling: If a hook fails, gracefully capture stderr, print a color-coded summary, and exit with code 1 to abort the git action.

devops.distribution_design: GitHub Actions building statically linked binaries for x86_64-linux, aarch64-linux, x86_64-apple-darwin, aarch64-apple-darwin, and x86_64-pc-windows-msvc
devops.release_management: Automated generation of Homebrew taps and Winget manifests on release tagging

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a highly performant, local CLI tool for Git hook management. Selected Rust for its zero-cost abstractions, fast startup time, and ability to compile to a single cross-platform binary. Implementing a Tokio-based parallel execution engine and an SQLite local cache to guarantee the sub-50ms execution constraint. Ensuring security by prompting for explicit user trust before running repository-defined shell scripts.
target_output.title: High-Performance Git Hook CLI
target_output.executive_summary: A blazingly fast, cross-platform Command Line Interface (CLI) built in Rust for managing developer Git hooks. Designed to replace slow, Python-based or bash-scripted alternatives, it provides centralized configuration via YAML, parallel execution of linters and security scanners, and local caching to ensure zero developer friction with a sub-50ms overhead.
target_output.architecture_overview: The tool operates entirely locally. Upon installation in a repository (`hook-cli init`), it symlinks Git's native hooks (e.g., `.git/hooks/pre-commit`) to the compiled Rust binary. When a developer executes a `git commit`, Git invokes the binary. The core execution engine parses a local `.hooks.yml` configuration, determines which files are staged for commit, and checks an internal SQLite cache in `~/.cache/hook-cli/` to bypass unmodified files. Using the Tokio async runtime, it spawns child processes to execute the required linters and scanners in parallel. It aggregates standard output and error, rendering a color-coded terminal UI, and returns a non-zero exit code to Git if any hook fails, safely halting the commit.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Rust (Edition 2021). CLI Parsing: `clap` crate. Async Runtime: `tokio` crate. Serialization: `serde` and `serde_yaml`. Local Cache: `rusqlite` (SQLite). Terminal UI: `indicatif` (progress bars), `colored` crate. Distribution: GitHub Actions, Homebrew, Winget, Cargo.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Init Command: Bootstraps a repository by creating the YAML config and symlinking `.git/hooks`. Config Parser: Validates `.hooks.yml` syntax and resolves environment variables. Parallel Executor: Runs independent hooks (e.g., ESLint and Gitleaks) concurrently. Smart Caching: Hashes staged files; skips execution if the hash matches a previously successful run. Interactive Output: Displays spinner animations during execution and provides clear, aggregated error summaries upon failure. Trust Management: Prompts for confirmation before executing hooks in a newly cloned repository to prevent arbitrary code execution.
target_output.system_components: CLI Entrypoint (`main.rs`), Config Subsystem, Git Interop Module (wraps `git rev-parse` and `git diff`), Task Scheduler, Caching Layer (SQLite), Terminal Renderer. All components are compiled into a statically linked, standalone binary.
target_output.workflows: Initialization: User runs `hook-cli install`. The tool detects the `.git` directory, creates `.git/hooks/pre-commit` pointing to itself, and generates a default `.hooks.yml`. Pre-Commit Execution: User types `git commit`. Git triggers the hook. The CLI reads `.hooks.yml`. It runs `git diff --cached --name-only` to get staged files. It checks the SQLite cache for these file hashes. It spawns `eslint` and `cargo fmt` in parallel via Tokio. It awaits the child processes. If all exit with `0`, it updates the cache and exits with `0`, allowing the commit. If `eslint` exits with `1`, it prints the ESLint stderr in red and exits with `1`, blocking the commit.
target_output.data_model: Configuration (`.hooks.yml`): List of hook definitions containing `id`, `name`, `entry` (command to run), `files` (regex pattern), and `pass_filenames` (boolean). SQLite Cache Schema: `file_hashes` (id INTEGER PK, file_path TEXT, sha256_hash TEXT, hook_id TEXT, last_success_ts TIMESTAMP).
target_output.api_design: No network APIs. The 'API' is the command-line interface: `hook-cli run` (manual run against all files), `hook-cli install` (setup), `hook-cli bypass` (run commit ignoring hooks). Exit codes strictly follow POSIX standards: `0` for success, `1` for general hook failure, `2` for configuration syntax error.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Operates locally with zero network telemetry or data exfiltration. Mitigates the 'malicious clone' attack vector: when `hook-cli` detects a `.hooks.yml` in a repository it hasn't seen before, it pauses execution and prompts the user: `Warning: This repo contains executable hooks. Do you trust this repository? [y/N]`. User consent is stored in the global `~/.config/hook-cli/trust.yml`.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Fully automated CI/CD via GitHub Actions. On every tag push, cross-compilation matrix builds binaries for macOS (M1/Intel), Linux, and Windows. Release artifacts are uploaded to GitHub Releases. A secondary Action automatically bumps the formula version in the Homebrew Tap repository and submits a PR to the Microsoft Winget registry.
target_output.observability: Standard UNIX observability. `--verbose` flag enables `tracing` crate output to stderr, showing exact span timings for Tokio tasks (e.g., `[INFO] hook ESLint took 42ms`). `--debug` outputs the raw SQLite cache hit/miss logic for troubleshooting.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero operational cloud cost. The software runs entirely on local developer compute. Scaling is handled organically; the Rust Tokio runtime will efficiently utilize all available local CPU cores when running parallel hooks, scaling performance dynamically from weak laptops to powerful workstations.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (MVP): Core execution engine, YAML parsing, sequential execution, and manual Homebrew distribution. Phase 2: Implementation of Tokio parallel execution and the SQLite file-hashing cache. Phase 3: Interactive terminal UI with progress bars and the security 'Trust' prompt. Phase 4: Enterprise registry feature for pulling centrally managed `.hooks.yml` files.
target_output.development_guidelines: Strict Rust compiler warnings (`#[deny(clippy::all)]`). No panics allowed in production code; all errors must map to graceful `Result` handling with contextual CLI output. Minimum Supported Rust Version (MSRV) locked to 1.70. Unit tests must use `tempfile` to mock the Git repository filesystem.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Developers finding the tool too restrictive and using `git commit --no-verify` constantly. Mitigation: Ensure the SQLite caching works flawlessly so hooks are instantaneous on subsequent runs, removing the friction. Tradeoff: Compiling SQLite into the binary via `rusqlite` adds a few megabytes to the final file size, but drastically improves the cache read/write speed compared to parsing JSON files.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Decided against writing the tool in Go; Rust was chosen specifically for its smaller static binary size and superior cargo ecosystem for CLI parsing (`clap`). Confirmed the necessity of the 'Trust' prompt after security review of supply-chain vulnerabilities.

metadata.notes: High performance developer CLI tool emphasizing local speed and rust safety.

project_goal.value: Develop a live bidding web application for luxury real estate auctions
project_goal.rationale: Digitize high-end property auctions to reach global buyers

target_users.value: High net-worth individuals and broker representatives
target_users.rationale: Exclusive user base requiring high reliability

project_class.rationale: Browser-based access is preferred by older demographic buyers

capabilities.rationale: Sub-second latency required for live bidding wars

complexity_level.rationale: Concurrency control during the final seconds of an auction is critical

risk_level.rationale: A dropped bid or latency spike could cost millions in final sale price

data_sensitivity.rationale: Proof of funds documentation, escrow deposits, and bid amounts

external_exposure.rationale: Global access required, though hidden behind strict login

access_model.value: Strict multi-factor authentication, manual account approval
access_model.rationale: Must verify proof of funds before granting bidding access

feature_scope.rationale: Replicate the physical auction room experience online

mvp_scope.value: Text-based real-time bidding, fixed increments, countdown extensions (anti-sniper)
mvp_scope.rationale: Focus on core bidding engine reliability before adding video

security_baseline.value: DDoS protection, WAF, immutable bid logs
security_baseline.rationale: Target for extortion DDoS during high-profile auctions

frontend_stack.rationale: Server-rendered WebSockets minimize client-side state issues

backend_stack.rationale: Erlang BEAM VM handles millions of concurrent WebSocket connections natively

data_platform.rationale: ACID compliance for the bid ledger

hosting_target.rationale: Deploy Elixir nodes globally close to the bidders to reduce latency

privacy_retention_policy.value: Bid logs kept permanently for legal auditing, PII deleted on request after 7 years
